topics.title: Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metals** are a class of engineering materials (predominantly elemental or alloy-based) characterized by **metallic bonding**, where positively charged ions are embedded in a delocalized "sea" of valence electrons. This unique bonding confers high electrical/thermal conductivity, luster, malleability, ductility, and predominantly crystalline structure at ambient conditions.

For the context of **PCIET205 - Materials and Manufacturing Engineering**, the term **"Metals"** broadly encompasses all commercially significant **ferrous metals**, **non-ferrous metals**, and **metal alloys** that form the backbone of structural, mechanical, electrical, and chemical-process industries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Marble-in-Jelly" Analogy:** Imagine a tray filled with orange jelly in which hundreds of marbles are randomly embedded. The marbles represent the **positive metal ions (cations)** locked in fixed positions, and the jelly represents the **mobile cloud of free electrons**. Because the "jelly" (electrons) can flow freely between marbles (ions), electricity and heat pass through easily — this is why metals conduct. If you push on the tray, the marbles can slide past each other through the jelly without breaking the structure — this is **ductility/malleability**.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU Syllabus Anchor)

| Broad Category | Sub-Class | Examples | Defining Trait |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous** | Iron & Iron-based | Cast iron, Wrought iron, Steels | Iron is the base metal (\>50%) |
| **Non-Ferrous** | Light metals | Al, Mg, Ti, Be | Low density (<5 g/cm³) |
| **Non-Ferrous** | Heavy metals | Cu, Zn, Pb, Sn, Ni | Density > 5 g/cm³ |
| **Non-Ferrous** | Precious metals | Au, Ag, Pt, Pd | High economic value, low reactivity |
| **Non-Ferrous** | Refractory metals | W, Mo, Ta, Nb, Re | High melting point (>2000 °C) |
| **Alloys** | Ferrous alloys | Stainless steel, Tool steel, HSLA | Tailored composition |
| **Alloys** | Non-ferrous alloys | Brass, Bronze, Duralumin, Inconel | Tailored composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 ("Metals"), the focus is typically on (i) classification, (ii) mechanical/physical/chemical properties, (iii) detailed study of commercially important ferrous and non-ferrous metals, and (iv) engineering applications relevant to **manufacturing process selection**.

### 1.4 Why Engineers Study Metals

Metals form approximately **85% of structural and machinery materials** used in modern industry. The choice of metal directly determines:

- Manufacturability (castability, weldability, machinability)
- Service life (corrosion, fatigue, creep resistance)
- Cost-to-performance ratio
- Energy consumption in processing

Mastering metals is therefore the **first gateway** to Materials & Manufacturing Engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Metal Properties

#### (A) Mechanical Properties — Govern Load-Bearing Response

Mechanical properties dictate how a metal behaves under **force, deformation, or impact**.

**1. Strength** — Maximum stress a metal can withstand without failure.
- *Tensile strength (σ_UTS):* Maximum stress during stretching.
- *Compressive strength (σ_c):* Resistance to crushing.
- *Shear strength (τ):* Resistance to sliding failure.
- *Fatigue strength (σ_f):* Endurance under cyclic loading.

**2. Stiffness** — Resistance to elastic deformation.
- Measured by **Young's Modulus (E)**.
- High E → rigid (steel: E ≈ 200 GPa); Low E → flexible (aluminum: E ≈ 70 GPa).

**3. Ductility** — Ability to deform plastically under tensile load before fracture.
- Quantified by **percentage elongation (%EL)** and **percentage reduction in area (%RA)**.

**4. Malleability** — Ability to deform under **compressive** load (rolling, forging, hammering).

**5. Hardness** — Resistance to localized plastic deformation (scratch, indentation).
- Measured by Brinell (HB), Vickers (HV), Rockwell (HRC/HRB).

**6. Toughness** — Energy absorbed before fracture; area under the stress-strain curve up to fracture.

**7. Resilience** — Energy absorbed in the elastic region only; area up to yield point.

**8. Fatigue Limit (Endurance Limit)** — Stress below which the metal can survive infinite cycles (~10⁶–10⁷).

#### (B) Physical Properties — Govern Thermal, Electrical, Magnetic Response

| Property | Best-in-Class Metal | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest Density | Magnesium (Mg) | **1.74 g/cm³** |
| Highest Density | Osmium (Os) | **22.59 g/cm³** |
| Lowest Melting Point | Mercury (Hg) | **−38.83 °C** |
| Highest Melting Point | Tungsten (W) | **3422 °C** |
| Highest Electrical Conductivity | Silver (Ag) | **6.30 × 10⁷ S/m** |
| Highest Thermal Conductivity | Silver (Ag) | **429 W/m·K** |
| Ferromagnetic (at RT) | Fe, Co, Ni | Curie T ≈ 770, 1121, 354 °C |

#### (C) Chemical Properties — Govern Environmental Degradation

- **Corrosion Resistance:** Cr, Al, Ti, Au form passive oxide layers.
- **Oxidation Resistance:** Refractory metals (W, Mo) form volatile oxides at high T.
- **Galvanic Behavior:** Position in the **galvanic series** determines corrosion when dissimilar metals are coupled in an electrolyte.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Equations for Metal Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> The following table forms the **minimum quantitative toolkit** that KTU examiners expect you to memorize for solving numericals in ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Property / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | F in N, A₀ in m² → σ in Pa | Force ÷ original area |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | ΔL, L₀ in m → dimensionless | Often reported as % |
| 3 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | E in Pa (GPa common) | Valid only in elastic region |
| 4 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | — | Linear-elastic regime |
| 5 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | ν between 0.2 and 0.5 for metals | Incompressibility: ν = 0.5 |
| 6 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | G in Pa | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ |
| 7 | Bulk Modulus | $K = \dfrac{-P}{\Delta V / V_0}$ | K in Pa | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ |
| 8 | % Elongation | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | L_f = final gauge length | Direct ductility indicator |
| 9 | % Reduction in Area | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | A_f = fracture area | — |
| 10 | Brinell Hardness | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$ | F = load (kgf), D = ball dia, d = indent dia | Units kgf/mm² |
| 11 | Vickers Hardness | $HV = \dfrac{1.8544 \, F}{d^2}$ | F in kgf, d in mm | Diagonal-based; load-independent |
| 12 | Rockwell C Hardness | $HRC = 100 - \dfrac{h}{0.002}$ | h = depth of penetration (mm) | Diamond cone, 150 kgf |
| 13 | Resilience (Modulus) | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | J/m³ | Strain energy per unit volume |
| 14 | Toughness (approx.) | $U_t \approx \dfrac{\sigma_y + \sigma_{UTS}}{2} \cdot \varepsilon_f$ | J/m³ | Area under σ-ε curve |
| 15 | Fatigue Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Pa | For S-N (Wöhler) analysis |
| 16 | Endurance Ratio | $ER = \dfrac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_{UTS}}$ | Typically 0.4–0.6 for steels | σ_e = endurance limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For metals, always remember the order of magnitude benchmarks:
> - Steel: E ≈ **200 GPa**, ρ ≈ **7.85 g/cm³**, σ_UTS ≈ **400–2000 MPa**
> - Aluminum alloy: E ≈ **70 GPa**, ρ ≈ **2.70 g/cm³**, σ_UTS ≈ **90–600 MPa**
> - Pure copper: E ≈ **110 GPa**, ρ ≈ **8.96 g/cm³**, σ_UTS ≈ **220 MPa**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These property relationships are **not theoretical** — they directly drive:

- **Material selection charts** (Ashby plots): plotting strength vs density to pick aerospace alloys.
- **Manufacturing process selection:** e.g., low %EL metals → brittle → not suitable for deep drawing; high %EL → formable sheets.
- **Failure analysis:** identifying whether fracture was ductile (cup-cone, dimples) or brittle (cleavage facets).
- **Heat treatment design:** choosing quenching media based on hardenability and critical cooling rates.
- **Quality control:** hardness-to-tensile-strength conversion (HB ≈ 0.3 × σ_UTS for steels).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between the Three Elastic Constants (E, G, ν, K)

This is a **high-frequency KTU derivation**. We begin from the generalized **Hooke's Law for an isotropic linear elastic solid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** Once you understand the relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$, you can compute *any* elastic constant from *any two measured constants*. This is indispensable for KTU numerical problems where data is incomplete.

---

#### **Step 1 — Define the General 3D Stress State**

For a point in a body under normal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ and shear stresses $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$, the strains are given by:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

and shear relations:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

#### **Step 2 — Apply Hydrostatic Pressure (Uniform Compression)**

Let the body be subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$ (sign convention: compressive is negative). Since pressure acts equally in all directions, all strains are equal:

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon$$

Substitute into the first equation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P - \nu(-P - P)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P + 2\nu P\right]$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{-P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

#### **Step 3 — Express Volumetric Strain**

By definition, the volumetric strain is:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3\varepsilon$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

#### **Step 4 — Recall the Definition of Bulk Modulus**

The bulk modulus $K$ is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic pressure to volumetric strain:

$$K = -\frac{P}{\Delta V / V_0}$$

Substituting Step 3:

$$K = -\frac{P}{\dfrac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$

#### **Step 5 — Derive Relation Between E, G, and ν**

For a **pure shear** stress state, consider the relationship between shear modulus $G$ and Young's modulus $E$. Using the geometry of shear strain in an elemental cube:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

#### **Step 6 — Combined Master Relation**

Eliminating $\nu$ between the two boxed equations gives:

$$K = \frac{E \cdot G}{9G - 3E} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{1}{E} + \frac{3}{9K} = \frac{1}{3G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the **triangle mnemonic** —
> E at top vertex, G and K at base vertices, ν inside the triangle:
> - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
> - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
> - $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical 5–7 Mark Question):**
> A steel specimen has Young's modulus $E = 210\,\text{GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$. Calculate: (i) Shear Modulus $G$, (ii) Bulk Modulus $K$, (iii) Volumetric strain when subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa.

#### **Solution — Step by Step**

**Given:**
- $E = 210\,\text{GPa} = 210 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.30$
- $P = 200\,\text{MPa} = 200 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$

**Part (i) — Shear Modulus G**

Apply $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.30)}$$

$$G = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2.60} = 80.77 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 80.77\,\text{GPa}}$$

**Part (ii) — Bulk Modulus K**

Apply $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.30)}$$

$$K = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3 \times 0.40} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{1.20}$$

$$\boxed{K = 175\,\text{GPa}}$$

**Part (iii) — Volumetric Strain**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3 \times 200 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.60)}{210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{-3 \times 200 \times 10^6 \times 0.40}{210 \times 10^9} = \frac{-240 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = -1.143 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} \approx -1.143 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{(i.e., volume decreases by 0.1143\%)}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Steel is **nearly incompressible** — a hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa shrinks its volume by only 0.114%, confirming that metals resist volumetric change strongly.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Engineering Metals

| Property | Mild Steel | Cast Iron | Aluminum | Copper | Brass (Cu-30Zn) | Titanium |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.85 | 7.10 | 2.70 | 8.96 | 8.50 | 4.51 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 200 | 100 | 70 | 110 | 100 | 116 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 400–550 | 150–400 | 90–600 | 220 | 300–550 | 230–1400 |
| % Elongation | 25–40 | <1 | 12–45 | 45 | 30–60 | 10–30 |
| Melting Point (°C) | 1500 | 1150–1300 | 660 | 1085 | 900–940 | 1668 |
| Electrical Cond. (% IACS) | 15 | 10 | 60 | 100 | 28 | 4 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Low | Low | Good | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent |
| Relative Cost | Low | Very Low | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Typical Use | Structures, shafts | Engine blocks, pipes | Aircraft, cans | Wires, contacts | Fittings, decor | Aerospace, implants |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Metals

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete taxonomic hierarchy of metals covered in KTU Module 1. This single diagram is exam-ready for any "classify metals" or "explain classification" question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Engineering Metals Universe]
    nodeFerrous[Ferrous Metals - Fe Base]
    nodeNonFerrous[Non-Ferrous Metals - No Fe Base]
    nodeAlloys[Alloys - Two or More Metals]
    nodePigIron[Pig Iron - C 4 to 5%]
    nodeCastIron[Cast Iron - C 2 to 4%]
    nodeWroughtIron[Wrought Iron - C less than 0.1%]
    nodeSteel[Steel - C 0.02 to 2%]
    nodeCarbonSteel[Carbon Steel]
    nodeAlloySteel[Alloy Steel]
    nodeStainlessSteel[Stainless Steel]
    nodeLightMetals[Light Metals - Al Mg Ti]
    nodeHeavyMetals[Heavy Metals - Cu Zn Pb Sn Ni]
    nodePreciousMetals[Precious Metals - Au Ag Pt]
    nodeRefractoryMetals[Refractory - W Mo Ta]
    nodeBrass[Brass - Cu plus Zn]
    nodeBronze[Bronze - Cu plus Sn]
    nodeDuralumin[Duralumin - Al plus Cu Mg Mn]
    nodeInconel[Inconel - Ni plus Cr Fe]

    nodeStart --> nodeFerrous
    nodeStart --> nodeNonFerrous
    nodeStart --> nodeAlloys

    nodeFerrous --> nodePigIron
    nodeFerrous --> nodeCastIron
    nodeFerrous --> nodeWroughtIron
    nodeFerrous --> nodeSteel

    nodeSteel --> nodeCarbonSteel
    nodeSteel --> nodeAlloySteel
    nodeAlloySteel --> nodeStainlessSteel

    nodeNonFerrous --> nodeLightMetals
    nodeNonFerrous --> nodeHeavyMetals
    nodeNonFerrous --> nodePreciousMetals
    nodeNonFerrous --> nodeRefractoryMetals

    nodeAlloys --> nodeBrass
    nodeAlloys --> nodeBronze
    nodeAlloys --> nodeDuralumin
    nodeAlloys --> nodeInconel

    nodeCarbonSteel -.Hot rolled beams shafts.-> nodeCarbonSteel
    nodeStainlessSteel -.Cutlery chemical tanks.-> nodeStainlessSteel
    nodeBrass -.Valves plumbing.-> nodeBrass
    nodeDuralumin -.Aircraft frames.-> nodeDuralumin
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Stress-Strain Response of Metals

Since Mermaid cannot plot curves, the following **block-level architecture** maps the *physical stages* along a typical engineering stress-strain curve for a ductile metal.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pointA[Stage 1: Origin - No Load]
    pointB[Stage 2: Elastic Limit - Hooke Region]
    pointC[Stage 3: Yield Point - Permanent Set Begins]
    pointD[Stage 4: Strain Hardening - Plastic Flow]
    pointE[Stage 5: Ultimate Tensile Strength Peak]
    pointF[Stage 6: Necking - Localized Reduction]
    pointG[Stage 7: Fracture - Final Failure]

    pointA -->|Slope equals E| pointB
    pointB -->|Slope decreases| pointC
    pointC -->|Steady plastic flow| pointD
    pointD -->|Load reaches max| pointE
    pointE -->|Cross section shrinks| pointF
    pointF -->|Crack propagates| pointG

    blockMod1[Modulus E equals stress by strain]
    blockMod2[Yield Strength sigma_y]
    blockMod3[UTS sigma max]
    blockMod4[% Elongation and % RA]

    pointB -.Annotation.-> blockMod1
    pointC -.Annotation.-> blockMod2
    pointE -.Annotation.-> blockMod3
    pointG -.Annotation.-> blockMod4
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Crystal Structure & Slip Mechanism

The metallic bond enables plastic deformation through **dislocation motion** along specific slip planes. The diagram below shows the logical chain from atomic bonding to macroscopic ductility.

```mermaid
graph TD
    atomNode[Metal Atoms in FCC BCC HCP Lattice]
    bondNode[Metallic Bond - Electron Sea Model]
    planeNode[Densely Packed Slip Planes - 111 for FCC]
    dislocNode[Dislocations Edge Screw Mixed]
    motionNode[Dislocation Motion Under Shear Stress]
    slipNode[Slip Step Formation on Surface]
    macroNode[Macroscopic Plastic Deformation]

    atomNode --> bondNode
    bondNode --> planeNode
    planeNode --> dislocNode
    dislocNode --> motionNode
    motionNode --> slipNode
    slipNode --> macroNode

    propBlock[Why FCC metals like Al Cu Au are ductile]
    propBlock2[Why HCP metals like Mg Ti are less ductile at RT]
    motionNode -.Link.-> propBlock
    motionNode -.Link.-> propBlock2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway from the diagram:** FCC metals have **12 slip systems** (highly ductile); BCC has **48 slip systems** but limited at low T; HCP has only **3 slip systems** → brittle at room temperature. This single fact explains **why aluminum sheets can be deep-drawn but magnesium cannot**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with respect to metals: (i) Malleability, (ii) Ductility, (iii) Toughness.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(i) Malleability** — It is the ability of a metal to deform plastically under **compressive** loads (e.g., rolling, hammering, forging) without cracking. Gold and lead are highly malleable. Quantified indirectly by the ability to be rolled into thin sheets.
>
> **(ii) Ductility** — It is the ability of a metal to undergo large **plastic deformation under tensile** loads before fracture. Quantified by **percentage elongation (%EL)** and **percentage reduction in area (%RA)**. Copper and aluminum are highly ductile.
>
> **(iii) Toughness** — It is the total energy absorbed by a metal up to fracture, equivalent to the **area under the stress-strain curve**. A tough metal is both strong and ductile. Measured by **impact tests** (Izod, Charpy).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between ferrous and non-ferrous metals. Give two examples of each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Ferrous Metals | Non-Ferrous Metals |
> |---|---|---|
> | Base metal | Iron (Fe) is principal constituent | Iron is absent or minor |
> | Rusting | Highly prone to rust (Fe₂O₃) | Generally corrosion-resistant |
> | Density | Mostly >7 g/cm³ | Wide range (0.5–22 g/cm³) |
> | Magnetic | Mostly ferromagnetic | Mostly non-magnetic |
> | Cost | Generally lower | Often higher (Cu, Ti, Ni) |
> | Examples | Cast iron, Mild steel, Stainless steel | Aluminum, Copper, Zinc, Lead, Tin, Titanium |
> | Applications | Structures, machinery, tools | Aerospace, electrical, chemical |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Metals" Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT write only definitions** for 14-mark questions. Examiners expect **classification + properties + applications + comparison table + at least one labelled diagram**.
> 2. **Always quantify properties numerically** (e.g., "Mild steel: σ_UTS ≈ 410 MPa" rather than just "high strength"). Generic adjectives lose marks.
> 3. **Connect properties to applications** — never list them separately. Say "Aluminum's low density (2.7 g/cm³) makes it ideal for aircraft frames."
> 4. **Avoid confusing cast iron and steel** — they are NOT the same. Cast iron has C > 2%; steel has C < 2%.
> 5. **Always state units** for any property mentioned.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify engineering metals into ferrous and non-ferrous categories. With a neat flowchart, explain the complete hierarchy. List the principal properties and applications of each major class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the properties, types, and engineering applications of **cast iron**. Compare grey cast iron and white cast iron. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Classification of Engineering Metals:**

**1. Ferrous Metals** (Fe is base metal, >50% by weight):
- **Pig Iron:** Directly from blast furnace. Contains 4–5% C, plus Si, Mn, S, P. Brittle, used as raw material for steelmaking.
- **Cast Iron:** 2–4% C. Re-melted pig iron with controlled composition. Brittle but excellent castability, damping capacity, and machinability. Used for engine blocks, machine tool beds, pipes.
- **Wrought Iron:** <0.1% C, very pure. Tough, ductile, corrosion-resistant. Used for chains, hooks, ornamental gates.
- **Steel:** 0.02–2% C. The most versatile engineering metal.
  - **Carbon Steel:** Plain Fe + C (with Mn, Si, S, P). Sub-classified as Low (<0.3% C), Medium (0.3–0.6%), High (>0.6%).
  - **Alloy Steel:** Additional alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, V) for enhanced properties.
  - **Stainless Steel:** >10.5% Cr for corrosion resistance. Types: Austenitic (304, 316), Ferritic (430), Martensitic (410).

**2. Non-Ferrous Metals:**
- **Light Metals:** Al (2.7 g/cm³), Mg (1.74 g/cm³), Ti (4.51 g/cm³) — aerospace, transport.
- **Heavy Metals:** Cu (8.96 g/cm³), Zn (7.14 g/cm³), Pb (11.34 g/cm³), Sn (7.31 g/cm³), Ni (8.90 g/cm³).
- **Precious Metals:** Au, Ag, Pt — electronics, jewellery, catalysis.
- **Refractory Metals:** W (3422 °C m.p.), Mo (2623 °C), Ta, Nb — high-temperature service.

*(Insert flowchart as per Section 4.1 above for full marks.)*

**Properties & Applications Summary:**

| Class | Key Properties | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon Steel | High strength, cheap, weldable | Structures, rails, gears |
| Stainless Steel | Corrosion-resistant, hygienic | Cutlery, chemical tanks, implants |
| Cast Iron | Brittle, high damping, fluid when molten | Engine blocks, pipes, machine beds |
| Aluminum Alloys | Light, corrosion-resistant, ductile | Aircraft, beverage cans, window frames |
| Copper Alloys | Excellent conductor, ductile | Wiring, motors, heat exchangers |
| Titanium Alloys | Highest strength-to-weight, biocompatible | Jet engines, spacecraft, bone implants |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — Part (b): Cast Iron**

**Definition:** Cast iron is a group of **Fe-C alloys containing >2% C** (typically 2–4%), along with 1–3% Si and traces of Mn, S, P. Because of high C content, it has **low melting point (~1150–1300 °C)**, excellent fluidity, and good castability — making it the **most widely cast metal**.

**General Properties of Cast Iron:**
1. High **compressive strength** (600–1000 MPa) but **low tensile strength** (150–400 MPa).
2. **Brittle** — % elongation typically < 1%.
3. Excellent **damping capacity** (vibration absorption) — 20–30× that of steel. **This is why machine tool beds are made of cast iron.**
4. High wear resistance due to graphite flakes/pearlite structure.
5. Good machinability (graphite acts as chip-breaker and lubricant).
6. Low cost — cheap raw material (pig iron) and simple casting process.

**Types of Cast Iron:**

| Type | C Form | Microstructure | Properties | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Grey Cast Iron** | Graphite flakes | Pearlite + Ferrite + Graphite flakes | Good machinability, damping; brittle | Engine blocks, cylinder heads, pipes |
| **White Cast Iron** | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Pearlite + Cementite | Very hard, brittle, wear-resistant | Mill liners, crusher parts, slurry pumps |
| **Malleable Cast Iron** | Temper graphite (rosettes) | Ferrite + Temper carbon | Ductile, tough, machinable | Pipe fittings, agricultural implements |
| **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** | Spheroidal graphite | Pearlite/Ferrite + Spherical graphite | High strength, ductile | Crankshafts, gears, pipes |
| **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)** | Short, thick graphite | Pearlite + Vermicular graphite | Combines strength + thermal conductivity | Diesel engine blocks, brake discs |

**Grey vs White Cast Iron — Comparison:**

| Parameter | Grey Cast Iron | White Cast Iron |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon form | Graphite flakes | Iron carbide (cementite, Fe₃C) |
| Colour of fracture | Grey (due to graphite) | White (due to cementite) |
| Hardness | 150–250 HB | 400–600 HB |
| Strength | Moderate (UTS 150–400 MPa) | Very hard, brittle |
| Machinability | Excellent | Very poor (requires grinding) |
| Damping capacity | High | Low |
| Composition | High Si, slow cooling | Low Si, fast cooling |
| Applications | Engine blocks, pipes | Wear surfaces, crushers |

**Cooling rate is the deciding factor:** Slow cooling → C precipitates as graphite → Grey. Fast cooling → C remains as Fe₃C → White.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the classification, composition, properties, and applications of **stainless steels**. Differentiate between austenitic, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the properties and applications of the following non-ferrous metals: (i) Aluminum and its alloys, (ii) Copper and its alloys, (iii) Titanium and its alloys. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — Part (a): Stainless Steel**

**Definition:** Stainless steel is a class of **Fe-based alloys containing a minimum of 10.5% Chromium (Cr)**, which forms a passive, self-healing Cr₂O₃ layer (~2 nm thick) on the surface. This layer prevents further oxidation, giving the alloy "stainless" behavior. Other alloying elements (Ni, Mo, Mn, N) may be added for specific property tuning.

**Why Cr matters:** At ≥10.5% Cr, the entire surface is covered by a continuous, adherent, self-repairing oxide film. If scratched, the film re-forms instantly in oxygen-containing environments. This is the **single most important concept** in stainless steel metallurgy.

**General Properties:**
- Excellent corrosion/oxidation resistance
- High strength (UTS 500–2000 MPa depending on grade)
- Good formability and weldability (austenitic grades)
- Hygienic and easy to clean — ideal for food/pharma
- Aesthetic surface finish
- Retains strength at elevated temperatures (up to ~800 °C for heat-resistant grades)
- 100% recyclable

**Classification of Stainless Steel:**

| Class | Crystal Structure | Cr (%) | Ni (%) | Magnetic? | Hardenable? | Typical Grades | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Austenitic** | FCC (γ-Fe) | 16–26 | 6–22 | No | No (work-hardens) | 304, 316, 321 | Kitchen sinks, chemical tanks, surgical implants |
| **Ferritic** | BCC (α-Fe) | 11–18 | <1 | Yes | No | 430, 409 | Automotive exhaust, architectural trim |
| **Martensitic** | BCT (α' after quench) | 11–18 | <1 | Yes | Yes (by heat treatment) | 410, 420, 440C | Cutlery, blades, surgical tools, turbine blades |
| **Duplex** | Mixed FCC + BCC | 18–28 | 4–8 | Yes (weak) | No | 2205, 2507 | Marine, oil & gas pipelines |
| **Precipitation-Hardening (PH)** | Martensitic + precipitates | 12–18 | 4–9 | Yes | Yes (aging) | 17-4 PH, 15-5 PH | Aerospace fittings, high-strength shafts |

**Detailed Differentiations:**

**1. Austenitic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 304 — "18/8" steel):**
- Composition: 18% Cr, 8% Ni.
- Crystal structure: **FCC** (stabilized by Ni). Cannot be hardened by quenching — but can be **work-hardened** (e.g., 301 in deep-drawn kitchen sinks).
- **Non-magnetic** in annealed condition.
- Excellent corrosion resistance, ductility, weldability.
- Maximum service temperature ~870 °C.

**2. Ferritic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 430):**
- Composition: 16–18% Cr, very low Ni.
- Crystal structure: **BCC** (stabilized by Cr). Magnetic.
- **Cannot be hardened** by heat treatment.
- Moderate corrosion resistance (less than austenitic).
- Excellent resistance to **stress corrosion cracking**.
- Cheaper than austenitic.

**3. Martensitic Stainless Steel (e.g., SS 410, 420):**
- Composition: 12–18% Cr, low Ni.
- Crystal structure: **BCT** (formed by rapid quenching from austenite region).
- **Hardenable by heat treatment** (quench + temper), reaching up to 60 HRC.
- Magnetic.
- Best for **cutting edges, surgical tools, bearings**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — Part (b): Non-Ferrous Metals**

**(i) Aluminum and its Alloys:**

Pure aluminum (Al ≥ 99.0%) is soft and weak. Alloying with Cu, Mg, Si, Mn, Zn produces a wide range of **high-strength, lightweight alloys** — the backbone of aerospace and transport.

**Key Properties:**
- **Very low density:** 2.70 g/cm³ (about **1/3 that of steel**).
- Excellent **corrosion resistance** due to self-healing Al₂O₃ layer.
- High **thermal (237 W/m·K)** and **electrical conductivity (~60% IACS)** — second only to Cu, Ag.
- Highly **ductile** and **malleable** — can be rolled to 6 µm foils.
- **Non-magnetic**, **non-sparking**, **non-toxic**.
- Easily recyclable (recycling saves 95% energy vs primary production).

**Wrought Alloy Designation System (e.g., 2024, 6061, 7075):**

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1xxx | Pure Al (>99%) | 1050, 1100 | Foils, electrical busbars |
| 2xxx | Cu | 2014, 2024 | Aircraft structures (Duralumin) |
| 3xxx | Mn | 3003, 3105 | Beverage cans, cookware |
| 5xxx | Mg | 5052, 5083 | Marine, ship hulls |
| 6xxx | Mg + Si | 6061, 6063 | Extrusions, window frames |
| 7xxx | Zn | 7075 | Aircraft, high-stress parts |

**Cast Aluminum Alloys:** Designated by 3-digit + .0 (e.g., 356.0, 380.0). Common in engine pistons, cylinder heads.

---

**(ii) Copper and its Alloys:**

**Key Properties of Pure Cu:**
- Density: 8.96 g/cm³.
- **Highest electrical conductivity** of any metal after silver (5.96 × 10⁷ S/m).
- High thermal conductivity (401 W/m·K).
- Excellent ductility (%EL up to 45%) and malleability.
- Good corrosion resistance (forms protective Cu₂O/CuO patina).
- Non-magnetic.

**Major Copper Alloys:**

| Alloy | Composition | Properties | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brass** | Cu + Zn (5–45% Zn) | Yellow, ductile, machinable | Cartridge cases, plumbing, decorative |
| **Bronze** | Cu + Sn (3–20% Sn) | Hard, wear-resistant | Bearings, bushings, statues |
| **Aluminum Bronze** | Cu + Al (5–12% Al) | High strength, corrosion-resistant | Marine propellers, pumps |
| **Cupronickel** | Cu + Ni (10–30% Ni) | Excellent seawater resistance | Condenser tubes, coinage |
| **Beryllium Copper** | Cu + 1.8% Be + Co | Very high strength, non-sparking | Springs, electrical contacts, tools |

---

**(iii) Titanium and its Alloys:**

**Key Properties:**
- Density: **4.51 g/cm³** (60% of steel).
- Very high melting point: **1668 °C**.
- Exceptional **strength-to-weight ratio** (specific strength best among metals).
- Outstanding **corrosion resistance** (forms TiO₂ passive layer).
- **Biocompatible** — integrates with bone and tissue.
- Non-magnetic.
- Poor thermal/electrical conductivity (poor heat conductor).

**Alloys:**
- **α-alloys** (e.g., Ti-5Al-2.5Sn): Single-phase HCP. Weldable, good high-T creep strength. Used in jet engine compressor blades.
- **α-β alloys** (e.g., **Ti-6Al-4V** — "workhorse" alloy): Most widely used Ti alloy. Aerospace structural parts, biomedical implants.
- **β-alloys** (e.g., Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al): BCC, high strength, formable. Used in landing gear, fasteners.

**Applications:** Jet engine compressor discs and blades, airframe components, spacecraft, submarine hulls, chemical reactors, surgical implants (hip joints, dental), golf clubs, watch cases.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Module 1 — Metals — Rapid Revision Points:**

- ⭐ **Ferrous metals** contain iron as the principal element (>50%); **non-ferrous metals** do not. Always state this distinction first.
- ⭐ **Cast iron:** C > 2%; **Steel:** C 0.02–2%; **Wrought iron:** C < 0.1%; **Pig iron:** C 4–5%.
- ⭐ **Grey cast iron** has graphite flakes → grey fracture, good damping, brittle. **White cast iron** has Fe₃C → white fracture, hard, brittle, wear-resistant. **Ductile/Nodular cast iron** has spheroidal graphite → high strength + ductility.
- ⭐ **Stainless steel** must contain **minimum 10.5% Cr** to be "stainless." **Austenitic** (FCC, non-magnetic, Ni-stabilized, e.g., 304). **Ferritic** (BCC, magnetic, no Ni, e.g., 430). **Martensitic** (BCT, magnetic, hardenable, e.g., 410).
- ⭐ **Aluminum alloys** are designated by 4-digit system: 2xxx (Cu, Duralumin for aircraft), 6xxx (Mg+Si, extrusions), 7xxx (Zn, highest strength). Wrought alloys = 4-digit; cast alloys = 3-digit + .0.
- ⭐ **Brass** = Cu + Zn; **Bronze** = Cu + Sn (originally, now broader); **Cupronickel** = Cu + Ni (seawater resistant); **Beryllium copper** = highest strength Cu alloy, non-sparking.
- ⭐ **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** (Grade 5) is the most widely used titanium alloy — aerospace + biomedical.
- ⭐ **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Ta, Nb) have melting points > 2000 °C. Tungsten has the highest melting point of any pure metal (3422 °C).
- ⭐ **Precious metals** (Au, Ag, Pt group) are valued for **chemical inertness**, **conductivity**, and **catalytic activity**.
- ⭐ **Mechanical property essentials:** $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$, $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$, $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$. Typical E values: Steel 200 GPa, Al 70 GPa, Cu 110 GPa, Ti 116 GPa.
- ⭐ **Hardness conversion:** For steels, $\sigma_{UTS}(\text{MPa}) \approx 3.45 \times HB$ (approx).
- ⭐ **Ductility metric:** % Elongation > 5% → ductile; < 5% → brittle (for metals).
- ⭐ **FCC** metals (Cu, Al, Au, γ-Fe) → ductile (12 slip systems). **HCP** metals (Mg, Zn, Ti α) → brittle at RT (3 slip systems). **BCC** metals (α-Fe, W, Cr) → brittle at low T (ductile-to-brittle transition).

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Warning:** In 14-mark questions, **always include**:
> (1) a classification table or hierarchy,
> (2) numerical property values with units,
> (3) a labelled block diagram (machine-drawn flowcharts preferred),
> (4) at least one **comparison** (e.g., grey vs white, austenitic vs ferritic vs martensitic),
> (5) at least 3–4 industrial applications with the **property-to-application reasoning** clearly explained.
> Generic text without these elements typically scores 6–8/14, not full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Polymers

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight organic compound (macromolecule) formed by the chemical combination of a very large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, linked together through covalent bonds via a process termed **polymerization**. The term is derived from the Greek words *poly* (many) and *meros* (parts), literally meaning "many parts."

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines polymers under the broader domain of engineering materials as substances whose primary structural feature is the long-chain arrangement of atoms (predominantly **carbon**, **hydrogen**, **oxygen**, **nitrogen**, **sulfur**, and **silicon**) that gives rise to characteristic engineering properties such as viscoelasticity, low density, corrosion resistance, and tunable mechanical strength.

The fundamental repeating unit in a polymer chain is called the **mer**. The total number of mers in a polymer molecule is the **degree of polymerization (DP or n)**, and the product of *n* and the molecular weight of a single mer (*M₀*) yields the polymer's molecular weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Definition Markers**
> Examiners expect students to mention three keywords when defining a polymer: **macromolecule**, **repeating unit (mer)**, and **degree of polymerization**. A definition missing any of these three terms may be marked incomplete in valuation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Think of a polymer as a **train**:
- Each **monomer** is a single, identical **railway coach**.
- The **polymer chain** is the entire **train** formed by joining hundreds or thousands of these coaches one after another.
- The **degree of polymerization (n)** is simply the **number of coaches** in the train.
- The **mechanical strength**, **flexibility**, and **melting point** of the polymer increase as the train becomes longer and heavier.

A single monomer is a gas or a low-viscosity liquid (think: ethylene gas, C₂H₄). When thousands of ethylene units join end-to-end, they form **polyethylene** — a tough, solid plastic that can hold water, wrap food, or insulate electrical cables. The transition from a single small molecule to a giant chain is what transforms an invisible gas into a tangible engineering material.

### 1.3 Historical & Engineering Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Background Knowledge**
> - **1839** – Charles Goodyear vulcanized natural rubber (first polymer modification).
> - **1907** – Leo Baekeland invented **Bakelite**, the world's first fully synthetic polymer.
> - **1922** – Hermann Staudinger proposed the macromolecular hypothesis (Nobel Prize 1953).
> - **1935** – Wallace Carothers at DuPont invented **nylon** and **neoprene** — the foundation of modern polymer engineering.

Today, polymers constitute roughly **18%** by volume of all materials used in modern engineering, second only to metals and alloys. They are indispensable in aerospace composites, biomedical implants, electronic packaging, automotive components, and additive manufacturing.

### 1.4 Classification Overview (The Foundation for Module 1)

Polymers are classified along **three orthogonal axes**, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw or describe these hierarchies:

| **Classification Axis** | **Categories** | **Example Polymers** |
|---|---|---|
| Source / Origin | Natural, Semi-synthetic, Synthetic | Cellulose, Cellulose acetate, Polythene |
| Thermal Response | Thermoplastic, Thermosetting, Elastomer | PP, Epoxy, Natural rubber |
| Polymerization Mechanism | Addition (Chain-growth), Condensation (Step-growth) | PVC, Nylon 6,6 |

This three-way classification is the **gateway** to the rest of the module; every subsequent property, application, and selection criterion flows from these categorizations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architecture of a Polymer Chain

Every polymer exhibits a hierarchy of structural organization that ultimately dictates its macroscopic engineering properties. The KTU 2024 curriculum expects students to recognize **four** distinct structural levels:

1. **Monomer (M₀)** – The smallest chemical unit capable of polymerizing.
2. **Repeat Unit (Mer)** – The structural motif that repeats along the chain.
3. **Macromolecule (Polymer Chain)** – The complete chain of *n* repeat units.
4. **Polymer Morphology** – The supramolecular arrangement (amorphous vs. semi-crystalline) of multiple chains.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification Logic (Step-by-Step)

#### A. Classification Based on **Source / Origin**

- **Natural Polymers**: Occur in nature without human synthesis. Examples: **Natural rubber (polyisoprene)**, **cellulose** (plant cell walls), **proteins**, **starch**, **silk**, and **DNA**. These are biodegradable and renewable.
- **Semi-Synthetic Polymers**: Chemically modified natural polymers. Examples: **Cellulose acetate (CA)**, **nitrocellulose**, **rayon**, and **vulcanized rubber**. Industry treats natural rubber with sulfur to cross-link the chains — this is a *semi-synthetic* modification.
- **Synthetic Polymers**: Entirely man-made from petrochemical feedstocks. Examples: **Polyethylene (PE)**, **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)**, **Nylon 6,6**, **Epoxy**, **Teflon (PTFE)**. These dominate the modern engineering market.

#### B. Classification Based on **Thermal Response**

This classification has the **highest weightage** in KTU questions because it directly determines processing method (injection molding, extrusion, compression molding, etc.).

- **Thermoplastics**: Linear or branched chains held together by weak **secondary bonds** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonds). On heating, they **soften and melt**; on cooling, they **solidify reversibly**. They can be remelted and reprocessed multiple times — making them **recyclable**. Examples: PE, PP, PS, PVC, PMMA, Nylon, PTFE. Think of a **wax candle** — melt it, it becomes liquid; cool it, it solidifies again.
- **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets)**: Chains are heavily **cross-linked** in three dimensions by strong **covalent bonds**. Once set, heating causes **degradation (charring), not melting**. They are **non-recyclable** by remelting. Examples: **Bakelite**, **Epoxy**, **Phenolic resins**, **Urea-formaldehyde**, **Melamine**, **Polyester resins (cured)**. Think of a **boiled egg** — once cooked, you cannot "un-cook" it.
- **Elastomers (Rubbers)**: Lightly cross-linked polymers with a **rubbery, elastic behavior**. They undergo **large elastic deformation (up to 500–1000%)** under stress and recover their original shape on stress release. The cross-links prevent permanent flow. Examples: **Natural rubber (vulcanized)**, **Neoprene**, **Silicone rubber**, **Polyurethane elastomer**, **Butyl rubber**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table (Frequently asked in KTU)**
> | Property | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Elastomer |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Cross-linking | None | Heavy 3D network | Light cross-links |
> | Heating behavior | Softens, melts | Degrades, chars | Softens, but does not flow |
> | Recyclability | Excellent | Very poor | Limited |
> | Young's modulus | 0.1 – 5 GPa | 2 – 10 GPa | 0.001 – 0.1 GPa |
> | Examples | PE, PP, PVC | Epoxy, Bakelite | Natural rubber, Silicone |

#### C. Classification Based on **Polymerization Mechanism**

- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization**: Monomers with **C=C double bonds** add to a growing chain one at a time via a reactive intermediate (free radical, cation, or anion). **No byproduct** is released. The empirical formula of the polymer is identical to that of the monomer. Examples: Polyethylene from ethylene, PVC from vinyl chloride.
- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization**: Bifunctional or polyfunctional monomers react in **stepwise** fashion, with the elimination of a **small molecule** (water, HCl, methanol, ammonia) as a byproduct. Examples: Nylon 6,6 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid (releases water); PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid (releases water); Bakelite from phenol + formaldehyde (releases water).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every** equation, constant, and unit students must memorize for this topic. The **polydispersity index (PDI)** and **Carothers equation** are KTU-favorite exam topics.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning of Symbols** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | $M_n$ = number-average mol. weight, $M_0$ = mer weight | Controls tensile strength, viscosity, melting point |
| Number-Average Molecular Weight | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i \, M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of molecules of size $M_i$ | Used in osmotic pressure measurements |
| Weight-Average Molecular Weight | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i \, M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i \, M_i}$ | Same as above | Used in light scattering, sedimentation |
| Polydispersity Index (PDI) | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Measures molecular weight distribution breadth | PDI = 1 → monodisperse (theoretical); PDI > 1 → polydisperse |
| Carothers Equation (Step Polymerization) | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$ | $X_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization, $p$ = fraction of functional groups reacted | Predicts chain length at any conversion $p$ |
| Carothers Equation (with stoichiometric imbalance) | $X_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | $r$ = stoichiometric ratio (minor/major functional groups), $p$ = conversion | Real case where monomers are not exactly equimolar |
| Free-Radical Rate of Polymerization | $R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M]$ | $k_p$ = propagation rate constant, $f$ = initiator efficiency, $k_d$ = initiator decomposition constant, $k_t$ = termination rate constant, $[I]$ = initiator conc., $[M]$ = monomer conc. | Designing industrial reactors |
| Flory-Fox Equation | $\eta_{inh} = K \cdot M_v^a$ | Intrinsic viscosity $\eta_{inh}$ relates to viscosity-average mol. weight $M_v$; $K$ and $a$ are polymer–solvent constants | Viscometry for molecular weight determination |
| Glass Transition Relation (Fox) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction, $T_{gi}$ = glass transition of pure components | Predicts $T_g$ of polymer blends/copolymers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Valuation**
> The Carothers equation $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ is valid **only for step-growth polymerization with exact stoichiometric equivalence**. If the stoichiometry is imbalanced (e.g., one monomer in slight excess), the modified form $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ must be used. Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for stating which version applies.

### 2.4 The Polymerization Reaction Kinetics (Why and How)

In **free-radical chain polymerization** (the most industrially important addition mechanism), three distinct stages occur:

1. **Initiation**: An initiator molecule (typically **benzoyl peroxide, BPO** or **azobisisobutyronitrile, AIBN**) thermally decomposes to form two free radicals. The radical attacks a monomer's C=C double bond, opening it and creating a new radical on the monomer.

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet} \quad \text{(initiator decomposition)}$$

$$R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} R-M^{\bullet} \quad \text{(radical attack on monomer)}$$

2. **Propagation**: The active chain radical sequentially adds monomer units at a very high rate (~1000 monomers per second in PE polymerization).

$$R-M^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} R-MM^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_p} \ldots \xrightarrow{k_p} R-(M)_n^{\bullet}$$

3. **Termination**: The active chain radical is deactivated by **combination** (two radicals join) or **disproportionation** (hydrogen transfer). At this instant, the chain stops growing.

$$R-(M)_n^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}(M)_m-R \xrightarrow{k_t} R-(M)_{n+m}-R \quad \text{(combination)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering**
> - **Reaction temperature** controls the molecular weight: higher temperature → faster termination → shorter chains → lower mechanical strength.
> - **Initiator concentration** controls the number of chains started: more initiator → more chains, but shorter average length.
> - **Inhibitors** (like hydroquinone) are deliberately added to commercial monomers to prevent unwanted polymerization during storage — a critical safety and logistics consideration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Carothers Equation for Step-Growth Polymerization

The Carothers equation is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams on this topic. The derivation is shown in full, with each algebraic step explicitly justified.

**Starting Premise**:
Consider a step-growth polymerization between **A-A** and **B-B** bifunctional monomers (e.g., a diacid and a diamine forming Nylon). Let:
- $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules initially present
- $N$ = total number of molecules (monomers + oligomers + polymers) remaining at conversion $p$
- $p$ = fraction of functional groups (A or B) that have reacted

**Step 1: Express the Number of Bonds Formed**

At conversion $p$, the number of A-groups (or B-groups) that have reacted is:

$$\text{Number of bonds formed} = p \cdot N_0$$

**Step 2: Relate Number of Molecules to Bonds Formed**

In a step-growth polymerization, **every bond formed reduces the total molecule count by exactly one**. Initially there are $N_0$ molecules. After forming $pN_0$ bonds, the total number of molecules is:

$$N = N_0 - p \cdot N_0 = N_0 (1 - p)$$

**Step 3: Define the Number-Average Degree of Polymerization**

By definition, the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$ is the initial number of monomer molecules divided by the final number of molecules:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 2 into Step 3**

Substituting $N = N_0(1-p)$:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1 - p)} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

**Step 5: Conclude with the Carothers Equation**

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}}$$

**Physical Interpretation**:
- When $p = 0$ (no reaction yet) → $X_n = 1$ (only monomers exist, no polymer chains).
- When $p = 0.5$ (half the groups reacted) → $X_n = 2$ (mostly dimers and trimers).
- When $p = 0.9$ (90% conversion) → $X_n = 10$ (still very short chains).
- When $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion) → $X_n = 100$ (engineering-grade polymer emerges).
- When $p \to 1$ (100% conversion) → $X_n \to \infty$ (infinite network — gelation in thermosets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway**: To achieve a useful degree of polymerization ($X_n \approx 100$ to $1000$), the conversion must exceed **99%**. This is why step-growth polymerization requires **extremely pure monomers** and **rigorous removal of the small-molecule byproduct** (Le Chatelier's principle drives the reaction forward).

### 3.2 Derivation: Polydispersity Index from Statistical Definitions

**Step 1: Define Number Fraction $n_i$**

The number fraction of molecules of size $M_i$ is:

$$n_i = \frac{N_i}{\sum_j N_j}$$

**Step 2: Rewrite $M_n$ in Terms of Number Fractions**

$$M_n = \sum_i n_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 3: Define Weight Fraction $w_i$**

The weight fraction of molecules of size $M_i$ is:

$$w_i = \frac{N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_j N_j \cdot M_j} = n_i \cdot \frac{M_i}{M_n}$$

**Step 4: Rewrite $M_w$ in Terms of Weight Fractions**

$$M_w = \sum_i w_i \cdot M_i = \sum_i n_i \cdot \frac{M_i^2}{M_n}$$

**Step 5: Express PDI**

$$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{\sum_i n_i M_i^2 / M_n}{\sum_i n_i M_i} = \frac{\sum_i n_i M_i^2}{M_n^2}$$

**Step 6: Recognize the Variance**

By the statistical definition of variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \sum_i n_i (M_i - M_n)^2 = \sum_i n_i M_i^2 - M_n^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{PDI = 1 + \frac{\sigma^2}{M_n^2}}$$

**Physical Interpretation**: PDI equals **1** when all molecules are identical in size (monodisperse, $M_w = M_n$). Any spread in molecular weight raises PDI above 1. Commercial synthetic polymers typically have PDI between **1.5 and 5**; living polymerizations can approach **1.05**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Engineering Polymer Property Database

The following table is the **definitive property reference** for the polymers most commonly asked in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Students are expected to know the values (at least order of magnitude) for comparison-based questions.

| **Polymer** | **Abbreviation** | **M₀ (g/mol)** | **Density (g/cm³)** | **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | **Glass Transition T_g (°C)** | **Melting Point (°C)** | **Major Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Density Polyethylene | LDPE | 28 | 0.91 – 0.94 | 10 – 25 | -110 | 115 | Plastic bags, squeeze bottles |
| High-Density Polyethylene | HDPE | 28 | 0.94 – 0.97 | 25 – 40 | -110 | 130 | Pipes, bottles, geomembranes |
| Polypropylene | PP | 42 | 0.90 – 0.91 | 30 – 40 | -10 | 165 | Automotive parts, packaging, hinges |
| Polyvinyl Chloride | PVC | 62.5 | 1.30 – 1.45 | 50 – 80 | 80 | 240 (decomposes) | Pipes, cable insulation, window profiles |
| Polystyrene | PS | 104 | 1.04 – 1.06 | 30 – 50 | 100 | 240 (decomposes) | Disposable cups, insulation foam |
| Polymethyl Methacrylate | PMMA | 100 | 1.17 – 1.20 | 70 – 80 | 105 | 160 | Aircraft windows, lenses, signage |
| Polytetrafluoroethylene | PTFE (Teflon) | 100 | 2.10 – 2.20 | 20 – 35 | 115 | 327 | Non-stick coatings, gaskets, bearings |
| Nylon 6,6 | PA 6,6 | 226 | 1.13 – 1.15 | 75 – 90 | 50 | 265 | Gears, bearings, tire cord, fibers |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | PET | 192 | 1.30 – 1.40 | 55 – 75 | 70 | 260 | Bottles, films, fibers |
| Epoxy (cured) | EP | varies | 1.10 – 1.40 | 35 – 100 | varies | crosslinked | Adhesives, composites, electronics |
| Phenol-Formaldehyde | PF (Bakelite) | varies | 1.25 – 1.30 | 30 – 50 | varies | crosslinked | Electrical switches, pan handles |
| Natural Rubber (vulcanized) | NR | 68 | 0.92 – 0.98 | 20 – 30 | -73 | 35 (softens) | Tires, seals, vibration dampers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reading Insight**
> The values shown are typical **room-temperature** values from standard references. KTU questions may ask students to identify, for example, *"which polymer among PE, PP, PVC, and PTFE has the highest density and why?"* — the answer relies on fluorine atoms in PTFE being the heaviest substituent, raising density to ~2.2 g/cm³.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Part B Practice)

**Problem**: A sample of polystyrene has the following molecular weight distribution (from gel permeation chromatography):

| Molecular Size $M_i$ (g/mol) | Number of Molecules $N_i$ (×10¹⁸) |
|---|---|
| 10,000 | 2.0 |
| 50,000 | 5.0 |
| 100,000 | 3.0 |
| 200,000 | 1.0 |

Calculate $M_n$, $M_w$, PDI, and the number-average degree of polymerization (given $M_0 = 104$ g/mol for styrene).

**Step 1: Compute $\sum N_i$ and $\sum N_i M_i$**

$$\sum_i N_i = (2.0 + 5.0 + 3.0 + 1.0) \times 10^{18} = 11.0 \times 10^{18}$$

$$\sum_i N_i M_i = (2.0 \cdot 10{,}000) + (5.0 \cdot 50{,}000) + (3.0 \cdot 100{,}000) + (1.0 \cdot 200{,}000) \text{ (in units of } 10^{18} \text{)}$$

$$= (20{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 200{,}000) \times 10^{18} = 770{,}000 \times 10^{18}$$

**Step 2: Compute $M_n$**

$$M_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{770{,}000 \times 10^{18}}{11.0 \times 10^{18}} = 70{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sum N_i M_i^2$**

$$\sum_i N_i M_i^2 = 2.0 \cdot (10{,}000)^2 + 5.0 \cdot (50{,}000)^2 + 3.0 \cdot (100{,}000)^2 + 1.0 \cdot (200{,}000)^2$$

$$= 2.0 \cdot 10^8 + 5.0 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 10^9 + 3.0 \cdot 10^{10} + 1.0 \cdot 4 \cdot 10^{10}$$

$$= 2.0 \times 10^8 + 1.25 \times 10^{10} + 3.0 \times 10^{10} + 4.0 \times 10^{10}$$

$$= 8.47 \times 10^{10} \text{ (in units of } 10^{18} \text{)}$$

**Step 4: Compute $M_w$**

$$M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{8.47 \times 10^{10} \times 10^{18}}{770{,}000 \times 10^{18}} = \frac{8.47 \times 10^{10}}{7.7 \times 10^5} \approx 110{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5: Compute PDI**

$$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{110{,}000}{70{,}000} \approx 1.57$$

**Step 6: Compute Degree of Polymerization**

$$DP = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{70{,}000}{104} \approx 673$$

**Conclusion**: This commercial polystyrene has $M_n = 70{,}000$ g/mol, $M_w = 110{,}000$ g/mol, PDI = 1.57 (typical of free-radical polymerized polymers), and an average chain length of 673 monomer units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Polymer Classification Master Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart encapsulates the **complete KTU 2024 classification framework** for polymers, with engineering examples at each terminal node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POLYMERS]:::rootNode

    A --> B1[By Source]:::lvl1
    A --> B2[By Thermal Response]:::lvl1

    A --> B4[By Chain Structure]:::lvl1

    B1 --> C1A[Natural]:::lvl2
    B1 --> C1B[Semi-Synthetic]:::lvl2
    B1 --> C1C[Synthetic]:::lvl2

    C1A --> D1A[Cellulose]:::leaf
    C1A --> D1B[Natural Rubber]:::leaf
    C1A --> D1C[Proteins and Silk]:::leaf

    C1B --> D1D[Cellulose Acetate]:::leaf
    C1B --> D1E[Vulcanized Rubber]:::leaf

    C1C --> D1F[Polyethylene]:::leaf
    C1C --> D1G[Nylon 66]:::leaf
    C1C --> D1H[Epoxy Resin]:::leaf

    B2 --> C2B[Thermosetting]:::lvl2
```

### 4.2 Free-Radical Polymerization Process Flow

This block diagram illustrates the sequential kinetic events in addition polymerization, mapping how a single initiator molecule triggers the formation of thousands of polymer chains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 INITIATOR DECOMPOSITION]:::stage1
    P2[Benzoyl Peroxide BPO splits into two free radicals]:::stage1
    P3[Stage 2 INITIATION Radical attacks monomer C double bond]:::stage2
    P4[Stage 3 PROPAGATION Chain grows by adding monomers rapidly]:::stage3
    P5[Stage 4 TERMINATION Combination or Disproportionation]:::stage4
    P6[FINAL POLYMER CHAIN with degree of polymerization n]:::final

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6

    Q1[Note: Higher temperature increases kd and kt, producing shorter chains]:::annotation
    Q2[Note: Higher initiator concentration creates more chains but each is shorter]:::annotation

    Q1 -.-> P4
    Q2 -.-> P2

    classDef stage1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
    classDef stage2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#000000
    classDef stage3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000
    classDef stage4 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000000
    classDef annotation fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#475569,color:#000000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.3 Thermal Response Architecture: Processing & Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Polymer Microstructure]:::struct
    S1 --> T1[Linear Chains Weak Secondary Bonds]:::branch
    S1 --> T2[Heavy 3D Crosslinks Covalent]:::branch
    S1 --> T3[Light Crosslinks Elastic Network]:::branch

    T1 --> R1[THERMOPLASTIC]:::resp
    T1 --> B1[Behavior Softens on heating]:::behav
    T1 --> P1[Processing Injection Molding Extrusion]:::proc
    T1 --> E1[Examples PE PP PS PVC]:::ex

    T2 --> R2[THERMOSET]:::resp
    T2 --> B2[Behavior Degrades chars on heating]:::behav
    T2 --> P2[Processing Compression Molding Casting]:::proc
    T2 --> E2[Examples Epoxy Bakelite Phenolic]:::ex

    T3 --> R3[ELASTOMER]:::resp
    T3 --> B3[Behavior Large elastic strain up to 1000 percent]:::behav
    T3 --> P3[Processing Vulcanization Curing]:::proc
    T3 --> E3[Examples Natural Rubber Silicone Neoprene]:::ex
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define the terms: **(a)** monomer, **(b)** mer, **(c)** degree of polymerization. Illustrate with an example of polyethylene.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Monomer (1 mark)**: A monomer is the small, reactive molecule possessing one or more functional groups that can combine with other similar molecules to form a polymer. Example: Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) for polyethylene.
- **Mer (1 mark)**: A mer (or repeat unit) is the smallest structural unit whose repetition along the chain constitutes the polymer. For polyethylene, the mer is –[CH₂–CH₂]–.
- **Degree of Polymerization (1 mark)**: The degree of polymerization (DP or $n$) is the number of repeat units (mers) in a single polymer chain. For polyethylene with molecular weight 280,000 g/mol, $DP = 280{,}000 / 28 = 10{,}000$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
Distinguish between **thermoplastics** and **thermosetting plastics** based on (a) molecular structure, (b) behavior on heating, and (c) one engineering application of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **(a) Molecular Structure (1 mark)**: Thermoplastics consist of **linear or branched chains** held together by weak secondary bonds (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds). Thermosetting plastics possess a **heavily cross-linked, three-dimensional network** of covalent bonds.
- **(b) Behavior on Heating (1 mark)**: Thermoplastics **soften and melt** reversibly on heating, allowing remolding. Thermosetting plastics **do not melt**; on excessive heating, they **degrade and char** irreversibly.
- **(c) Engineering Application (1 mark)**: Thermoplastics — **PVC pipes for plumbing**. Thermosets — **Bakelite switches and electrical insulators** (or epoxy-matrix carbon-fiber composites in aircraft).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 1, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **free-radical addition polymerization** mechanism of polyethylene from ethylene monomer. Discuss the **initiation, propagation, and termination** steps in detail with chemical equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) – 7 Marks**:
- **Introduction (1 mark)**: Free-radical addition polymerization is a chain-growth mechanism in which monomers containing C=C double bonds (e.g., ethylene, CH₂=CH₂) sequentially add to an active chain end carrying an unpaired electron (free radical). The polymerization is initiated by thermal decomposition of an initiator such as benzoyl peroxide (BPO).
- **Initiation (2 marks)**:
  - Step 1 — Initiator decomposition: (C₆H₅COO)₂ → 2 C₆H₅COO• (rate constant $k_d$)
  - Step 2 — Radical attack on monomer: C₆H₅COO• + CH₂=CH₂ → C₆H₅COO–CH₂–CH₂• (rate constant $k_i$)
- **Propagation (2 marks)**: The chain radical sequentially adds ethylene molecules, propagating the active site:
  - R–CH₂–CH₂• + n CH₂=CH₂ → R–(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–CH₂–CH₂• (rate constant $k_p$)
  - Each addition moves the radical to the new chain end. The chain grows rapidly (10²–10³ monomers per second in industrial conditions).
- **Termination (2 marks)**: The active radical is destroyed by either:
  - **Combination**: Two chain radicals join: R–(CH₂–CH₂)ₘ• + •(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–R → R–(CH₂–CH₂)ₘ₊ₙ–R
  - **Disproportionation**: Hydrogen transfer from one chain to another, producing two dead polymer chains, one with a saturated end and one with an unsaturated end.
  - Both have rate constant $k_t$.

**(b)** A sample of poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) is found to have a number-average molecular weight of 62,500 g/mol. The mer weight of vinyl chloride (–CH₂–CHCl–) is 62.5 g/mol. Determine: **(i)** the **degree of polymerization**, **(ii)** the **number of mers in an average chain**, and **(iii)** the **approximate chain-end-to-chain-end length** (assuming a C–C bond length of **0.154 nm** and tetrahedral bond angle of **109.5°**). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b) – 7 Marks**:
- **(i) Degree of Polymerization (2 marks)**:
  $$DP = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{62{,}500}{62.5} = 1000$$
- **(ii) Number of mers in an average chain (1 mark)**: The number of mers equals the degree of polymerization = **1000 mers per chain**.
- **(iii) Approximate chain length (4 marks)**:
  - For a fully extended polymer chain with tetrahedral C–C bond angle ($\theta = 109.5°$), the projection of each C–C bond onto the chain axis is: $d = l \cos(\theta/2) = 0.154 \cdot \cos(54.75°) = 0.154 \cdot 0.5774 = 0.0889$ nm per bond.
  - For a polymer with $n = 1000$ mers, there are $2n = 2000$ C–C bonds in the backbone.
  - Therefore, the contour length $L$ is:
  $$L = 2n \cdot l \cos(\theta/2) = 2000 \cdot 0.0889 = 177.8 \text{ nm} \approx 0.178 \text{ μm}$$
  - Note: The factor of 2 arises because each mer (–CH₂–CHCl–) contributes 2 backbone C–C bonds.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 1, Alternative Path]**

**(a)** With suitable chemical equations, describe the **condensation polymerization** mechanism for the synthesis of **Nylon 6,6** from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid. Explain why the byproduct (water) must be continuously removed to achieve high molecular weight. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) – 7 Marks**:
- **Introduction (1 mark)**: Condensation (step-growth) polymerization is a reaction between bifunctional monomers in which each step releases a small molecule (here, water) as a byproduct. Nylon 6,6 is synthesized from **1,6-hexanediamine (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂)** and **adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH)**.
- **Chemical Equation (2 marks)**: The overall reaction is:
  $$n \, \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 + n \, \text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} \rightarrow$$
  $$\text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1) \, \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
  Each step involves an **amide linkage (–CO–NH–)** formation with elimination of water.
- **Step-by-step mechanism (2 marks)**:
  - Step 1: Protonation of –COOH by amine (forms –COO⁻ and –NH₃⁺).
  - Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of –NH₂ on the carbonyl carbon.
  - Step 3: Loss of water, formation of amide bond.
  - Step 4: Repeat with the next monomer.
- **Why water removal is essential (2 marks)**: The condensation reaction is **reversible** (an equilibrium reaction). As water accumulates, the reverse hydrolysis reaction (depolymerization) becomes significant. By Le Chatelier's principle, **continuous removal of water** shifts the equilibrium toward the polymer, allowing high conversions ($p > 0.99$) and thus high molecular weights ($X_n > 100$) to be achieved. Industrial Nylon 6,6 production uses **thin-film evaporators** or **inert-gas sparging** to remove water continuously.

**(b)** A polymer sample has the following molecular weight distribution obtained from GPC analysis. Compute $M_n$, $M_w$, and the polydispersity index (PDI).

| Molecular Size $M_i$ (g/mol) | Number of Molecules $N_i$ (×10¹⁶) |
|---|---|
| 5,000 | 4 |
| 25,000 | 6 |
| 75,000 | 4 |
| 150,000 | 2 |

Comment on the **uniformity of the polymer chain lengths** based on the PDI value. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b) – 7 Marks**:
- **Step 1 (1 mark)**: Compute $\sum N_i$ and $\sum N_i M_i$:
  $$\sum N_i = (4 + 6 + 4 + 2) \times 10^{16} = 16 \times 10^{16}$$
  $$\sum N_i M_i = (4 \cdot 5{,}000) + (6 \cdot 25{,}000) + (4 \cdot 75{,}000) + (2 \cdot 150{,}000) \text{ (in } 10^{16} \text{ units)}$$
  $$= (20{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 300{,}000) \times 10^{16} = 770{,}000 \times 10^{16}$$
- **Step 2 (1 mark)**: Compute $M_n$:
  $$M_n = \frac{770{,}000 \times 10^{16}}{16 \times 10^{16}} = 48{,}125 \text{ g/mol}$$
- **Step 3 (2 marks)**: Compute $\sum N_i M_i^2$:
  $$\sum N_i M_i^2 = 4(5{,}000)^2 + 6(25{,}000)^2 + 4(75{,}000)^2 + 2(150{,}000)^2$$
  $$= 4(2.5 \times 10^7) + 6(6.25 \times 10^8) + 4(5.625 \times 10^9) + 2(2.25 \times 10^{10})$$
  $$= 1.0 \times 10^8 + 3.75 \times 10^9 + 2.25 \times 10^{10} + 4.5 \times 10^{10}$$
  $$= 7.135 \times 10^{10} \text{ (in } 10^{16} \text{ units)}$$
- **Step 4 (1 mark)**: Compute $M_w$:
  $$M_w = \frac{7.135 \times 10^{10} \times 10^{16}}{770{,}000 \times 10^{16}} = \frac{7.135 \times 10^{10}}{7.7 \times 10^5} \approx 92{,}662 \text{ g/mol}$$
- **Step 5 (1 mark)**: Compute PDI:
  $$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{92{,}662}{48{,}125} \approx 1.93$$
- **Comment (1 mark)**: PDI ≈ 1.93 indicates a **moderately broad** molecular weight distribution, typical of conventional free-radical polymerized polymers. A perfectly monodisperse polymer would have PDI = 1.0; values close to 1 are obtained only with **living polymerizations** (anionic, RAFT, ATRP). The chain lengths are not uniform, and the material will exhibit a range of mechanical and rheological behaviors reflecting the molecular weight spread.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> **Most Common Mistakes in Part B Polymer Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $M_n$ and $M_w$ in the Carothers derivation**: Students often write $X_n = 1/(1-p^2)$ by incorrectly squaring the conversion term. The correct form is $X_n = 1/(1-p)$, and the imbalanced-stoichiometry form is $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2× bond factor in chain length calculation**: A polymer with $n$ mers has $2n$ backbone C–C bonds, NOT $n$. Examiners specifically test this with PVC and polyethylene problems.
> 3. **Mixing up addition and condensation polymerization**: Addition has **no byproduct**; condensation **always releases a small molecule** (H₂O, HCl, NH₃, methanol). Drawing an addition reaction that releases water is an immediate mark-loss.
> 4. **Neglecting units in numerical answers**: $M_n$ must be in g/mol; chain length in nm or μm; DP is dimensionless.
> 5. **Stating "polymer" without specifying the structural difference** between thermoplastic and thermoset — the examiner looks for the **cross-linking** explanation as the fundamental cause of property differences.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polymer = macromolecule** formed by joining many **monomers** through covalent bonds; the repeating unit is the **mer**.
- **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** = $M_n / M_0$ — a direct measure of chain length and an indirect measure of strength and viscosity.
- **Three classifications** to master: (1) Source (Natural / Semi-synthetic / Synthetic), (2) Thermal Response (Thermoplastic / Thermoset / Elastomer), (3) Polymerization Mechanism (Addition / Condensation).
- **Addition polymerization**: Chain-growth, no byproduct, requires initiator (BPO, AIBN), three stages: Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- **Condensation polymerization**: Step-growth, **releases a small molecule** (H₂O, HCl, NH₃), reversible equilibrium; continuous byproduct removal is essential to achieve high molecular weight.
- **Carothers Equation** (step-growth): $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ for stoichiometrically balanced reactions; the imbalanced form is $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- **$M_n$ vs $M_w$**: $M_n$ is the simple arithmetic mean (sensitive to small molecules); $M_w$ weights heavier molecules more heavily (sensitive to large molecules).
- **PDI = $M_w / M_n$** ≥ 1 always; PDI = 1 (monodisperse) is theoretical; commercial polymers have PDI between 1.5 and 10.
- **Thermoplastics** = linear/branched, weak secondary bonds, **recyclable**, melt reversibly.
- **Thermosets** = heavily cross-linked 3D covalent network, **non-recyclable**, degrade on heating.
- **Elastomers** = lightly cross-linked, **rubbery elastic behavior** (up to 1000% strain), recovers shape on stress release.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)**: Below $T_g$, polymer is rigid and glassy; above $T_g$, it is rubbery and flexible. Critical for service-temperature selection.
- **Engineering Polymers to Know**: PE (LDPE/HDPE), PP, PVC, PS, PMMA, PTFE, Nylon 6,6, PET, Epoxy, Bakelite, Natural Rubber.
- **PTFE** has the highest density (~2.2 g/cm³) among common polymers due to the heavy fluorine atoms.
- **Chain length formula**: $L = 2n \cdot l \cdot \cos(\theta/2)$, where $n$ is DP, $l$ = 0.154 nm for C–C, $\theta$ = 109.5° tetrahedral angle → $L = 2n \cdot 0.0889$ nm.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers: $M_n$ in g/mol, $T_g$ in °C, length in nm or μm, DP is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ceramics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ceramics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **Ceramics** are inorganic, non-metallic materials composed of metallic and non-metallic elements (such as oxides, nitrides, carbides, and silicates) held together by a combination of **ionic bonds** and **covalent bonds**. They are typically crystalline (or partly amorphous, as in glasses) and are characterized by high hardness, high melting points, brittleness, and excellent chemical and thermal stability.

The word *ceramic* originates from the Greek word **"keramos"**, meaning *potter's clay* or *burnt stuff*. Traditional ceramics (clay, silica, cement) have been used since the Stone Age, whereas **advanced (or engineering) ceramics** are synthetically processed (alumina, zirconia, silicon carbide, silicon nitride) for high-performance applications.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Ionic Brick Wall":**
> Imagine a **brick wall** in which each **brick (cation, +ve charge)** is held tightly to its neighbouring **brick (anion, –ve charge)** not by simple gravity or mechanical nailing, but by **strong magnetic attraction** across every face. If you try to slide one brick, the entire wall fractures — this is why ceramics are **hard but brittle**. The wall is fireproof (high melting point), cannot pass electricity (electrical insulator), and is chemically inert. That is essentially a ceramic microstructure.

### 1.3 The Two Pillars of Ceramic Bonding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Bonding Types in Ceramics:**
> 1. **Ionic Bonding** — Transfer of valence electrons; e.g., Na⁺ and Cl⁻ in NaCl. Non-directional, strong, gives high melting points.
> 2. **Covalent Bonding** — Sharing of valence electron pairs; e.g., Si–O in SiO₂. Highly directional, very strong, gives high hardness.

Most ceramics exhibit a **mixed (ionic + covalent)** character. The **electronegativity difference (ΔEN)** between the constituent atoms determines the dominant bond type:

$$
\Delta EN = \left( EN_{cation} - EN_{anion} \right)
$$

- **ΔEN > 1.7** → Predominantly **ionic**
- **ΔEN < 1.7** → Predominantly **covalent**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Common Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values for Common Engineering Ceramics:**
> - **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Density ≈ **3.95 g/cm³**, Melting point ≈ **2072 °C**, Knoop Hardness ≈ **2100 kgf/mm²**
> - **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** Density ≈ **3.21 g/cm³**, Melting point ≈ **2700 °C**, Knoop Hardness ≈ **2500 kgf/mm²**
> - **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** Density ≈ **5.68 g/cm³**, Melting point ≈ **2715 °C**
> - **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄):** Density ≈ **3.18 g/cm³**, Melting point ≈ **1900 °C**
