# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Frenkel and Schottky defects. In which type of crystal structures are these defects predominantly observed?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Frenkel Defect | Schottky Defect |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A cation leaves its lattice site and moves to an interstitial site, creating a vacancy-interstitial pair | A pair of cation and anion vacancies are created, with atoms moving to the surface |
| **Effect on Density** | **No change** (atoms remain inside) | **Decrease** (atoms leave the crystal) |
| **Ionic Charge** | Displaced ion preserves local charge balance | Maintains overall charge neutrality |
| **Common In** | Ionic crystals with **size disparity** between cation and anion (e.g., AgCl, ZnS, CaF₂, TiO₂) | Ionic crystals with **similar-sized ions** and high coordination (e.g., NaCl, KCl, MgO, LiF) |
| **Composition** | Stoichiometry preserved | Stoichiometry preserved |

**(1 mark each for definition, density effect, and examples.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Burgers vector? How is it used to distinguish between edge and screw dislocations?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$** is a vector that quantifies the **magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion** produced by a dislocation. It represents the slip displacement when a dislocation moves through the crystal and is a **conserved property** of the dislocation (independent of position along the line).

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for distinguishing):**

- **Edge Dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line direction $\vec{\xi}$. Mathematically, $\vec{b} \cdot \vec{\xi} = 0$.
- **Screw Dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to $\vec{\xi}$. Mathematically, $\vec{b} \times \vec{\xi} = 0$ (or $|\vec{b} \times \vec{\xi}| = 0$).
- **Mixed Dislocation**: $\vec{b}$ is at an arbitrary angle to $\vec{\xi}$.

The Burgers vector is found by drawing a **Burgers circuit** (a closed loop of atom-to-atom steps) around the dislocation in the defective crystal and comparing it to a perfect crystal. The closure failure vector is $\vec{b}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**"Q. (a) With neat diagrams, explain the different types of point defects in crystalline solids. (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the expression for the equilibrium concentration of vacancies in a crystalline solid and discuss how it varies with temperature. (7 marks)"**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Types of Point Defects (7 marks)**

The point defects can be classified as follows:

**1. Vacancy Defect (1 mark + diagram):**
A vacancy is produced when an atom is missing from a regular atomic site in the crystal lattice. The number of vacancies depends strongly on temperature (see part b). Vacancies are responsible for atomic diffusion in substitutional alloys and the ionic conductivity of solid electrolytes.

**2. Self-Interstitial Defect (1 mark):**
A host atom is displaced from its regular site to an interstitial position (a small space between regular atoms). Because the interstitial void is small compared to the atomic size, this defect produces a large lattice distortion. It has high formation energy (~4 eV for Cu) and is therefore not commonly found in equilibrium.

**3. Substitutional Defect (1 mark):**
A foreign atom (impurity) replaces a host atom on a regular lattice site. The impurity atom may be of a different size (causing local lattice expansion or contraction) or a different valence (altering electrical properties). This is the basis of **semiconductor doping** — adding P or As to Si for n-type, or B to Si for p-type.

**4. Interstitial Impurity (1 mark):**
A small foreign atom (much smaller than host) occupies the interstitial space. Classic examples: **C, N, O, H** in transition metals. The solubility of interstitial impurities is generally very low (e.g., max C in Fe ≈ 2.14 wt% in austenite).

**5. Frenkel Defect (1 mark):**
A host ion leaves its lattice site and moves to an adjacent interstitial site, leaving behind a vacancy. Net result: a (vacancy + interstitial) pair inside the crystal. Density unchanged. Common in ionic crystals like AgCl, ZnS.

**6. Schottky Defect (1 mark):**
A pair of cation and anion vacancies are created. To maintain stoichiometry, the missing pair diffuses to the surface. Crystal density **decreases** (the crystal contracts). Common in NaCl, KCl, MgO.

**(1 mark for schematic diagrams of any 3 defects — see diagrams in Section 4.)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Derivation of Vacancy Concentration (7 marks)**

**Given:** A crystal with $N$ atomic sites at temperature $T$ develops $N_v$ vacancies. Each vacancy requires an energy $Q_v$ to form.

**Step 1 — Configurational Entropy (2 marks):**
The number of ways of arranging $N_v$ vacancies among $N$ sites:

$$\Omega = \frac{N!}{N_v! (N - N_v)!}$$

By Boltzmann's relation, the configurational entropy is:

$$\Delta S = k_B \ln\Omega = k_B \ln\left[\frac{N!}{N_v! (N - N_v)!}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Free Energy Change (1 mark):**

$$\Delta G = N_v Q_v - T \Delta S = N_v Q_v - k_B T \ln\left[\frac{N!}{N_v! (N - N_v)!}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Stirling's Approximation (1 mark):**
For $N \gg 1$ and $N_v \gg 1$, using $\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$:

$$\Delta S = k_B \left[ N \ln N - N_v \ln N_v - (N - N_v) \ln (N - N_v) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Minimize Free Energy (1.5 marks):**
Equilibrium requires $\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dN_v} = 0$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dN_v} = Q_v - k_B T \ln\left[\frac{N - N_v}{N_v}\right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_v = k_B T \ln\left[\frac{N - N_v}{N_v}\right]$$

For $N_v \ll N$:

$$\boxed{\frac{N_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\frac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)}$$

**Discussion of Temperature Dependence (1 mark):**
- At **$T \to 0$ K**, $N_v \to 0$ (perfect crystal at absolute zero — 3rd law of thermodynamics).
- As $T$ increases, $N_v$ increases **exponentially**.
- Near the **melting point** $T_m$, $N_v/N \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- This explains why diffusion is rapid at high temperature (used in heat treatment, sintering, hot working).

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Q. (a) Explain the formation of edge and screw dislocations with the help of diagrams. Define the Burgers vector and explain the Burgers circuit. (7 marks)**
**(b) With neat sketches, describe the various types of surface defects in polycrystalline materials. How does grain size influence the yield strength? (7 marks)"**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Edge and Screw Dislocations (7 marks)**

**Edge Dislocation (2.5 marks + diagram):**
An edge dislocation can be visualized as an extra **half-plane of atoms** inserted into the crystal. Consider a perfect crystal that is cut partway through, and an additional plane of atoms is wedged into the cut. The boundary of this half-plane within the crystal is the **dislocation line**. Above the slip plane, the lattice is in **compression** (atoms squeezed together); below the slip plane, it is in **tension**.

When a shear stress is applied, the extra half-plane can move through the crystal like a propagating ripple, producing a permanent plastic deformation of one Burgers vector per pass.

**Screw Dislocation (2.5 marks + diagram):**
Imagine a crystal cut partway through and the two halves displaced **parallel** to the cut by one atomic spacing. The displacement creates a **spiral ramp** (like a parking ramp or spiral staircase) on the cut surface. The dislocation line runs along the axis of the spiral. As the spiral advances, plastic deformation of one Burgers vector occurs.

**Burgers Vector (1 mark):**
The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is a measure of the **magnitude and direction of the slip** caused by a dislocation. For an edge dislocation, $\vec{b}$ is perpendicular to the dislocation line. For a screw dislocation, $\vec{b}$ is parallel to the dislocation line.

**Burgers Circuit (1 mark):**
A Burgers circuit is drawn by tracing an **equal number of atom-to-atom steps** in a closed loop around the dislocation line in the defective crystal. The same steps in a perfect crystal would not close — the **closure failure vector is the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$**.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Surface Defects and Hall-Petch (7 marks)**

**Types of Surface Defects (4 marks + diagrams):**

1. **External Surface**: The outermost layer of atoms has dangling bonds, leading to **surface energy** $\gamma_s$. Surface atoms are in a different environment from bulk atoms.

2. **Grain Boundary (2D)**: The interface between two grains (crystallites) of different orientation in a polycrystal. A grain boundary is essentially a thin (~1–2 atomic layers) region of disorder. Two extreme cases:
   - **Low-angle tilt boundary**: Composed of a regular array of edge dislocations stacked vertically. Energy: $E \propto \theta (A - \ln \theta)$.
   - **High-angle boundary**: Disordered, energy $\approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ J/m².

3. **Twin Boundary**: A mirror-symmetric plane across which the lattice is the reflection of itself. Energy is much lower than a general grain boundary (≈ 0.05 J/m² for coherent twin in Cu). Common in FCC annealing twins and HCP deformation twins.

4. **Stacking Fault**: A localized error in the stacking sequence of close-packed planes (e.g., ABC**AC**ABC instead of ABCABCABC). Energy is the **stacking fault energy** $\gamma_{SFE}$ — low for brass, austenitic steel, copper; high for aluminum.

5. **Antiphase Boundary (APB)**: Occurs in ordered alloys (e.g., CuZn, Ni₃Al) where the regular pattern of two atom types is locally inverted across a plane.

**Effect of Grain Size on Yield Strength — Hall-Petch Equation (3 marks):**

The yield strength of a polycrystalline metal depends on the average grain diameter $d$ through the **Hall-Petch equation**:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (resistance of the lattice itself)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (material-dependent)
- $d$ = mean grain diameter

**Physical Basis**: Grain boundaries act as **barriers to dislocation motion** because a dislocation cannot easily pass from one grain (with one orientation) to another (with a different orientation). Dislocations pile up at the boundary, and the resulting stress concentration must be sufficient to activate slip in the adjacent grain.

**Engineering Significance**:
- **Finer grains → higher yield strength** (and higher hardness, higher fatigue strength).
- However, very fine grains may reduce **creep resistance** and **ductility at high temperature** (grain boundary sliding).
- Grain size strengthening is exploited in **HSLA steels, aluminum alloys, and microalloyed steels** through controlled rolling and thermomechanical processing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Frenkel and Schottky defects**: Many students write "both are pairs of defects." Correct answer: Frenkel = 1 vacancy + 1 interstitial; Schottky = 2 vacancies (cation + anion), with atoms migrating to surface.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention equilibrium**: Vacancies are the **only** point defects in thermodynamic equilibrium. Self-interstitials are **frozen-in**, not equilibrium defects.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Boltzmann relation**: $N_v = N \cdot \exp(-Q_v / k_B T)$, **not** $+Q_v$. Sign error gives nonsensical results.
> 4. **Burgers vector for edge vs screw**: Many students write "Burgers vector is parallel to dislocation line" — this is true ONLY for **screw** dislocations. For edge, it is perpendicular.
> 5. **Mixing up Hall-Petch constants**: $\sigma_0$ is in MPa (not dimensionless); $k_y$ has units of MPa·mm^(1/2) or MPa·μm^(1/2). Always state units in numerical problems.
> 6. **Diagram labels**: In exam, every diagram must have a **clear caption and labeled axes / crystal directions**. A diagram without labels gets 0 in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Density effect**: Schottky defects **decrease** crystal density; Frenkel defects **do not** change density. This is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Imperfections are the rule, not the exception.** A "perfect" crystal is a theoretical construct. All real engineering materials contain defects.

- **Classification by dimensionality** (most important for KTU short questions): 0-D point, 1-D line, 2-D surface, 3-D volume.

- **Vacancies are equilibrium defects** governed by $N_v/N = \exp(-Q_v/k_B T)$. They enable substitutional diffusion and atomic migration.

- **Interstitials** (self and impurity) are **non-equilibrium** (frozen-in from quenching, irradiation, or plastic deformation).

- **Frenkel vs Schottky**: Frenkel = cation moves to interstitial (density unchanged); Schottky = cation + anion leave to surface (density decreases). Frenkel is favored when ions are of very different sizes.

- **Dislocations = line defects** that govern plastic deformation. The two basic types are **edge** ($\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$) and **screw** ($\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$). Most real dislocations are **mixed**.

- **Burgers vector** is the slip displacement and is a **conserved property** of the dislocation.

- **Slip (glide)** occurs on the slip plane at any temperature; **climb** (perpendicular) requires vacancy diffusion and is only significant at **$T \geq 0.4\,T_m$**.

- **Schmid's Law**: $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ — determines the tensile stress required to activate slip in a single crystal.

- **Surface defects include**: external surface, grain boundary, twin boundary, stacking fault, antiphase boundary.

- **Hall-Petch equation**: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$ — finer grains give higher yield strength. This is why grain refinement is the **single most important strengthening mechanism** for structural metals.

- **Volume defects**: voids, inclusions, precipitates, cracks. Precipitates are the basis of **age hardening** (e.g., Al-Cu alloys, maraging steels).

- **Key numerical values to memorize**:
  - $k_B = 8.62 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
  - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
  - $Q_v$ for typical metals ≈ 0.5 to 2 eV
  - Burgers vector for FCC: $\frac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$; for BCC: $\frac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$
  - Vacancy concentration at $T_m \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$

- **Engineering applications of defects**:
  - Point defects → **Doping** of semiconductors, **Carburizing/Nitriding** of steel, **Ionic conductivity** in fuel cells, **Creep** in turbine blades.
  - Line defects → **Work hardening**, **Cold drawing** of wires, **Dislocation strengthening** in alloys.
  - Surface defects → **Grain boundary engineering** for superalloys, **Hall-Petch strengthening**, **Nanotwinned metals** for high-strength conductors.
  - Volume defects → **Precipitation hardening** of Al alloys, **Dispersion strengthening** (ODS alloys, SAP), **Crack propagation** studies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microscopic Techniques: Polishing and Etching Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microscopic Techniques: Polishing and Etching Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metallographic Polishing and Etching** is the sequential surface-preparation procedure used to produce a flat, mirror-like, deformation-free surface on a metallic (or ceramic/polymeric) specimen so that its true microstructure can be observed under an optical or electron microscope. The process broadly consists of:

1. **Sectioning** → **Mounting** → **Grinding** → **Polishing** → **Etching** → **Microscopic Examination**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Metallography is the scientific study of the microstructure of metals and alloys. Because the as-cut or as-fractured surface of a metal is rough, deformed, and optically non-reflective, a standardized sequence of grinding and polishing operations must be performed before the surface can be chemically attacked (etched) to reveal grains, grain boundaries, phases, inclusions, and other micro-constituents.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are asked to look at the *internal* structure of a piece of wood (its rings, knots, and grain). If you simply chop the wood with an axe and look at the broken face, you only see torn fibres, splinters, and dust — not the underlying pattern. To truly see the rings, you must:

- **Cut it cleanly** (Sectioning)
- **Sand it progressively** with rougher to finer sandpapers (Grinding)
- **Buff it with a soft cloth and polishing compound** until it shines like glass (Polishing)
- **Apply a light stain or weak acid** that darkens some rings more than others, *revealing* the structure (Etching)

Metallographic preparation is the **engineering cousin** of this carpentry workflow. The "rings" of a metal (called **grains**) and the borders between them (called **grain boundaries**) are invisible to the naked eye, but a properly polished and etched surface makes them stand out clearly under a microscope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** Almost every mechanical property of a metal — strength, ductility, toughness, fatigue life, hardenability — is governed by its microstructure. Without polished + etched specimens, a materials engineer cannot verify heat-treatment quality, detect casting/forging defects, or certify components for aerospace or nuclear service.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Grit Number (FEPA / ANSI):** Abrasive particle size designation; **higher grit = finer abrasive** (e.g., **P120 ≈ 125 µm**, **P600 ≈ 30 µm**, **P1200 ≈ 15 µm**).
- **Surface Roughness (Ra):** Typical polished surface **Ra ≤ 0.05 µm**; ground surface Ra ≈ 0.4 – 1.6 µm.
- **Etchant Residence Time:** Usually **2 – 30 seconds** for chemical etching of steels.
- **Standard Specimen Diameter:** **10 – 25 mm** (mount size ≈ 25 – 40 mm).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Check Point:** A 2-mark KTU question may simply ask *"Define metallographic etching."* The standard one-line answer is: *Etching is the controlled chemical (or electrochemical) attack of a polished metal surface using a suitable reagent to reveal microstructural features such as grain boundaries, phases, and inclusions under a microscope.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Logic

The metallographic preparation sequence is **cumulative**: each stage must remove the damage introduced by the previous one.

### Stage 1 — Sectioning
- Use a **cut-off wheel** (abrasive or precision diamond wheel) with copious **coolant** to minimise heat-affected zone (HAZ) and plastic deformation.
- Ideal cut depth of damage: **< 100 µm**.

### Stage 2 — Mounting
- **Hot mounting** (Bakelite, phenolic resin) at **150 – 200 °C** under **20 – 30 MPa** — standard for routine work.
- **Cold mounting** (epoxy, acrylic) — for heat-sensitive or brittle specimens (e.g., electronic components, ceramics).
- Purpose: provide a uniform geometry, protect edges, and enable automated handling.

### Stage 3 — Grinding
Abrasive papers (SiC) are used in a **strictly sequential grit progression**, typically:
- **P120 / P220** → P320 → P400 → P600 → P800 → P1000 → P1200
- Each step rotates the specimen by **90°** to the previous scratch direction.
- Coolant: water (for SiC) to flush debris and prevent thermal damage.

### Stage 4 — Polishing
- **Rough Polishing:** 6 µm and 1 µm diamond paste on a low-nap cloth.
- **Final Polishing:** 0.05 – 0.25 µm colloidal silica (or alumina) on a chemotextile or microcloth — produces the **mirror finish**.

### Stage 5 — Etching
- The polished, mirror-like surface is dipped or swabbed with an **etchant**.
- Grain boundaries (high-energy regions) and phases with different chemical potentials dissolve preferentially, creating **topographic contrast** and/or **optical contrast** that the microscope can detect.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Etching — Core Mechanistic Logic

| Reason | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Grain boundary attack** | GBs have higher free energy → dissolve faster → form a groove → light scatters at the groove → boundary appears **dark** |
| **Oriented grains** | Different grains have different crystallographic orientations → etch at different rates → reflect light at different angles → some appear **bright**, others **dark** |
| **Phase differentiation** | Different phases (ferrite vs pearlite) have different chemical potentials → differential dissolution → clear phase contrast |
| **Inclusion / void visibility** | Inclusions may not dissolve → remain in relief → visible as bright/dark spots |

## 2.3 Types of Etching Techniques

### (A) Chemical Etching (most common)
- **Immersion Etching:** Specimen fully dipped; typical time **5 – 30 s**.
- **Swab Etching:** Cotton swab applies etchant; gives better control.
- Reagents: acids, bases, or mixtures (see table 2.4).

### (B) Electrolytic (Electrolytic) Etching
- Specimen acts as **anode** in an electrolytic cell.
- Used for corrosion-resistant alloys (stainless steel, Ti, Nb) where chemical etching is sluggish.
- Parameters: **5 – 30 V DC**, **0.1 – 2 A/cm²**, time **1 – 60 s**.

### (C) Thermal Etching
- Specimen heated in **vacuum or inert atmosphere** to **0.5 – 0.7 × T_melting**.
- Surface atoms migrate → grooves form at GBs due to surface-energy minimisation.
- Used for ceramics, refractory metals, and single-crystal studies.

### (D) Ionic (Plasma / Cathodic) Etching
- Bombardment by inert-gas ions in a low-pressure plasma.
- Atomic-scale material removal → very fine surface contrast.
- Used in SEM preparation and for advanced ceramics.

### (E) Colour Etching
- Thin oxide / sulphide film forms on the surface → interference colours under white light.
- Used to distinguish carbides (e.g., **Klemm's tint etch** for ferrite/martensite).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Etchant Selection Table

| Material | Etchant Name | Composition | Reveals |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon / Low-alloy Steel | **Nital** | 2 – 5 % HNO₃ + Ethanol | Ferrite grain boundaries, pearlite |
| Carbon Steel (pearlitic detail) | **Picral** | 4 % Picric acid + Ethanol | Fine pearlite, carbide networks |
| Tool / High-speed Steel | **Kalling's No. 2** | 5 g CuCl₂ + 100 ml HCl + 100 ml Ethanol | Martensite, carbides, prior-A grain |
| Stainless Steel (austenitic) | **Glyceregia** | 3 HCl : 2 Glycerol : 1 HNO₃ | Grain boundaries, δ-ferrite |
| Stainless Steel (duplex) | **KOH Electrolytic** | 10 – 50 % KOH, 2 – 6 V DC | σ-phase, ferrite–austenite contrast |
| Cast Iron | **Nital + Picral mix** | 2 % Nital + 4 % Picral | Graphite morphology, matrix |
| Aluminium alloys | **Keller's reagent** | 2 ml HF + 3 ml HCl + 5 ml HNO₃ + 190 ml H₂O | Grain structure, precipitates |
| Copper / Brass | **NH₄OH + H₂O₂** | NH₄OH : H₂O₂ = 1 : 1 | Grain boundaries, twins |
| Titanium | **Kroll's reagent** | 2 ml HF + 6 ml HNO₃ + 92 ml H₂O | α/β phase contrast |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Grinding / Polishing Physics)

While the topic is largely procedural, a few quantitative relations appear in KTU questions:

$$R_a \approx \frac{d}{4}$$

where $R_a$ is the average surface roughness and $d$ is the abrasive grit diameter. This is used to estimate the minimum grit size required to achieve a mirror finish.

The material removal rate during grinding can be approximated as:

$$\dot{V} = k \cdot F \cdot v$$

where $\dot{V}$ = volumetric removal rate (mm³/s), $F$ = normal force (N), $v$ = relative velocity (mm/s), and $k$ = a system constant dependent on abrasive type.

Etching depth (Beale's relation, simplified) for a uniform surface:

$$h_{etch} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

where $M$ = molar mass of dissolved species, $I$ = current (A) for electrolytic etching, $t$ = time (s), $n$ = valence, $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96 485 C/mol**, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In quality-control labs of automotive and aerospace component manufacturers, every batch of heat-treated gears, shafts, and turbine blades is sampled and subjected to this exact sequence. Any deviation in etching time or etchant concentration can mask critical defects such as **untempered martensite, carbide networks, or hydrogen-induced cracking**, leading to catastrophic field failures.

## 2.6 Common Polishing Defects (KTU "What Went Wrong" Table)

| Defect | Likely Cause |
|---|---|
| **Scratches** (unidirectional) | Skipped a grit step, contaminated cloth |
| **Comet tails** | Coarse abrasive carried over to fine stage |
| **Pitting / pull-outs** | Hard brittle phase fractured during grinding |
| **Smearing** | Insufficient lubrication, soft material (Pb, Al) over-polished |
| **Orange peel** | Over-etching; chemical attack too aggressive |
| **Water staining** | Specimen not dried properly after final rinse |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because metallographic preparation is fundamentally a *process engineering* topic, the analytical depth required by KTU 2024 falls under (i) procedural logic, (ii) selection rationale, and (iii) quantitative correlations. The derivations below are **fully worked out** and exhaustively detailed — no step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating Abrasive Grit Size to Required Removal Depth

**Problem (KTU-style):** After sectioning a steel specimen with a cut-off wheel, the surface damage layer is measured to be **250 µm deep**. The laboratory's SiC grinding papers are stocked only in the standard FEPA grit sequence: P120, P220, P320, P400, P600, P800, P1000, P1200, P2500. *Each successive grit removes approximately one-half of the previous scratch depth.* Determine the **minimum number of grinding steps** required to reach a damage-free surface, and the **final expected surface roughness** if P1200 is the last step used.

### Step 1 — State the initial condition

Let the initial deformation depth be $D_0 = 250 \ \mu m$.

The FEPA standard grit diameters (approximate):
- P120 → $d_1 = 125 \ \mu m$
- P220 → $d_2 = 68 \ \mu m$
- P320 → $d_3 = 46 \ \mu m$
- P400 → $d_4 = 30 \ \mu m$
- P600 → $d_5 = 20 \ \mu m$
- P800 → $d_6 = 15 \ \mu m$
- P1000 → $d_7 = 10 \ \mu m$
- P1200 → $d_8 = 7 \ \mu m$

### Step 2 — Verify the damage-removal requirement

The P120 paper has a grit diameter of **125 µm**. By the empirical rule, each grinding step removes the previous damage layer plus its own deformation layer (which is roughly equal to its own grit diameter). Thus, **one pass of P120 removes a layer of thickness approximately equal to its grit diameter plus the previous layer's damage.**

We need the *cumulative* removed depth to exceed $D_0 = 250 \ \mu m$.

Cumulative removed depth after $n$ steps:

$$D_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$$

### Step 3 — Perform cumulative summation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 125 \ \mu m \\
D_2 &= 125 + 68 = 193 \ \mu m \\
D_3 &= 193 + 46 = 239 \ \mu m \\
D_4 &= 239 + 30 = 269 \ \mu m \quad (\text{exceeds } 250 \ \mu m)
\end{aligned}
$$

So, **4 grinding steps** (P120 → P220 → P320 → P400) are sufficient to remove the 250 µm damage layer.

### Step 4 — Compute the final surface roughness

The surface roughness $R_a$ is empirically related to the last abrasive grit size by:

$$R_a \approx \frac{d_{final}}{4}$$

For P1200 as the final grinding step:

$$R_a \approx \frac{7 \ \mu m}{4} = 1.75 \ \mu m$$

If we proceed to **final polishing with 0.25 µm diamond**, then:

$$R_a \approx \frac{0.25}{4} = 0.0625 \ \mu m \approx 0.05 \ \mu m$$

This satisfies the **mirror-finish** criterion for metallographic microscopy.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks the candidate to justify the *order* of grit progression. The correct answer must mention that the rule is: **each successive grit must remove the damage from the previous grit**, which is why the particle size should decrease by a factor of about **√2 (≈ 1.4×)** between consecutive papers — this is the basis of the standard FEPA sequence.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Etching Depth for an Electrolytic Etchant

**Problem:** A stainless-steel specimen is to be electrolytically etched in 10 % oxalic acid at a current density of **0.5 A/cm²** for **15 seconds**. The exposed area is **2 cm²**, and the metal dissolves as Fe²⁺ (atomic mass **55.85 g/mol**, density **7.87 g/cm³**, valence **n = 2**). Compute the depth of material removed.

### Step 1 — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

The mass dissolved is:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

- $M = 55.85 \ g/mol$
- $I = J \cdot A = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \ A$
- $t = 15 \ s$
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96\,485 \ C/mol$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{55.85 \times 1.0 \times 15}{2 \times 96\,485} \\
&= \frac{837.75}{192\,970} \\
&= 4.34 \times 10^{-3} \ g
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert mass removed to volumetric removal

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{4.34 \times 10^{-3}}{7.87} = 5.52 \times 10^{-4} \ cm^3$$

### Step 4 — Compute the etch depth (uniform dissolution)

$$h = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{5.52 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 2.76 \times 10^{-4} \ cm$$

Converting to micrometres:

$$h = 2.76 \ \mu m$$

This is the **optimum** depth — enough to develop grain-boundary grooves and phase contrast without obliterating fine microstructural detail.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selection of Etchant Using Dissolution-Rate Logic

**Problem (Essay Type):** A heat-treated medium-carbon steel (0.4 % C) is suspected to contain **untempered martensite**. Compare the suitability of **2 % Nital** and **4 % Picral** for revealing this microstructure.

**Solution Logic:**

1. **Martensite is a supersaturated, highly strained BCT phase** — it is more reactive than tempered martensite, ferrite, or pearlite.
2. **Nital (2 % HNO₃ in ethanol)** attacks ferrite grain boundaries readily but is **too aggressive on martensite** — it over-etches, darkening the entire structure and obscuring features.
3. **Picral (4 % Picric acid in ethanol)** attacks **carbide–ferrite interfaces** preferentially. It produces a **uniform grey tone** for martensite and clearly reveals carbide distribution and prior-austenite grain boundaries.
4. **Conclusion:** Picral is the **preferred etchant** for distinguishing tempered vs untempered martensite and for revealing prior-austenite grain size in hardened steels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing "more aggressive" with "better." A stronger etchant reveals *less* in a high-carbon or hardened steel because it dissolves the entire structure uniformly. Etchant selection is always a balance between *contrast* and *detail preservation*.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Etching Method

| Sample Condition | Recommended Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Routine low-carbon steel QC | **Chemical (Nital)** | Fast, inexpensive, well-known contrast |
| Corrosion-resistant alloy (stainless, Ti) | **Electrolytic** | Chemical etchant is sluggish due to passive film |
| Brittle ceramic, sintered carbide | **Thermal etching** | Chemical attack is negligible; surface diffusion exposes GBs |
| Failure analysis (small fracture region) | **Swab etching** | Local control, minimal over-etch |
| SEM imaging at high magnification | **Ionic etching** | No chemical residue; nanometre-scale contrast |
| Phase identification (multiple carbides) | **Colour etching (Klemm's, Beraha's)** | Tint-based discrimination of similar phases |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Procedural Algorithm for a Standard Carbon-Steel Specimen

1. Section a 15 mm coupon using a precision abrasive cut-off wheel; use water-soluble coolant.
2. Mount in phenolic resin at 180 °C, 25 MPa for 5 min.
3. Grind sequentially on SiC papers P120 → P220 → P320 → P400 → P600 → P800 → P1000, rotating the specimen 90° between each step.
4. Rinse with water; dry with hot-air blower.
5. Polish on 6 µm diamond paste (low-nap cloth) for 3 min at 200 rpm.
6. Polish on 1 µm diamond paste (medium-nap cloth) for 2 min.
7. Final polish on 0.05 µm colloidal silica for 1 min until mirror finish is achieved.
8. Rinse in ethanol; dry.
9. Etch by immersion in **2 % Nital for 5 – 10 s**.
10. Immediately rinse in running water, then ethanol, then dry.
11. Examine under optical microscope at 100× – 1000× magnification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequential Process Flow of Metallographic Preparation

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete operational workflow** of a typical metallographic specimen-preparation pipeline, including the iterative feedback loop when an inspection stage fails.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Raw Specimen / Coupon] --> sectA[Sectioning - Abrasive Cut-off Wheel]
    sectA --> mntA{Mounting Required?}
    mntA -- Yes --> hotA[Hot Mounting - Phenolic Resin 180C 25 MPa]
    mntA -- No --> grndA
    hotA --> grndA[Grinding Stage - SiC Papers P120 to P1000]
    grndA --> polA[Rough Polishing - 6um and 1um Diamond Paste]
    polA --> polB[Final Polishing - 0.05um Colloidal Silica]
    polB --> inspA{Surface Mirror Finish and Scratch Free?}
    inspA -- No --> grndA
    inspA -- Yes --> etchA[Etching - Chemical or Electrolytic]
    etchA --> etchB{Etch Contrast Adequate?}
    etchB -- No --> polA
    etchB -- Yes --> microA[Optical or Electron Microscopy]
    microA --> reportA[Microstructural Analysis and Report]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Etching Mechanism Map

The following diagram explains the **physical and chemical interaction** between the etchant and the polished metal surface, mapping how different microstructural features are *revealed*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subA[Polished Mirror Surface] --> gb1[Grain Boundary - High Energy]
    subA --> g1[Grain Interior - Low Index Plane]
    subA --> ph1[Second Phase Particle - Carbide]
    subA --> inc1[Inclusion - Oxide or Sulphide]
    gb1 --> d1[Dissolves Faster - Groove Forms - Appears Dark]
    g1 --> d2[Dissolves Slower - Stays Smooth - Reflects Light - Appears Bright]
    ph1 --> d3[Selective Attack - Phase Contrast]
    inc1 --> d4[Does Not Dissolve - Stays in Relief - Appears Bright or Coloured]
    d1 --> obs1[Optical Microscope - Topographic Contrast]
    d2 --> obs1
    d3 --> obs1
    d4 --> obs1
```

## 4.3 Etchant Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Material Type?}
    q1 -- Carbon or Low Alloy Steel --> q2{Heat Treatment State?}
    q2 -- Annealed Normalised --> eA[Use 2 percent Nital]
    q2 -- Quenched or Hardened --> eB[Use 4 percent Picral or Kalling No 2]
    q2 -- Spheroidised --> eC[Use Picral to reveal carbides]
    q1 -- Stainless Steel --> q3{Alloy Class?}
    q3 -- Austenitic --> eD[Use Glyceregia or Electrolytic 10 percent Oxalic Acid]
    q3 -- Duplex --> eE[Use Electrolytic KOH or Beraha Tint]
    q3 -- Martensitic --> eF[Use Vilella Reagent]
    q1 -- Aluminium Alloy --> eG[Use Kellers Reagent]
    q1 -- Copper or Brass --> eH[Use NH4OH plus H2O2]
    q1 -- Titanium --> eI[Use Krolls Reagent]
    q1 -- Ceramic --> eJ[Use Thermal Etching in Vacuum]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Polishing Station Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    block1[Specimen Holder - Pneumatic or Mechanical] --> block2[Rotating Disc - 200 to 600 rpm]
    block2 --> block3[Abrasive Layer - SiC Paper or Polishing Cloth]
    block3 --> block4[Abrasive Particles - Diamond Alumina or Silica]
    block4 --> block5[Specimen Surface - Controlled Contact]
    block5 --> block6[Coolant Supply - Water or Lubricant]
    block5 --> block7[Force Application - 5 to 30 N]
    block6 --> block8[Debris Flushing]
    block7 --> block9[Material Removal Layer]
    block8 --> block9
    block9 --> block10[Mirror Finish or Etched Surface]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define metallographic etching. Why is it necessary after polishing and before microscopic examination?**

**Model Answer:**
Metallographic etching is the controlled chemical or electrochemical attack of a finely polished metal surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) to reveal its microstructural features under a microscope. After polishing, the surface is a flat, featureless mirror in which grain boundaries, phases, and inclusions are invisible because all features are at the same level and reflect light uniformly. Etching selectively dissolves regions of higher chemical potential — typically **grain boundaries** (high-energy regions) and **second phases** — creating either topographic grooves or optical contrast. When viewed under the microscope, the etched regions scatter light and appear as dark lines, while un-attacked grain interiors remain smooth and reflect light to appear bright. Thus, etching is essential to convert an *optically featureless* mirror surface into a *microstructurally informative* image. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between chemical etching and electrolytic etching. In which situation is electrolytic etching preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Chemical Etching | Electrolytic Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Spontaneous chemical dissolution | Anodic dissolution driven by external DC source |
| Reagent | Acidic/alkaline solution (e.g., Nital) | Mild electrolyte (e.g., 10 % oxalic acid) |
| Control | Time, concentration, temperature | Voltage, current density, time |
| Equipment | Beaker + swab | Electrolytic cell with cathode and DC supply |
| Specimen as | Passive | Anode (positive terminal) |
| Application | Carbon steels, non-ferrous alloys | Stainless steels, Ti alloys, corrosion-resistant alloys |

**Electrolytic etching is preferred** for corrosion-resistant alloys (austenitic stainless steel, titanium, niobium) that form a **passive oxide film**, which resists spontaneous chemical attack. The applied anodic potential breaks down the passive layer locally and enables controlled dissolution. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for metallographic specimen preparation** of a medium-carbon steel specimen, starting from a raw as-cast block. For each step, justify *why* it is performed and state the typical process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of chemical etching**. With neat sketches (or descriptive explanation), show how grain boundaries, grains of different orientation, and second-phase particles become visible after etching. List **four common chemical etchants** used for carbon steels, stainless steels, aluminium alloys, and copper alloys respectively. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Sectioning (1 mark)**
- The as-cast block is cut using an **abrasive cut-off wheel** (e.g., Al₂O₃ or SiC bonded wheel).
- A water-soluble **coolant** is flooded over the cut to minimise thermal damage and HAZ.
- The cut surface should have damage depth **< 100 µm** and be flat to within ±0.5 mm.

**Step 2 — Mounting (1 mark)**
- The cut coupon is hot-mounted in **phenolic resin (Bakelite)** at **180 °C** and **25 MPa** for 5 – 7 minutes.
- This gives a cylindrical mount of standard diameter 25 – 40 mm, easy to handle in automatic polishers and ensures **edge preservation** (critical for coating / case-hardened samples).
- For heat-sensitive or porous samples, **cold mounting with epoxy** is used instead.

**Step 3 — Grinding (2 marks)**
- Sequential grinding on **SiC abrasive papers** in the grit order: **P120 → P220 → P320 → P400 → P600 → P800 → P1000**.
- Between each step, the specimen is **rotated 90°** so that the previous scratch pattern is uniformly removed before progressing to a finer grit.
- Water is used as the **coolant and lubricant** to flush away abrasive debris.
- **Justification:** Each step must remove the *plastic deformation layer* introduced by the previous coarser grit; skipping a step causes the final scratches to retain deeper damage and appear as artefacts under the microscope.

**Step 4 — Polishing (1.5 marks)**
- **Rough polishing** on a low-nap cloth with **6 µm diamond paste**, followed by **1 µm diamond paste**. Disc speed 200 – 300 rpm; applied force 10 – 20 N; duration 2 – 4 min.
- **Final polishing** on a microcloth with **0.05 µm colloidal silica** (or alumina suspension) for ~1 min to obtain a true **mirror finish** with Ra ≤ 0.05 µm.
- Diamond is preferred for hard materials (steel); alumina for soft materials (Al, Cu).

**Step 5 — Cleaning and Drying (0.5 mark)**
- Specimen is rinsed in **ethanol** (not water, to prevent rusting of carbon steel), then dried in a **hot-air stream**. Any residual slurry will cause spurious etching.

**Step 6 — Etching (0.5 mark)**
- Specimen is immersed in **2 % Nital** for **5 – 10 s**, then immediately rinsed in water → ethanol → dried.
- Over-etching is avoided; if contrast is insufficient, the surface can be **re-polished lightly and re-etched**.

**Step 7 — Microscopic Examination (0.5 mark)**
- Examined under an **optical microscope** at magnifications of **100×, 500×, 1000×**, or under SEM for higher resolutions.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Mechanism of Chemical Etching (3 marks)**

Chemical etching is a **preferential dissolution** process driven by the difference in **chemical potential (free energy)** between microstructural features. The general reaction is:

$$M_{(s)} + \text{acid}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow M^{n+}_{(aq)} + \text{reduction products} + H_2 \uparrow$$

The rate of attack is **not uniform**; it depends on:

1. **Grain-boundary energy:** GBs have higher free energy and a disordered atomic arrangement → **faster dissolution** → a **groove** forms at the boundary. Under vertical illumination, the groove side-walls scatter light into the objective, so the GB appears as a **dark line**.
2. **Crystallographic orientation:** Different grains have different low-index planes exposed at the surface. Etch rates vary because atomic packing density differs → some grains appear **bright** (smooth, reflect light) and others **dark** (rough, scatter light).
3. **Second-phase particles:** Phases such as **carbides** may be either **anodic** (dissolve, leaving a depression) or **cathodic** (do not dissolve, remain in relief) relative to the matrix → produces **phase contrast**.
4. **Inclusions:** Non-metallic inclusions (oxides, sulphides) are **insoluble** and stand out as bright or coloured spots in relief.

**Etchant List (4 marks)**

| Material | Etchant | Composition (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon steel | **Nital** | 2 – 5 % HNO₃ in ethanol |
| Stainless steel (austenitic) | **Glyceregia** | 3 HCl : 2 glycerol : 1 HNO₃ (by volume) |
| Aluminium alloys | **Keller's reagent** | 2 ml HF + 3 ml HCl + 5 ml HNO₃ + 190 ml H₂O |
| Copper / brass | **NH₄OH + H₂O₂ (1:1)** | Ammonium hydroxide + 30 % hydrogen peroxide |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the 90° rotation rule** between grit steps. Examiners expect this — losing 1 mark if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing "rough polish" with "final polish."** A student who says *"polish with diamond paste"* without specifying **6 µm → 1 µm → 0.05 µm** sequence gets only partial credit.
> 3. **Writing "acid" generically** without naming specific etchant concentrations (e.g., "2 % Nital" not "some acid").
> 4. **Forgetting the post-etch rinse** in water and ethanol. Without this, residual etchant continues to attack and ruins the microstructure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Module Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **need for mounting** in metallographic specimen preparation. Compare **hot mounting** and **cold mounting** techniques, listing at least **three advantages and two limitations** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **any four types of etching techniques** other than chemical etching, indicating the **principle, typical process parameters, and the class of materials** for which each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Need for Mounting (2 marks)**
Mounting is required to:
1. Provide a **uniform geometry** (usually a cylindrical disc 25 – 40 mm diameter) for handling in **automatic polishers** and for clamping in the microscope stage.
2. **Protect fragile edges** of specimens (e.g., thin sheets, wires, coated samples, case-hardened surfaces) that would otherwise round off during grinding.
3. Enable **multiple specimens to be mounted together** for batch processing, increasing lab throughput.
4. Allow **precise orientation** (e.g., transverse vs longitudinal) of the section plane.
5. Provide a **labelling surface** for sample identification.

**Hot Mounting (Conductive / Phenolic) (2.5 marks)**

*Principle:* Specimen is placed in a thermosetting powder (phenolic, Bakelite, diallyl phthalate) and compressed at **150 – 200 °C** and **20 – 30 MPa** in a hydraulic mounting press for 5 – 10 minutes.

*Advantages:*
1. **Fast** cycle (5 – 10 min per specimen).
2. Produces a **hard, durable mount** suitable for automated polishing.
3. **Edge retention is excellent** with proper edge-retentive fillers (e.g., mineral fillers).
4. Can be made **electrically conductive** (using graphite or copper-filled resin) for SEM or electrolytic etching.

*Limitations:*
1. **High temperature** (up to 200 °C) may alter the microstructure of **heat-treated steels or quenched specimens** if not cooled rapidly.
2. Not suitable for **porous, brittle, or friable** samples that may crack under mounting pressure.
3. Requires **specialised hydraulic press** equipment.

**Cold Mounting (Epoxy / Acrylic) (2.5 marks)**

*Principle:* Specimen is placed in a mould and a **room-temperature curing resin** (typically epoxy or methyl methacrylate) is poured over it and allowed to cure (5 – 60 min).

*Advantages:*
1. **No heat or pressure** — ideal for **heat-sensitive, porous, or brittle** materials.
2. **Low-viscosity epoxy** penetrates pores and cracks → excellent for **failure analysis**.
3. **Transparent resins** allow visual positioning of the specimen.
4. Simple, low-cost equipment; no press required.

*Limitations:*
1. **Longer curing time** (up to 24 h for some epoxies).
2. **Softer mount** — lower edge retention than hot mounts.
3. Epoxy is **electrically insulating** — conductive coating required for SEM.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Four Non-Chemical Etching Techniques (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)**

### (1) Electrolytic (Electrolytic) Etching
- **Principle:** The specimen is made the **anode** in an electrolytic cell containing a suitable electrolyte (e.g., 10 % oxalic acid, 10 – 50 % KOH). An applied **DC voltage (2 – 30 V)** drives anodic dissolution; the cathode is usually stainless steel or platinum.
- **Process Parameters:** Voltage 2 – 30 V; current density 0.1 – 2 A/cm²; time 1 – 60 s.
- **Materials:** Austenitic stainless steels, titanium alloys, nickel superalloys, niobium — all of which form passive oxide films and resist chemical etching.
- **Advantage:** Highly controllable; produces uniform contrast; minimal chemical hazard.

### (2) Thermal Etching
- **Principle:** The polished specimen is heated in a **vacuum or inert atmosphere** to **0.5 – 0.7 × T_melting** for 30 – 60 min. Atoms at grain boundaries migrate to lower surface energy positions, creating **thermal grooves** at the GBs.
- **Process Parameters:** Temperature 0.5 – 0.7 T_m; time 30 – 60 min; atmosphere — high vacuum (~10⁻⁴ Pa) or inert gas (Ar, He).
- **Materials:** Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂), refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta), single-crystal superalloys.
- **Advantage:** No corrosive chemicals; very high purity contrast for grain-boundary studies.

### (3) Ionic (Cathodic / Plasma) Etching
- **Principle:** A low-pressure inert-gas plasma (typically **Ar⁺ ions** at 1 – 10 keV) bombards the surface, sputtering away atoms in a controlled manner. Differential sputtering rates (dependent on atomic mass, crystal orientation, and phase composition) produce contrast.
- **Process Parameters:** Accelerating voltage 1 – 10 keV; ion current 1 – 50 mA; chamber pressure 1 – 10 Pa; time 5 – 60 min.
- **Materials:** SEM/TEM sample preparation, advanced ceramics, semiconductors, geological thin sections.
- **Advantage:** Nanometre-scale precision; no chemical residue; depth-profiling capability.

### (4) Colour (Tint) Etching
- **Principle:** A thin **oxide, sulphide, or molybdate film** (10 – 100 nm thick) is grown on the polished surface, either chemically or electrolytically. The film thickness varies from grain to grain, producing **interference colours** (Newton's rings) under white-light illumination. Different colours identify different phases or orientations.
- **Process Parameters:** Film growth time 5 – 60 s; tint etchants — Klemm's, Beraha's, Murakami's.
- **Materials:** Carbide identification in tool steels, ferrite–martensite–austenite discrimination in stainless steels, duplex stainless-steel phase quantification.
- **Advantage:** Allows discrimination of phases with **similar chemical reactivity** but different crystallography — impossible with conventional chemical etching.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing textbook-style paragraphs without sub-headings** — KTU examiners look for *structured* answers. Use tables and bullet points liberally.
> 2. **Skipping numerical parameters** — Always include **temperature, pressure, time, voltage, concentration** wherever applicable.
> 3. **Generic statements like *"electrolysis is done"* without specifying electrolyte, voltage, and current density** — this loses up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Failure to state the *purpose* of each technique** — KTU tests application reasoning, not just definition recall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Metallographic sequence (Mnemonic: "S-M-G-P-E-M")** → **S**ectioning → **M**ounting → **G**rinding → **P**olishing → **E**tching → **M**icroscopy.
- **Grinding rule:** Always progress from **coarse to fine** (P120 → P1200), **rotating 90°** between each step, with water as coolant. Each step must remove the damage of the previous one.
- **Polishing rule:** **Diamond paste** for hard materials; **alumina/colloidal silica** for soft materials; final polish must give **Ra ≤ 0.05 µm** (true mirror).
- **Etching rule:** Etchant must **selectively attack** higher-energy regions (GBs, certain phases) to create **topographic** *or* **optical contrast**.
- **Nital (2 – 5 % HNO₃ in ethanol)** is the **default etchant for carbon steel**; **Picral (4 % picric acid in ethanol)** for **fine pearlite and martensite** in hardened steels.
- **Keller's reagent** is the standard etchant for **aluminium alloys**.
- **Glyceregia** or **electrolytic 10 % oxalic acid** is used for **austenitic stainless steel**.
- **Kroll's reagent** is used for **titanium**; **Kalling's No. 2** for **tool steels**.
- **Electrolytic etching** is preferred for **corrosion-resistant alloys** with passive films.
- **Thermal etching** is used for **ceramics and refractory metals**; **ionic etching** for SEM and high-resolution work.
- **Common defects and their causes:** scratches (skipped grit), comet tails (carry-over), pitting (brittle phases), smearing (soft materials), orange peel (over-etch), water stains (poor drying).
- **Faraday's constant** $F = 96\,485 \ C/mol$ — used in electrolytic etch-depth calculations.
- **Final surface roughness rule:** $R_a \approx d_{grit}/4$ — for a mirror finish, $R_a \leq 0.05 \ \mu m$, which requires final abrasive $\leq 0.25 \ \mu m$.
- **Safety rule (often asked):** Nital is a **flammable, corrosive mixture** — must be used in a **fume hood** with **gloves and goggles**; HF-containing etchants (Keller's, Kroll's) require **special HF-resistant gloves** and a **calcium-gluconate first-aid kit** on hand.
- **Important KTU one-liner:** *"Etching converts an optically featureless mirror surface into a microstructurally informative image."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Microscopy: Transmission Electron Microscopy and Scanning Electron Microscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electron Microscopy: Transmission Electron Microscopy and Scanning Electron Microscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electron Microscopy — Formal Definition

**Electron Microscopy (EM)** is a high-resolution imaging technique that uses a beam of accelerated electrons as the illumination source to examine the microstructure, morphology, and crystallography of materials at the nanometer to atomic scale. Because electrons possess a wavelength thousands of times shorter than visible light, EM overcomes the diffraction-limited resolution barrier of conventional optical microscopy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module demands understanding of both **TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy)** and **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)**, their working principles, construction, and applications in materials characterization.

## 1.2 Resolution Limit of Optical Microscopy — The Motivation

According to the **Abbe diffraction limit**, the smallest resolvable distance $d$ between two points under a light microscope is governed by:

$$d = \frac{0.61 \,\lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident radiation
- $n$ = refractive index of the medium between lens and specimen
- $\alpha$ = half-angle of the maximum cone of light entering the lens
- $n \sin \alpha$ is also called the **Numerical Aperture (NA)**

For visible light ($\lambda \approx 400\text{–}700$ nm), even with the best immersion oil ($n \approx 1.5$), the maximum achievable resolution is around **200 nm**. To resolve finer microstructural details (grain boundaries, dislocations, precipitates), we need radiation of much shorter wavelength — and this is where **electrons** come in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Whispers in a Noisy Room":**
> Imagine you are trying to hear two people whispering in a very noisy room. If the "noise" (wavelength) is as large as the whispers themselves, the whispers drown out. To "hear" smaller whispers, you need a finer probe — electrons, behaving as waves with extremely short wavelengths, act as this finer probe, letting us "hear" atomic-scale whispers inside materials.

## 1.3 Why Electrons? — The de Broglie Revolution

Louis de Broglie (1924) proposed that every moving particle has an associated wavelength. For an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ (non-relativistic):

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

Where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant $= 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $m_e$ = electron rest mass $= 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e$ = electron charge $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

At a typical accelerating voltage of **100 kV**, the de Broglie wavelength of an electron is only about **0.0037 nm** — roughly **100,000 times shorter** than visible light. This is what gives EM its extraordinary resolving power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** A modern TEM operating at 200–300 kV routinely achieves a point-to-point resolution of approximately **0.05–0.2 nm** — sufficient to image individual atomic columns in crystalline materials.

## 1.4 Two Main Branches of Electron Microscopy

| Branch | Acronym | Image Formation Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission Electron Microscopy | **TEM** | Electrons *transmitted* through an ultra-thin specimen form a 2D projection image on a fluorescent screen |
| Scanning Electron Microscopy | **SEM** | A focused electron beam *scans* the specimen surface; secondary/backscattered electrons form the image |

Both techniques are essential in any materials/industrial engineering characterization laboratory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
In TEM, a high-energy, monochromatic electron beam is generated, focused by electromagnetic lenses, and passed through an ultra-thin specimen (typically **< 100 nm** thick). The transmitted electrons carry structural and crystallographic information about the specimen. These electrons are then magnified by a series of electromagnetic projector lenses and projected onto a fluorescent viewing screen or a CCD camera.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** TEM works like a giant "shadow projector." The specimen is like a stained-glass window; electrons pass through (or are blocked by) the dense regions, and the resulting "shadow" (contrast) is projected onto a screen — but at magnifications up to 50 million times!

### 2.1.2 Constructional Components of TEM (in order from top to bottom)

1. **Electron Source (Gun):** Produces electrons via thermionic emission (tungsten filament, LaB₆ crystal) or field emission (FEG). Operates under high vacuum ($\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ Torr).
2. **Condenser Lens System:** Demagnifies the electron source into a fine, coherent beam of required diameter (a few microns).
3. **Condenser Aperture:** Restricts the beam to remove stray electrons and control beam coherence.
4. **Specimen Stage:** A high-precision, multi-axis goniometer holding the ultra-thin specimen. Allows translation, tilt, and rotation.
5. **Objective Lens:** The most critical lens; forms the first intermediate image. The objective aperture controls contrast (bright-field vs dark-field imaging).
6. **Intermediate and Projector Lenses:** Further magnify the intermediate image and project it onto the viewing screen.
7. **Fluorescent Screen / CCD Camera:** Converts the electron image to a visible image.
8. **Vacuum System:** Multi-stage (mechanical, diffusion, ion pumps) to ensure electron mean free path is long.
9. **Apertures and Stigmators:** For beam alignment and correction of lens aberrations.

### 2.1.3 Image Modes in TEM
- **Bright-Field (BF) Image:** Formed by directly transmitted beam; dense/thick regions appear dark.
- **Dark-Field (DF) Image:** Formed using a diffracted beam; defect contrast is enhanced.
- **Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED):** Crystallographic information of a chosen region.
- **High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM):** Resolves atomic lattice fringes.

### 2.1.4 Specimen Preparation for TEM
- Specimens must be **< 100 nm thick** for electron transparency.
- Methods: **electropolishing, ion-beam milling, ultramicrotomy, focused ion beam (FIB) lift-out, replica techniques.**

### 2.1.5 Applications of TEM
- Imaging of **dislocations, stacking faults, grain boundaries, precipitates**.
- **Crystal structure** identification via electron diffraction.
- **Phase identification** and lattice parameter measurement.
- **Nano-particle size and shape** analysis.
- **Atomic-scale imaging** of interfaces in HRTEM.

## 2.2 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
In SEM, a finely focused electron beam (spot size $\sim 1\text{–}10$ nm) is **scanned** in a raster pattern across the specimen surface using electromagnetic scan coils. The interaction of this primary beam with the specimen generates several signals:
- **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low-energy ($< 50$ eV) electrons from surface atoms — used for **topographic contrast**.
- **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** High-energy electrons elastically reflected from deeper regions — used for **compositional (Z-contrast)**.
- **Characteristic X-rays:** Used for **Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS/EDX)** chemical analysis.
- **Auger Electrons** and **Cathodoluminescence.**

A detector (Everhart-Thornley for SE, solid-state for BSE) collects these signals and synchronously modulates the brightness of a CRT/LCD display scanned in raster lock-step with the beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** SEM is like "running your fingertip across a surface in the dark" — but instead of touch, a tiny electron beam scans the surface, and the "echoes" (secondary electrons) paint a 3D-like picture on a screen.

### 2.2.2 Constructional Components of SEM

1. **Electron Gun:** Thermionic (W, LaB₆) or field emission source. Accelerating voltage typically **0.5–30 kV**.
2. **Condenser Lens:** Demagnifies the source; sets beam current.
3. **Objective Lens:** Final focusing lens; produces a tiny spot on the specimen.
4. **Scan Coils:** Electromagnetic deflection coils that raster the beam across the specimen.
5. **Specimen Stage:** Multi-axis stage with translation, tilt, and rotation; larger specimens accepted than in TEM.
6. **Detectors:** Everhart-Thornley (SE), solid-state BSE detector, EDS detector.
7. **Vacuum System:** Prevents electron scattering and specimen contamination.
8. **Display & Imaging System:** Synchronized raster display.

### 2.2.3 Specimen Preparation for SEM
- Specimens must be **electrically conductive** (or coated with a thin layer of Au, Pt, C, or ITO).
- **Drying** of biological samples via critical point drying to preserve structure.
- **Mounting** on aluminum stubs using conductive carbon tape or silver paste.
- Specimen dimensions typically up to **~10 cm**.

### 2.2.4 Applications of SEM
- **Fracture surface analysis** (ductile vs brittle, fatigue striations).
- **Surface topography** of machined, polished, etched, or coated surfaces.
- **Microstructural examination** of metals, ceramics, polymers, composites.
- **Particle size and morphology** analysis.
- **Failure analysis** of engineering components.
- **Compositional analysis** (combined with EDS).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Significance / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Abbe Resolution Limit | $d = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}$ | Fundamental optical resolution limit; motivates EM |
| 2 | de Broglie Wavelength (non-relativistic) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}$ | Wavelength of accelerated electron; basis of EM resolution |
| 3 | Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2m_e c^2}\right)}}$ | Accurate wavelength above ~50 kV |
| 4 | Electron Kinetic Energy | $E = eV$ | Energy acquired by electron through accelerating voltage |
| 5 | Magnification (lens formula) | $M = \dfrac{L}{f}$ | Where $L$ is image distance, $f$ is focal length |
| 6 | Interaction Volume (Kanaya-Okayama) | $R_{BSE} \approx \dfrac{0.0276 A E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \rho}$ | Maximum depth of electron penetration in SEM (in µm) |
| 7 | Accelerating Voltage-Resolution Trade-off | Higher $V$ $\Rightarrow$ shorter $\lambda$ $\Rightarrow$ better resolution, but more beam damage | Key operational parameter in TEM/SEM |
| 8 | Magnification Range (SEM) | $M = \dfrac{\text{Display size}}{\text{Scan area on specimen}}$ | Typical SEM: 10× to 500,000× |
| 9 | Magnification Range (TEM) | Up to $\sim 5 \times 10^{7} \times$ | TEM achieves far higher magnification than SEM |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - In **aerospace industries**, TEM/SEM are used to verify the microstructure of turbine blades (single-crystal superalloys).
> - In **semiconductor fabrication**, SEM inspects photolithography patterns at sub-10 nm nodes.
> - In **failure analysis labs**, SEM fractography identifies the root cause of broken shafts, gears, and welds.
> - In **nanotechnology research**, HRTEM images carbon nanotubes and atomic columns in 2D materials like graphene and MoS₂.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution Limit of Optical Microscope (Abbe Criterion)

The diffraction pattern from a point source through a circular aperture is an **Airy disk**. Two points are just resolvable when the central maximum of one coincides with the first minimum of the other (**Rayleigh criterion**).

For a circular aperture of diameter $D$, the angular radius of the first Airy minimum is:

$$\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \,\lambda}{D}$$

The numerical aperture is defined as $NA = n \sin \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the half-angle subtended by the lens at the specimen. Combining these:

$$d = \frac{1.22 \,\lambda}{2 \, n \sin \alpha} = \frac{0.61 \,\lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$$

This is the **Abbe resolution limit** (also called the Rayleigh criterion), and it is the *theoretical ceiling* for any imaging system using photons of wavelength $\lambda$.

**Engineering interpretation:** Because $\lambda_{\text{visible}} \approx 550$ nm and $n\sin\alpha \leq 1.5$ even with oil immersion, the best possible $d$ is about $0.61 \times 550 / 1.5 \approx 224$ nm. To see finer features, we must reduce $\lambda$ — hence the use of accelerated electrons.

## 3.2 Derivation: de Broglie Wavelength of an Electron (Non-Relativistic)

An electron of mass $m_e$ and charge $e$, accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, gains kinetic energy $E = eV$.

Using the work-energy theorem:

$$E = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}}$$

The momentum is:

$$p = m_e v = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$

Substituting into the de Broglie relation $\lambda = h / p$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** (for $V = 100$ kV, non-relativistic — note: at this voltage, relativistic correction is already significant, but we use the simpler form first):

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 100{,}000}}$$

Step-by-step simplification:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-31-19+5}}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2.9189 \times 10^{-44}}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.7085 \times 10^{-22}}$$

$$\lambda \approx 3.88 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00388 \text{ nm}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Relativistic Correction for de Broglie Wavelength

For accelerating voltages above ~50 kV, the electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of the speed of light $c$, and relativistic mechanics must be used. The total energy is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_k + m_e c^2 = m_e c^2 + eV$$

The relativistic momentum is:

$$E_{\text{total}}^2 = (p c)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$$

$$p c = \sqrt{E_{\text{total}}^2 - (m_e c^2)^2} = \sqrt{(m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_e c^2)^2}$$

Expanding:

$$p c = \sqrt{2 m_e c^2 \cdot eV + (eV)^2}$$

$$p = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e eV\left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}{c}$$

Substituting into $\lambda = h / p$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV\left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** at $V = 200$ kV (a common high-resolution TEM voltage):

First, compute the relativistic correction factor:

$$\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 200{,}000}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}$$

$$= \frac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$= \frac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6396 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 0.1955$$

So $1 + eV/(2m_ec^2) \approx 1.1955$.

Non-relativistic $\lambda$ at 200 kV (ignoring correction): $\lambda_0 = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V} \approx 0.00274$ nm.

Relativistic $\lambda$:

$$\lambda_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1.1955}} = \frac{0.00274}{1.0934} \approx 0.00251 \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At 200 kV, the relativistic wavelength is about **2.5 pm**, which is shorter than the interatomic spacing in most solids. This is why high-voltage TEMs can resolve atomic columns directly.

## 3.4 Derivation: Magnification Equation in Electron Microscopy

For a thin electromagnetic lens, the lens equation (similar to optical lenses) holds:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$

Where $f$ is the focal length, $u$ is the object distance, and $v$ is the image distance. The **lateral magnification** is:

$$M = \frac{v}{u}$$

In practice, the **total magnification** of a TEM is the product of the magnifications of all lenses in series:

$$M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{objective}} \times M_{\text{intermediate}} \times M_{\text{projector}}$$

In a typical TEM:
- Objective: $M_1 \approx 20\text{–}100\times$
- Intermediate: $M_2 \approx 2\text{–}10\times$
- Projector: $M_3 \approx 100\text{–}1000\times$

$$M_{\text{total}} \approx 100 \times 10 \times 1000 = 10^6 \text{ to } 10^7$$

For SEM, the magnification is defined by the **ratio of the display screen size to the scan area** on the specimen:

$$M_{\text{SEM}} = \frac{\text{Display scan length (mm)}}{\text{Specimen scan length (mm)}}$$

For a 100 mm display rastering a 1 µm scan area:

$$M = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000 \times$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TEM vs SEM

| Parameter | TEM | SEM |
|---|---|---|
| **Imaging mode** | Transmission (2D projection) | Surface scanning (3D-like) |
| **Specimen thickness** | < 100 nm (ultra-thin) | Bulk (mm scale), but conductive |
| **Resolution** | 0.05–0.2 nm (atomic) | 1–10 nm |
| **Magnification** | 10³ to 10⁷ | 10 to 5 × 10⁵ |
| **Signals used** | Transmitted + diffracted electrons | SE, BSE, X-rays |
| **Specimen prep** | Difficult (ion milling, electropolish) | Easier (mount + coat) |
| **Information obtained** | Internal structure, crystal structure, defects | Surface topography, morphology |
| **Diffraction capability** | SAED, CBED (excellent) | EBSD (limited) |
| **EDS/EDX possible** | Yes (with STEM mode) | Yes (commonly) |
| **Vacuum level** | Ultra-high (10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁹ Torr) | High (10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Torr) |
| **Cost** | Very high | High (but less than TEM) |
| **Common applications** | Dislocation imaging, HRTEM, SAED | Fractography, failure analysis, surface study |

    G1 --> I1[Solid State BSE Detector]
