# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: What is a TTT diagram? Why is it called an isothermal transformation diagram?**

**Model Answer:**
A TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram is a plot of **temperature (°C)** on the Y-axis versus **logarithm of time (s)** on the X-axis, showing the **start and finish times** for the phase transformations of austenite into pearlite, bainite, and martensite in a steel. It is called an **isothermal transformation diagram** because it is constructed by **rapidly quenching** small steel specimens from the austenitizing temperature (above A₁) into a **molten salt/lead bath held at a constant (isothermal) temperature**, holding for various time intervals, and then examining the microstructure metallographically to determine when transformation begins and ends. The locus of these start and finish points at each temperature produces the characteristic C-shaped curves of the diagram.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Distinguish between TTT diagram and CCT diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TTT Diagram | CCT Diagram |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transformation mode | Isothermal hold at constant T | Continuous cooling at constant rate |
| Bainite region | Always shown | Often absent for plain-carbon steels |
| Nose position | Reference (e.g., 550 °C / 1 s) | Shifted to **lower T and longer time** |
| Practical use | Theoretical / austempering | Industrial heat treatment |
| Construction method | Salt-bath quenching + metallography | Programmed cooling + dilatometry |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Solve BOTH (a) and (b). Examiner will check both. Two alternative full-length questions (A and B) are provided — answer ANY ONE.

---

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat labelled sketch, explain the **TTT diagram for eutectoid steel (0.77 % C)**. List all the microstructures obtainable at different transformation temperatures and their approximate hardness range. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A specimen of eutectoid steel is austenitized at 800 °C, then rapidly quenched into a **salt bath maintained at 300 °C**, held for **1000 seconds**, and then **water quenched** to room temperature. Using the TTT diagram, determine:
  1. The final microstructure
  2. The approximate hardness
  3. The name of the heat-treatment process. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define and describe the construction (2 marks)**
The TTT diagram is constructed by austenitizing small specimens above A₁, quenching them rapidly into a salt bath at various constant sub-A₁ temperatures, holding for graded times, quenching to room temperature, and examining microstructures to determine transformation start and finish times. Plotting these on T vs. log-t axes produces C-shaped curves.

**Step 2 — Label the diagram components (3 marks)**

The diagram has:
- **Y-axis (linear):** Temperature in °C, with reference lines A₁ (727 °C), Mₛ (215 °C), M_f (50 °C)
- **X-axis (log scale):** Time in seconds
- **Pearlite C-curve:** Upper region, bounded by P-start and P-finish curves, with nose at ~ 550 °C / 1 s
- **Bainite C-curve:** Lower region, between ~ 550 °C and ~ 250 °C
- **Martensite region:** Below Mₛ = 215 °C, shown as **horizontal lines** (athermal)

**Step 3 — List microstructures and hardness (2 marks)**

| Temperature Range | Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 727 – 650 °C | Coarse Pearlite | 20 |
| 650 – 550 °C | Fine Pearlite | 30–40 |
| 550 – 350 °C | Upper Bainite (feathery) | 40–45 |
| 350 – 250 °C | Lower Bainite (acicular) | 50–55 |
| Below 215 °C (Mₛ) | Martensite (BCT) | 60–65 |

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Trace the cooling path on TTT diagram (1 mark)**
- Start: 800 °C (fully austenite, since 800 > A₁ = 727 °C)
- Quench to 300 °C (drops vertically into bainite region)
- Hold at 300 °C for 1000 s (horizontal line at 300 °C)
- Final water quench to 25 °C (vertical line from 300 °C to 25 °C)

**Step 2 — Identify phase at 300 °C after 1000 s hold (2 marks)**
- At 300 °C, bainite start time $B_s \approx 10$ s, bainite finish time $B_f \approx 300$ s
- Holding for 1000 s > 300 s → **bainite transformation is 100 % complete**
- 300 °C is below the 350 °C threshold → **lower bainite** forms

**Step 3 — Effect of subsequent water quench (2 marks)**
- Austenite is fully consumed during the isothermal hold → no parent austenite remains
- Therefore, **no martensite** forms during the water quench
- Microstructure is locked in as **100 % lower bainite**

**Step 4 — Hardness and process name (2 marks)**
- Lower bainite hardness: **~ 50–55 HRC**
- The process is called **AUSTEMPERING** — isothermal holding in the bainite region to obtain a bainitic structure with minimal distortion and good toughness.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\text{Microstructure: 100 \% Lower Bainite, Hardness } \approx 50 \text{–} 55 \text{ HRC, Process: AUSTEMPERING}}$$

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **CCT diagram for eutectoid steel**. Discuss the effect of different cooling rates on the final microstructure and hardness. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** For an eutectoid plain-carbon steel, the **nose of the TTT diagram** lies at **550 °C and 1 second**. If the steel is austenitized at 760 °C, calculate the **critical cooling rate** required to obtain **100 % martensite** on quenching to room temperature. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define CCT diagram (1 mark)**
A CCT (Continuous Cooling Transformation) diagram plots temperature (Y-axis) versus log time (X-axis) and shows the **transformation behaviour of austenite under continuous cooling** at various constant rates, rather than isothermal holding.

**Step 2 — Describe construction (1 mark)**
Specimens are austenitized and cooled at programmed rates (e.g., 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000 °C/s); the start and finish of transformation are detected by **dilatometry** or metallography. The C-curves obtained are **shifted to lower temperatures and longer times** compared to the TTT diagram.

**Step 3 — Sketch features (2 marks)**
- A₁ line at 727 °C
- Pearlite C-curve, nose at ~ 500 °C / 5–10 s (shifted from TTT)
- Mₛ = 215 °C, M_f = 50 °C (martensite horizontal lines)
- **Bainite region often absent** in plain-carbon steel CCT
- Superimposed cooling curves of different slopes

**Step 4 — Effect of cooling rate (3 marks)**

| Cooling Rate (°C/s) | Cooling Medium | Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| ~ 1000 | Ice brine | 100 % Martensite | 60–65 |
| ~ 100 | Water | Martensite + minor pearlite | 55–60 |
| ~ 50 | Oil | Pearlite + Martensite (mixed) | 40–50 |
| ~ 5 | Air (normalizing) | Fine pearlite + ferrite network | 25–30 |
| ~ 0.1 | Furnace (annealing) | Coarse pearlite / spheroidite | 15–20 |

Cooling curves that **bypass the nose** (very fast quench) yield 100 % martensite; those that **cross the nose** yield partial pearlite + martensite; very slow curves crossing the full pearlite band yield soft pearlite.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the principle (1 mark)**
To obtain 100 % martensite, the cooling curve must **miss the nose** of the TTT diagram. The minimum rate that achieves this is the **critical cooling rate (CCR)**, given by the slope of the line from A₁ tangentially grazing the nose to Mₛ.

**Step 2 — State the formula (1 mark)**

$$CCR_{min} = \frac{A_1 - T_{nose}}{t_{nose}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the given data (1 mark)**
- $A_1 = 760 \text{ °C}$ (austenitizing temperature, also approximately eutectoid temperature for 0.77 % C steel)
- $T_{nose} = 550 \text{ °C}$
- $t_{nose} = 1 \text{ s}$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute (2 marks)**

$$CCR_{min} = \frac{760 - 550}{1} = \frac{210}{1} = 210 \text{ °C/s}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and conclusion (2 marks)**
- The **critical cooling rate** = **210 °C/s**
- Quenching media capable of exceeding this rate:
  - **Water quench** ~ 200–500 °C/s → marginal
  - **Brine (10 % NaCl) quench** ~ 600–1000 °C/s → safe
  - **Oil quench** ~ 50–100 °C/s → insufficient
  - **Air cooling** ~ 5–10 °C/s → far below CCR
- Therefore, **brine or water quenching** is required to fully harden this eutectoid steel to 100 % martensite.

$$\boxed{CCR_{min} = 210 \text{ °C/s; Use brine or water quench}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the A₁ reference temperature:** Many students use 800 °C or 760 °C (austenitizing temperature) instead of the **eutectoid temperature 727 °C** as the upper bound. Always use the **highest temperature at which transformation can begin** (i.e., A₁ for eutectoid steel).
> 2. **Wrong units in CCR:** The denominator must be in **seconds**, not minutes. Convert carefully: 1 min = 60 s.
> 3. **Mixing up TTT and CCT noses:** The **TTT nose** is used to calculate the **critical cooling rate for hardening**, even when the actual cooling is continuous. The CCT nose is shifted further, making the *actual* required CCR slightly higher — for KTU numericals, use the **TTT nose** unless specifically given a CCT diagram.
> 4. **Confusing austempering with martempering:** Austempering = isothermal hold in **bainite region** → 100 % bainite. Martempering = hold just above Mₛ → 100 % martensite after final quench.
> 5. **Not labelling Mₛ and M_f:** Examiners specifically look for Mₛ and M_f values (215 °C, 50 °C) in any TTT/CCT sketch question. Failing to label them costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision before any KTU exam.**

### 🟢 Core Concept Bullets

- ☐ **TTT diagram** = Isothermal transformation; X-axis is **log(time)**, Y-axis is **Temperature (°C)**
- ☐ **CCT diagram** = Continuous cooling; curves are **shifted to lower T and longer time** compared to TTT
- ☐ **Eutectoid steel (0.77 % C)** is the reference composition: A₁ = **727 °C**, Mₛ = **215 °C**, M_f = **50 °C**
- ☐ **Nose of TTT** for eutectoid steel: **~ 550 °C at ~ 1 second** (lowest incubation time)
- �TTT curve has **three transformation regions**: **Pearlite** (top), **Bainite** (middle), **Martensite** (bottom)
- ☐ **Martensite transformation is athermal** — shown as **horizontal Mₛ / M_f lines**, not C-curves
- ☐ **Koistinen-Marburger equation:** $f_m = 1 - e^{-0.011(M_s - T_q)}$
- ☐ **Critical cooling rate formula:** $CCR_{min} = (A_1 - T_{nose}) / t_{nose}$ ≈ **177 °C/s** for eutectoid steel
- ☐ **CCT diagrams often omit bainite region** for plain-carbon steels; bainite appears in CCT only for alloy steels or very slow cooling

### 🟢 Microstructure → Hardness Quick Reference

- ☐ Coarse pearlite → **~ 20 HRC** (soft, machinable)
- ☐ Fine pearlite → **~ 30–40 HRC** (normalizing product)
- ☐ Upper bainite → **~ 40–45 HRC** (austempering at 350–550 °C)
- ☐ Lower bainite → **~ 50–55 HRC** (austempering at 250–350 °C)
- ☐ 100 % Martensite → **~ 60–65 HRC** (hardest, most brittle)

### 🟢 Industrial Heat-Treatment Processes Mapped to TTT/CCT

- ☐ **Full annealing** (furnace cool) → 100 % coarse pearlite
- ☐ **Normalizing** (air cool) → 100 % fine pearlite
- ☐ **Hardening** (water/brine quench) → 100 % martensite (requires CCR > critical)
- ☐ **Austempering** (salt bath 250–400 °C, hold > t_finish) → 100 % bainite
- ☐ **Martempering** (salt bath just above Mₛ, then air cool) → 100 % martensite with less distortion
- ☐ **Tempering** (reheat martensite to 150–650 °C) → tempered martensite (sorbite/troostite)

### 🟢 Common KTU-Exam "Instant-Answer" Triggers

- ☐ If a problem says "**held isothermally at T < A₁ for time t**" → use **TTT diagram**
- ☐ If a problem says "**cooled continuously from austenite to room temperature**" → use **CCT diagram**
- ☐ If the cooling curve **bypasses the nose** → 100 % martensite
- ☐ If the holding time is **less than t_start** → 100 % austenite retained
- ☐ If the holding time is **greater than t_finish** → 100 % product (pearlite or bainite)
- ☐ If the holding time is **between t_start and t_finish** → **lever rule** for phase fractions

### 🟢 Memorization Aids

- ☐ "**P**earlite is **P**earl-shaped at **H**igh T" — forms at high sub-A₁ temperatures
- ☐ "**B**ainite is **B**elow Pearlite" — forms below the nose
- ☐ "**M**artensite is **M**ilitary — quick and **M**andatory at low T" — athermal, immediate at Mₛ
- ☐ Mnemonic: **"A₁-Ms-Mf" = "Always Memorize seventy-two-seven, two-one-five, fifty"** = 727, 215, 50 (°C)
- ☐ CCR for eutectoid steel = **"177"** (one-seventy-seven) — remember as the year of the **Bain-Davenport discovery simplified**

### 🟢 Likely KTU 2024 Question Patterns to Expect

- ☐ "Sketch and label the TTT diagram for eutectoid steel" (10 marks)
- ☐ "A specimen of eutectoid steel is austenitized at X °C, quenched to Y °C, held for Z seconds, then quenched to room temperature. Identify the microstructure and hardness." (7–10 marks)
- ☐ "Calculate the critical cooling rate from TTT data" (7 marks)
- ☐ "Compare and contrast TTT and CCT diagrams" (7 marks)
- ☐ "Explain austempering and martempering with reference to TTT diagram" (7 marks)
- ☐ "What microstructure is obtained for cooling rate of X °C/s in a CCT diagram?" (3–7 marks)

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formation of Pearlite, Bainite, and Martensite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formation of Pearlite, Bainite, and Martensite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Preamble to Phase Transformations in Steel

In the Fe–Fe₃C (Iron–Iron Carbide) phase diagram, the **eutectoid reaction** at the eutectoid temperature of **727 °C** and eutectoid composition of **0.76 % C** is the cornerstone for understanding the three most industrially important microstructures of steel:

$$\gamma \text{ (Austenite, FCC)} \xrightarrow{\text{Cooling}} \alpha \text{ (Ferrite, BCC)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C (Cementite)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **CO2 / CO3 mapped** high-weightage area. Expect direct 7-mark sub-questions on formation mechanisms and 3-mark definitions of pearlite, bainite, and martensite in End Semester Examinations.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

### Pearlite
A **two-phase, lamellar (layered) eutectoid mixture** of alternating plates of **ferrite (α-Fe)** and **cementite (Fe₃C)**, formed by the **diffusion-controlled** decomposition of austenite at temperatures just below the eutectoid temperature (~650–727 °C).

### Bainite
A non-lamellar, **acicular (needle-like / feathery) mixture** of ferrite and carbide (cementite or ε-carbide), formed at **intermediate transformation temperatures (~250–550 °C)** where atomic diffusion is sluggish but not absent.

### Martensite
A **supersaturated solid solution of carbon in iron** with a **Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT)** crystal structure, formed by a **diffusionless (displacive/shear) transformation** when austenite is rapidly quenched below the **Martensite Start (Ms)** temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> The single sentence that distinguishes the three is: *Pearlite is diffusion-controlled and lamellar; Bainite is a hybrid with limited diffusion and acicular morphology; Martensite is diffusionless and shear-dominated.*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **baking a layered cake (pancake stack)** where hot sugar syrup (austenite) is poured onto a cold plate:

- **Slow cooling (long time, high temperature):** Sugar has plenty of time to crystallize into thick, distinct sugar layers alternating with a softer cream — this is **Pearlite** (thick lamellae).
- **Medium cooling (medium time, medium temperature):** Sugar only partially crystallizes, forming feather-shaped or needle-like crystals embedded in a softer matrix — this is **Bainite** (acicular crystals).
- **Sudden freezing (very fast cooling):** There is no time for sugar to migrate. The whole syrup is "frozen in place" into a distorted, hard, glassy solid — this is **Martensite** (supersaturated, distorted lattice).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for the exam hall:** Think **"P = Patience (slow/diffusion), B = Balance (mixed), M = Mayhem (rapid/shear)."**

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Plain Carbon Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Eutectoid temperature | $T_{eut}$ | **727 °C** |
| Eutectoid composition | $C_{eut}$ | **0.76 wt % C** |
| Martensite start temperature | $M_s$ | ~550 °C (depends on alloy) |
| Martensite finish temperature | $M_f$ | ~200 °C (depends on alloy) |
| Bainite start temperature | $B_s$ | ~550 °C |
| Upper bainite range | – | **350–550 °C** |
| Lower bainite range | – | **250–350 °C** |
| Maximum carbon in α-Fe (equilibrium) | – | **0.022 wt % C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation of Pearlite — Diffusion-Controlled Transformation

### 2.1.1 Mechanism (Nucleation and Growth)

Pearlite forms by a **cooperative eutectoid reaction** in which two solid phases (ferrite and cementite) grow simultaneously from a parent austenite grain. The process occurs in three sub-stages:

1. **Nucleation:** Carbon-enriched and carbon-depleted regions form in austenite at grain boundaries. A cementite (Fe₃C) nucleus precipitates first, which is energetically favourable because of its lower interfacial energy with austenite than ferrite.
2. **Sidewise (Cooperative) Growth:** Carbon atoms diffuse away from the cementite nucleus into the surrounding austenite, locally enriching it. The carbon-depleted austenite adjacent to the cementite transforms into ferrite (BCC). The cementite plate grows lengthwise; ferrite grows in tandem.
3. **Spheroidization of the Final Product:** Once fully transformed, the lamellar structure is metastable and tends to reduce interfacial energy by spheroidization of cementite on prolonged holding.

### 2.1.2 Interlamellar Spacing and the Zener-Hillert Relation

The **interlamellar spacing (λ)** of pearlite is a critical parameter that controls its mechanical properties. It is governed by the **Zener–Hillert relationship**:

$$\lambda = \frac{4 \sigma V_m T_e}{\Delta H_f \Delta T}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = interfacial energy of the ferrite–cementite boundary
- $V_m$ = molar volume
- $T_e$ = eutectoid temperature (727 °C)
- $\Delta H_f$ = enthalpy of transformation
- $\Delta T$ = undercooling below $T_e$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conclusion:** As **undercooling $\Delta T$ increases**, the **interlamellar spacing $\lambda$ decreases**. Finer pearlite is harder, stronger, and tougher than coarse pearlite.

### 2.1.3 Morphological Variants

| Variant | Formation Temperature | Interlamellar Spacing | Hardness (HV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coarse Pearlite** | ~700–727 °C | Large (~1 µm) | ~175 HV |
| **Fine Pearlite** | ~650–700 °C | Small (~0.1 µm) | ~250 HV |

---

## 2.2 Formation of Bainite — Intermediate Transformation

### 2.2.1 Mechanism (Displacive + Diffusion Hybrid)

Bainite forms at temperatures **between the nose of the TTT curve and the Ms temperature**. The transformation is *not* purely diffusional. It begins with the **displacive (shear) formation of sub-units of supersaturated ferrite**, followed by the **diffusion-controlled precipitation of carbides** from the carbon-enriched retained austenite.

The transformation sequence is:
1. **Sub-unit nucleation:** A plate-like subunit of ferrite forms by a shear/displacive mechanism (like martensite), but at a higher temperature so that excess carbon can diffuse out.
2. **Carbon partitioning:** Carbon is rejected from the ferrite sub-unit into the surrounding austenite, stabilizing it.
3. **Carbide precipitation:** Carbides (Fe₃C in upper bainite, ε-carbide in lower bainite) precipitate within or between the sub-units.

### 2.2.2 Morphological Variants

| Variant | Temperature Range | Morphology | Carbide Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Upper Bainite (Feathery)** | 350–550 °C | Bundles of parallel ferrite laths | Carbides between laths (inter-lath) |
| **Lower Bainite (Acicular)** | 250–350 °C | Plate-like ferrite with acute tips | Carbides inside laths at ~60° (intra-lath) |

> [!NOTE]
> Lower bainite is **harder and tougher** than upper bainite because of finer carbide dispersion.

---

## 2.3 Formation of Martensite — Diffusionless Shear Transformation

### 2.3.1 Mechanism

Martensite forms by a **diffusionless, displacive (military) transformation** when austenite is cooled rapidly (quenched) below the **Ms temperature** so that carbon atoms have *no time* to diffuse out of the FCC lattice. The parent FCC lattice undergoes a coordinated shear distortion to a **Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT)** lattice, trapping the carbon atoms in octahedral interstitial sites.

The lattice parameter relation is given by the **Bain distortion** (originally proposed by Edgar Bain):

$$\frac{c}{a} = 1 + 0.045 \cdot (\text{wt \% C})$$

where $a$ and $c$ are the lattice parameters of the BCT cell. For pure α-Fe, $c/a = 1$ (becomes BCC). For 1.0 wt % C martensite, $c/a \approx 1.045$.

### 2.3.2 Start and Finish Temperatures

- **Ms (Martensite Start):** Temperature at which martensite begins to form. Typical plain carbon steel: ~200–550 °C depending on carbon content.
- **Mf (Martensite Finish):** Temperature at which transformation is ~99 % complete.
- **M90:** Temperature at which 90 % martensite is formed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ms temperature decreases with increasing carbon and alloy content.** Above ~0.6 wt % C, **retained austenite** is unavoidable because Mf drops below room temperature.

### 2.3.3 Key Characteristics

- **Hardness:** Up to **65 HRC** (highest among the three).
- **Brittleness:** Very high (no ductility, low toughness).
- **Magnetic:** Yes (ferromagnetic).
- **Density:** Lower than austenite (volume expansion ~4 %).
- **Morphology:** Lath martensite (<0.6 % C) or Plate (acicular) martensite (>1.0 % C).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{4 \sigma V_m T_e}{\Delta H_f \Delta T}$ | Interlamellar spacing of pearlite | m |
| 2 | $c/a = 1 + 0.045 \cdot (\%C)$ | Tetragonality of martensite | dimensionless |
| 3 | $H_{pearlite} \approx H_0 + k_d / \lambda$ | Hall–Petch style hardness relation | HV |
| 4 | $V_{martensite} = 1 - e^{-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)}$ | Koistinen–Marburger equation (volume fraction formed at quench temp $T_q$) | – |
| 5 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Driving force for transformation | J/mol |
| 6 | $M_s (°C) \approx 550 - 350 \cdot (\%C) - 40 \cdot (\%Mn) - 20 \cdot (\%Cr) - 17 \cdot (\%Ni) - 10 \cdot (\%Mo)$ | Empirical Ms formula (Andrews) | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In the Andrews equation, students often add the alloying terms (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo) instead of *subtracting* them. All alloying elements **lower** the Ms temperature.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pearlite** forms the matrix of rails, high-strength wires, and prestressing strands (drawn pearlite).
- **Bainite** is the matrix of modern **high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels** and **carbide-free bainitic (CFB) rail steels** used in extreme wear conditions.
- **Martensite** is the as-quenched matrix of all **hardened tool steels, cutlery, gears, and bearings**, subsequently tempered to desired toughness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Zener–Hillert Interlamellar Spacing Relation

We shall derive the fundamental relation that ties the interlamellar spacing of pearlite to the degree of undercooling below the eutectoid temperature.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Free Energy Change

The total free energy change per unit volume of transforming pearlite is composed of two opposing contributions:
- A **negative bulk chemical free energy** that *drives* the transformation.
- A **positive interfacial energy** that *resists* the formation of new ferrite–cementite boundaries.

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{interface}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Chemical Free Energy Term

The chemical driving force per unit volume is proportional to the undercooling $\Delta T = T_e - T$ (where $T_e$ is the eutectoid temperature and $T$ is the actual transformation temperature):

$$\Delta G_{chem} = -\frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_e}$$

Here, $\Delta H_f$ is the latent heat of transformation (positive magnitude).

### Step 3 — Express the Interfacial Energy Term

If the interlamellar spacing is $\lambda$ and the ferrite–cementite interfacial energy per unit area is $\sigma$, then the interfacial energy per unit volume of pearlite is:

$$\Delta G_{interface} = \frac{2 \sigma}{\lambda}$$

The factor 2 accounts for the two interfaces (ferrite/cementite on each side) per lamellar pair.

### Step 4 — Write the Total Free Energy Change

Combining the two terms:

$$\Delta G_{total} = -\frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_e} + \frac{2 \sigma}{\lambda}$$

### Step 5 — Find the Minimum Spacing by Minimisation

For the transformation to be physically possible, the total free energy must be **negative** ($\Delta G_{total} < 0$). The **minimum (critical) spacing** is obtained by setting $\Delta G_{total} = 0$:

$$\frac{2 \sigma}{\lambda_{min}} = \frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_e}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\lambda_{min}$

Rearranging the equation to isolate $\lambda_{min}$:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{2 \sigma T_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}$$

This is the **minimum interlamellar spacing**. The actual spacing is generally 1.5–2 times this value. Including the volume factor $V_m$ and the conventional 4σ prefactor (to account for two interfaces and geometry), we obtain the engineering form:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{4 \sigma V_m T_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}}$$

### Step 7 — Physical Interpretation

- As $\Delta T$ **increases** (greater undercooling), $\lambda$ **decreases**, giving **finer, harder pearlite**.
- As $\sigma$ **decreases** (lower interfacial energy), $\lambda$ **increases**, leading to **coarser pearlite**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern)

> **Problem:** A eutectoid plain carbon steel is austenitised at 760 °C and isothermally transformed at 650 °C. Given $T_e = 727 °C$, $\sigma = 0.7 \, J/m^2$, $V_m = 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3/mol$, and $\Delta H_f = 3.0 \times 10^8 \, J/m^3$, calculate the interlamellar spacing.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the undercooling.

$$\Delta T = T_e - T = 727 - 650 = 77 \, °C \;(\text{or K})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Zener–Hillert equation.

$$\lambda = \frac{4 \times 0.7 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000}{3.0 \times 10^8 \times 77}$$

> (We convert $T_e$ to K: $T_e = 727 + 273 = 1000 \, K$.)

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$4 \times 0.7 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 0.0196$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$3.0 \times 10^8 \times 77 = 2.31 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the ratio.

$$\lambda = \frac{0.0196}{2.31 \times 10^{10}} = 8.48 \times 10^{-13} \, m$$

**Step 6:** Express in convenient engineering units.

$$\lambda \approx 0.85 \, nm$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This extremely fine spacing confirms that at 650 °C, the resulting pearlite is "fine pearlite" (also called sorbite/troostite in older texts).**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Koistinen–Marburger Equation

The volume fraction of martensite formed by quenching to a temperature $T_q$ below $M_s$ is:

$$f_{martensite} = 1 - e^{-\alpha (M_s - T_q)}$$

**Derivation outline:**

1. The driving force for martensitic transformation is proportional to $(M_s - T_q)$.
2. The nucleation rate of martensite plates is proportional to the driving force.
3. Integrating the nucleation rate with respect to time gives an exponential saturation, yielding the constant $\alpha \approx 0.011$ for plain carbon steels.

> This equation is **exam-relevant** for problems on retained austenite calculation in quench-hardened steels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Parameter | Pearlite | Bainite | Martensite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formation mechanism | **Diffusion-controlled** (nucleation & growth) | **Displacive + diffusion** (mixed) | **Diffusionless** (shear) |
| Temperature range | 550–727 °C | 250–550 °C | Below Ms (~200–550 °C) |
| Crystal structure of matrix | BCC (α-ferrite) | BCT ferrite | BCT martensite |
| Carbide form | Fe₃C (cementite) | Fe₃C (upper) or ε-Fe₂.₄C (lower) | None (as-formed) |
| Morphology | Lamellar (alternating plates) | Acicular / feathery | Lath or plate (lenticular) |
| Hardness | 175–250 HV | 250–400 HV | 600–900 HV (up to 65 HRC) |
| Cooling rate | Slow / moderate | Moderate | Very fast (quench) |
| Diffusion of C | Complete partitioning | Partial partitioning | **No diffusion** |
| Toughness | Moderate | High (esp. lower bainite) | Low (brittle) |
| Magnetic | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Density relative to γ | Slightly higher | Higher than γ | Lowest (largest volume expansion) |
| Industrial example | Rails, wire rods | HSLA plates, armour | Cutlery, gears, dies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) Diagram

The TTT diagram (also called the **isothermal transformation diagram** or **S-curve**) is the single most important schematic for this KTU topic. It maps the start and finish of the eutectoid transformation for a eutectoid steel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: 100% Austenite at 727 C] --> A1{Cooling Path}
    A1 -- Quench to 700 C --> B1[Region 1: 700-650 C]
    A1 -- Quench to 400 C --> B2[Region 2: 550-250 C]
    A1 -- Quench to 200 C --> B3[Region 3: Below Ms]
    B1 -- Hold --> C1[Coarse Pearlite forms first]
    C1 -- Continued hold --> C2[100% Coarse Pearlite]
    B1 -- Cool to 400 C --> C3[Fine Pearlite forms]
    B2 -- Hold --> D1[Upper Bainite forms at 450 C]
    B2 -- Hold at 300 C --> D2[Lower Bainite forms]
    B3 -- Quench --> E1[Martensite forms instantly]
    E1 --> E2[Retained Austenite if Mf not reached]
    C1 --- C2
    C2 --- C3
    D1 --- D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> Read the diagram left-to-right: as time progresses at a constant temperature, the transformation percentage increases from 0 % (start curve) to 100 % (finish curve).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — CCT (Continuous Cooling Transformation) Diagram

The CCT diagram differs from the TTT diagram in that it shows the transformation products for **continuous cooling** (which is the practical heat-treatment scenario).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Start: Austenite 760 C] --> F1{Apply Cooling}
    F1 -- Furnace Cool slow --> G1[Cooling line crosses 727 C region]
    G1 --> G2[Coarse Pearlite]
    F1 -- Air Cool moderate --> H1[Cooling line crosses 650-700 C]
    H1 --> H2[Fine Pearlite]
    F1 -- Oil Quench fast --> I1[Cooling line misses nose]
    I1 --> I2[Reaches Ms curve at 250 C]
    I2 --> I3[Martensite forms]
    F1 -- Water Quench very fast --> J1[Misses all curves]
    J1 --> J2[Pure Martensite plus Retained Austenite]
    G2 --- H2
    H2 --- I2
    I2 --- J2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** To obtain **100 % martensite**, the cooling curve must **miss the nose of the CCT diagram entirely**. If it touches the nose, some pearlite or bainite will form and you will not get a fully martensitic structure.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Block Architecture of Formation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0[Austenite gamma-FCC at 760 C] --> K1{Cooling Path}
    K1 -->|Slow T less than 727| K2[Diffusion Possible]
    K2 --> K3[Carbon partitions to cementite]
    K3 --> K4[Pearlite Lamellar]
    K1 -->|Medium T 250-550| K5[Partial Diffusion plus Shear]
    K5 --> K6[Sub-units of ferrite form]
    K6 --> K7[Carbide precipitates]
    K7 --> K8[Bainite Acicular]
    K1 -->|Fast T below Ms| K9[No Diffusion]
    K9 --> K10[Shear distortion FCC to BCT]
    K10 --> K11[Martensite BCT Lath or Plate]
    K4 --- K8
    K8 --- K11
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Comparative Microstructure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pearlite] --> P2[Alternating L1 Ferrite and L2 Cementite]
    P2 --> P3[Lamellar Spacing lambda 0.1 to 1.0 micrometer]
    B1[Bainite] --> B2[Sub-unit Laths of Ferrite]
    B2 --> B3[Carbide Particles Between or Within Laths]
    M1[Martensite] --> M2[Plate or Lath of BCT]
    M2 --> M3[High Dislocation Density plus Twins]
    P3 --- B3
    B3 --- M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> All four schematics above are **functionally equivalent** representations of the Fe–Fe₃C transformation products. They are preferred over physical drawings because of the difficulty in producing a **lattice-level illustration of BCT shear** in plain ASCII.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pearlite. Mention its formation temperature and morphology. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Pearlite is a **two-phase, lamellar eutectoid mixture of alternating ferrite (α-Fe) and cementite (Fe₃C)** formed by the **diffusion-controlled** decomposition of austenite at temperatures just below the **eutectoid temperature of 727 °C** (typically in the range 650–727 °C). It exhibits a characteristic **layered, plate-like morphology** with interlamellar spacing dependent on the undercooling. The interlamellar spacing is governed by the Zener–Hillert relation $\lambda = 4\sigma V_m T_e / (\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T)$, and finer spacing gives higher hardness.

---

### Question 2: What is retained austenite? Why is it undesirable? `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Supposed]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Retained austenite is the **untransformed parent FCC austenite** that remains at room temperature after quenching because the Mf temperature is below ambient. It is **undesirable** because: (i) it is **soft and unstable**, reducing hardness and wear resistance; (ii) it may transform to brittle martensite during service, causing **dimensional instability**; and (iii) it lowers the overall strength of the hardened component. It is mitigated by **sub-zero treatment** (cryogenic quenching) or by **tempering**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the formation mechanism of pearlite in a eutectoid plain carbon steel. Discuss the effect of undercooling on interlamellar spacing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Composition (2 marks):**
Pearlite is a two-phase lamellar aggregate of ferrite (α-Fe) and cementite (Fe₃C) formed by the eutectoid decomposition of austenite (γ-Fe) at 727 °C in a 0.76 % C steel:
$\gamma \, (0.76\,\%C) \rightarrow \alpha \, (0.022\,\%C) + Fe_3C \, (6.67\,\%C)$.

**Nucleation and Growth Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. Carbon-enriched and carbon-depleted zones form in austenite at grain boundaries.
2. A cementite nucleus forms first (favoured by low interfacial energy).
3. Adjacent austenite, depleted of carbon, transforms into ferrite.
4. Cooperative sidewise growth produces alternating lamellae of ferrite and cementite that grow as a "nodule" into the untransformed austenite.

**Interlamellar Spacing and Undercooling (2 marks):**
The interlamellar spacing is given by $\lambda = 4\sigma V_m T_e / (\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T)$. As undercooling $\Delta T$ increases, $\lambda$ decreases, producing **finer pearlite** with greater hardness.

**Schematic (refer Section 4.3 block diagram).**

---

**(b) Describe the formation, morphology, and properties of martensite in plain carbon steel. Explain the Koistinen–Marburger equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Formation (2 marks):**
Martensite forms when austenite is quenched (rapidly cooled) below the **Ms temperature**, so that carbon atoms have insufficient time to diffuse out. The transformation is **diffusionless and displacive (shear)**, converting FCC austenite to **Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT)** martensite.

**Morphology (2 marks):**
- **Lath martensite** (low carbon, < 0.6 % C): parallel laths within prior austenite grains.
- **Plate (lenticular) martensite** (high carbon, > 1.0 % C): lenticular plates with mid-rib, surrounded by retained austenite.

**Properties (1 mark):**
Hard, brittle, ferromagnetic, up to 65 HRC, low ductility, ~4 % volume expansion relative to austenite.

**Koistinen–Marburger Equation (2 marks):**
The volume fraction of martensite formed at a quench temperature $T_q$ is:
$f = 1 - e^{-\alpha (M_s - T_q)}$, where $\alpha \approx 0.011$ for plain carbon steel. This exponential relationship quantifies retained austenite.

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks, Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**(a) Compare the formation mechanisms of pearlite, bainite, and martensite with respect to (i) diffusion, (ii) temperature range, (iii) morphology, and (iv) hardness. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Pearlite | Bainite | Martensite |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Diffusion | **Full** diffusion of carbon | **Partial** diffusion | **No** diffusion |
| (ii) Temperature | 550–727 °C | 250–550 °C | Below Ms |
| (iii) Morphology | Lamellar | Acicular / feathery | Lath or plate |
| (iv) Hardness | 175–250 HV | 250–400 HV | 600–900 HV (up to 65 HRC) |

Pearlite forms by a **diffusion-controlled nucleation-and-growth** process; bainite by a **displacive sub-unit formation** followed by **carbide precipitation**; martensite by a **purely diffusionless shear** of the FCC lattice to a BCT structure. **(Allocate 1.75 marks per row of comparison.)**

---

**(b) Differentiate between upper and lower bainite. What is the effect of alloying elements on the Ms temperature? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Upper vs Lower Bainite (4 marks):**

| Feature | Upper Bainite | Lower Bainite |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 350–550 °C | 250–350 °C |
| Morphology | Feathery, parallel laths | Plate-like with acute tips |
| Carbide | Cementite **between** laths (inter-lath) | ε-carbide **within** laths at ~60° to lath axis (intra-lath) |
| Hardness | Lower | Higher |
| Toughness | Moderate | Higher |

**Effect of Alloying Elements on Ms (3 marks):**
All common alloying elements (C, Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, Si) **decrease** the Ms temperature. The empirical **Andrews equation** quantifies this:
$M_s (°C) \approx 550 - 350(\%C) - 40(\%Mn) - 20(\%Cr) - 17(\%Ni) - 10(\%Mo)$.
This is why alloy steels require lower quench temperatures and why **retained austenite** is more prevalent in highly alloyed tool steels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing the order of formation:** Pearlite → Bainite → Martensite as cooling rate increases. Students often write the reverse.
> 2. **Calling martensite "diffusion-controlled":** It is **diffusionless**. Loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Andrews equation sign:** Alloying elements **decrease** Ms, not increase.
> 4. **Mixing up upper and lower bainite:** Carbides are *inter-lath* in upper bainite and *intra-lath* in lower bainite. This is the single most tested distinction.
> 5. **Omitting the BCT structure of martensite:** This is non-negotiable. Always state the crystal structure.
> 6. **Drawing the TTT diagram incorrectly:** The start and finish curves must not cross; the bainite bay must lie *below* the pearlite nose and *above* the Ms line.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Pearlite forms by **diffusion-controlled** eutectoid reaction at **727 °C / 0.76 % C** in **lamellar** morphology.
- Bainite forms at **250–550 °C** in **acicular** morphology by a **mixed diffusion + shear** mechanism.
- Martensite forms **below Ms** by **diffusionless shear**, giving a **BCT** structure with very high hardness.
- **Tetragonality** of martensite: $c/a = 1 + 0.045 \cdot (\%C)$.
- **Interlamellar spacing** of pearlite: $\lambda = 4\sigma V_m T_e / (\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T)$ — decreases with undercooling.
- **Coarse pearlite** → high T; **Fine pearlite** → low T; **Sorbite/Troostite** → very fine pearlite formed at high undercooling.
- **Upper bainite** → feathery, inter-lath carbides; **Lower bainite** → plate-like, intra-lath carbides.
- **Koistinen–Marburger equation**: $f = 1 - e^{-\alpha(M_s - T_q)}$ gives the volume fraction of martensite.
- **Andrews equation** gives the empirical Ms temperature; **all alloying elements lower Ms**.
- **Retained austenite** arises when Mf < room temperature; common in high-carbon and alloy steels.
- **TTT diagram** is for **isothermal** (constant T) holding; **CCT diagram** is for **continuous cooling** (engineering reality).
- For **100 % martensite**, the cooling curve must **miss the nose of the CCT diagram**.
- Pearlite → Bainite → Martensite corresponds to **increasing cooling rate** and **decreasing transformation temperature**.
- All three are **magnetic** (ferromagnetic) and are **supersaturated or metastable** relative to equilibrium ferrite + cementite.
- Tempering of martensite produces **tempered martensite** with a fine dispersion of ε-carbide or cementite in ferrite — a topic for Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic for the Exam Hall:**
> *"**P**earlite = **P**late, **P**atient, **P**earl-like"*
> *"**B**ainite = **B**ird-feather, **B**etween"*
> *"**M**artensite = **M**ilitary, **M**artens finish = room temp, **M**aximum hardness"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Treatment: Basic Principles of Annealing, Normalizing, Hardening, Spheroidizing, and Tempering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Treatment: Basic Principles of Annealing, Normalizing, Hardening, Spheroidizing, and Tempering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Heat Treatment?

**Heat Treatment** is a controlled industrial process involving the precise heating and cooling of metals (primarily steels and alloys) in the solid state to deliberately alter their **microstructure** and thereby obtain specific **mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** without changing the product's shape or overall chemical composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Heat treatment is defined as a combination of heating and cooling operations applied to a metal or alloy in its solid state to obtain desired mechanical properties through controlled changes in microstructure.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a loaf of bread. You don't change the *ingredients* (flour, water, yeast), but by *controlling* the oven temperature, the rising time, and how fast you let it cool, you get a soft cake, a crusty baguette, or a hard cracker — all from the *same dough*.

Heat treatment is the "metallurgical kitchen." The iron-carbon alloy is the dough; the furnace is the oven; and the cooling rate (water, oil, or air) is the technique that turns the *same* steel into a soft, ductile sheet, a tough gear, or a razor-sharp blade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** Phase transformations in iron (allotropic changes) are the foundation of all heat treatment processes. Iron exists in different crystalline forms — BCC (α-Fe) and FCC (γ-Fe, Austenite) — depending on temperature.

---

## 1.2 The Five Major Heat Treatment Processes — Overview

| # | Process | Purpose | Cooling Medium |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Annealing** | Softening, relieve internal stresses, improve ductility | Very slow (furnace cooling) |
| 2 | **Normalizing** | Refine grain structure, improve toughness | Moderate (air cooling) |
| 3 | **Hardening** | Increase hardness and strength | Rapid (water/oil/brine) |
| 4 | **Spheroidizing** | Maximize softness and machinability | Very slow prolonged heating |
| 5 | **Tempering** | Reduce brittleness after hardening, increase toughness | Reheating below eutectoid temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All five processes are based on the **Iron-Carbon (Fe-Fe₃C) Phase Diagram**, the **TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) Diagram**, and the **CCT (Continuous Cooling Transformation) Diagram**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Eutectoid Temperature (A₁):** **727 °C** (1004 °F) — the temperature below which Austenite is unstable.
- **Upper Critical Temperature (A₃):** Varies with carbon content (e.g., 910 °C for pure iron, ~727 °C at 0.8% C).
- **Eutectoid Composition:** **0.8% Carbon** (the "magic" carbon content).
- **Martensite Start (Mₛ) Temperature:** ~215 °C for eutectoid steel.
- **Martensite Finish (M_f) Temperature:** ~ -50 °C for eutectoid steel (requires sub-zero treatment for full conversion).
- **Standard hardness scale used:** **Rockwell C (HRC)** and **Vickers (HV)** for hard steels.

---

## 1.4 The Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram — Foundation Reference

```
Temperature (°C)
   ↑
   |  1538°C (Melting of pure Fe)
   |        \
   |         \  δ-Fe (BCC)
   |    1394°C ----A₄----
   |         \           \
   |          \    γ-Fe (FCC, Austenite)  
   |    910°C  \A₃        \
   |     -------+-----------A₁ (727°C)---
   |            |  α-Fe (BCC, Ferrite)  |  Pearlite (α + Fe₃C)
   |            |                       |
   |____________|_______________________|____ %C
   0          0.022        0.8         6.67
           Ferrite      Eutectoid   Cementite
```

The three critical horizontal lines governing all heat treatment are:
1. **A₁ (727 °C)** — Lower critical / Eutectoid line
2. **A₃** — Upper critical (γ → α transformation start)
3. **A_cm** — Upper critical (Cementite solubility limit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Heat Treatment Cycle

Every heat treatment operation fundamentally consists of **three controlled stages**:

### Stage 1: Heating (Austenitization)
- Heat the steel to a temperature above its critical range (A₃ or A₁).
- The existing microstructure (Ferrite + Pearlite / Bainite / Martensite) **transforms completely into Austenite (γ-Fe, FCC)**.
- Austenite is **soft, ductile, and non-magnetic**, making it ideal for further processing.

### Stage 2: Soaking (Holding)
- Maintain the temperature for a specific duration to allow:
  - **Homogenization** of carbon atoms throughout the FCC lattice
  - **Complete dissolution** of cementite (Fe₃C) particles
  - **Grain growth** to desired size (controlled by time)

> [!NOTE]
> **Soaking time rule of thumb:** Approximately **1 hour per inch of cross-section** (or 25 mm) of the largest section thickness, plus 1 hour for the first inch.

### Stage 3: Cooling (Transformation)
- Cool at a *controlled* rate to obtain the desired microstructure.
- The cooling medium (water, oil, air, furnace) determines the final phase:
  - **Slow cooling** → Ferrite + Pearlite (soft)
  - **Moderate cooling** → Fine Pearlite / Bainite (tough)
  - **Rapid cooling (quenching)** → Martensite (hard, brittle)

---

## 2.2 Process 1: ANNEALING — Detailed Analysis

### Definition
Annealing is a heat treatment process that involves **heating the steel above its critical temperature (A₃ or A₁)**, holding (soaking) at that temperature, and then **cooling it very slowly in the furnace itself** to obtain a soft, ductile, and stress-free structure.

### Purpose / Objectives
1. **Relieve internal stresses** induced by previous operations (welding, machining, cold working)
2. **Softening** the steel for easy machining and forming
3. **Refine the grain structure** (full annealing)
4. **Improve electrical and magnetic properties**
5. **Increase ductility and toughness**

### Types of Annealing

| Type | Temperature Range | Purpose |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Full Annealing** | 30–50 °C above A₃ (Hypoeutectoid) | Maximum softness, coarse pearlite |
| **Process Annealing** | Below A₁ (~550–650 °C) | Recrystallization of cold-worked steel |
| **Stress-Relief Annealing** | 550–650 °C | Remove residual stresses only |
| **Spheroidize Annealing** | Just below A₁ (prolonged) | Cementite → spherical form |

### The "Why" — Metallurgical Logic
When austenite is cooled **extremely slowly**, carbon atoms have ample time to **diffuse out of the FCC lattice** and form a coarse lamellar structure of **Ferrite (α-Fe) and Cementite (Fe₃C)** called **Pearlite**. This coarse pearlite is the softest, most ductile transformation product.

---

## 2.3 Process 2: NORMALIZING — Detailed Analysis

### Definition
Normalizing is a heat treatment process in which the steel is heated to a temperature **30–50 °C above the upper critical temperature (A₃ or A_cm)**, held for sufficient time, and then **cooled in still air** to room temperature.

### Cooling Rate Comparison
$$ T_{furnace} < T_{air} < T_{oil} < T_{brine/water} $$

Normalized cooling is **faster than annealed** cooling but **slower than quenched** cooling.

### Why Normalize?
- Produces a **finer pearlite** than annealing (because faster cooling refines the lamellae).
- Results in **higher hardness, strength, and toughness** than annealed steel.
- **Homogenizes** the grain structure across the cross-section.
- **Improves machinability** of medium-carbon steels.
- Used as a **preliminary treatment** before hardening.

### Pearlite Spacing Relationship
$$ S_{pearlite} \propto \frac{1}{R_{cooling}} $$

where $S$ is interlamellar spacing and $R$ is cooling rate. **Faster cooling → finer pearlite → harder steel.**

---

## 2.4 Process 3: HARDENING (Quenching) — Detailed Analysis

### Definition
Hardening is a heat treatment process in which the steel is heated above the critical temperature (A₃ or A₁) to form austenite, and then **rapidly cooled (quenched)** in a suitable medium to obtain **Martensite**, a hard and brittle phase.

### The Martensitic Transformation
Unlike diffusion-controlled transformations (Pearlite, Bainite), martensite forms by a **diffusionless shear (military) transformation** where carbon atoms are *trapped* inside the BCC lattice, **distorting it into a body-centered tetragonal (BCT)** structure.

$$ \gamma \text{ (FCC, Austenite)} \xrightarrow{\text{rapid cooling (no C diffusion)}} \alpha' \text{ (BCT, Martensite)} $$

### Quenching Media — Severity of Quench (Grossmann's H-value)
- **Brine (10% NaCl solution):** Highest severity (H = 5.0 for still water + salt)
- **Water:** Moderate severity (H = 1.0 for still water)
- **Oil:** Lower severity (H = 0.25–0.30)
- **Air:** Lowest severity

> [!NOTE]
> **H-value (Heat Transfer Coefficient):** Defines the cooling power of a quenching medium. Higher H → faster cooling → deeper hardening.

### Hardness vs. Carbon Content (Key Relationship)
$$ HRC \approx 60 \times (\%C)^{0.5} \quad \text{(empirical, for fully hardened steel)} $$

| % Carbon | Approx. HRC after quenching |
|----------|------------------------------|
| 0.20 | ~30 HRC |
| 0.40 | ~40 HRC |
| 0.60 | ~50 HRC |
| 0.80 | ~62 HRC (max for plain carbon steel) |
| 1.00 | ~64 HRC |

---

## 2.5 Process 4: SPHEROIDIZING — Detailed Analysis

### Definition
Spheroidizing is a specialized heat treatment (a form of sub-critical annealing) in which **cementite (Fe₃C) in the pearlite is transformed into a spheroidal (globular) form** dispersed throughout a continuous ferrite matrix.

### Why Spheroidize?
- Produces the **softest possible structure** (even softer than fully annealed steel).
- The globular carbides present **minimum surface area per unit volume**, reducing the ferrite-carbide interfacial energy.
- **Maximum machinability** — chips break cleanly, tool life increases.
- Best structure for **cold working and cold heading** operations.
- Excellent **ductility and formability**.

### Methods of Spheroidizing
1. **Prolonged holding** at a temperature just below A₁ (~700 °C) for 15–25 hours.
2. **Cyclic (Oscillation) heating** between 700 °C and 740 °C for many cycles.
3. **Sub-critical quenching** from a temperature just above A₁.

---

## 2.6 Process 5: TEMPERING — Detailed Analysis

### Definition
Tempering is a heat treatment process in which **quenched (hardened) steel is reheated to a temperature *below* the eutectoid temperature (A₁ = 727 °C)**, held for a specific time, and then cooled, usually in air. It is **always performed after hardening** to reduce brittleness.

### The "Why" — Why Tempering is Necessary
As-quenched martensite is **extremely hard but dangerously brittle** — it can crack spontaneously or fracture under impact. Tempering is *not* an option; it is a **mandatory follow-up** to hardening for almost all practical applications.

### The Three Stages of Tempering
| Stage | Temperature | Transformation |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Stage 1 (Low-Temp)** | 100–250 °C | Martensite → **ε-Carbide** + low-C Martensite (retains high hardness) |
| **Stage 2 (Medium-Temp)** | 250–400 °C | Decomposition of **retained austenite** → Bainite |
| **Stage 3 (High-Temp)** | 400–650 °C | **Cementite (Fe₃C) precipitation and coarsening** + ferrite recovery |

### Hardness-Toughness Trade-off
$$ HRC \downarrow \quad \text{as} \quad T_{temper} \uparrow $$

$$ K_{IC} \text{ (Fracture Toughness)} \uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad T_{temper} \uparrow $$

### Categorization by Tempering Temperature
- **Low-Temperature Tempering (100–250 °C):** Cutting tools, bearings, gauges. Retains high HRC (~60).
- **Medium-Temperature Tempering (350–500 °C):** Springs, chisels, punches. HRC ~40–50.
- **High-Temperature Tempering (500–650 °C):** Gears, axles, structural components. HRC ~25–35, best toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME)"** occurs in the range **250–400 °C**. Steel tempered in this range shows a **local minimum in impact toughness**. **Avoid** this range for critical components.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| Critical Temperature A₁ | $A_1 = 727$ | °C (Eutectoid) |
| Hardness-Carbon relation | $HRC \approx 60\sqrt{\%C}$ | Empirical |
| Pearlite spacing | $S \propto 1/R_{cool}$ | Spacing ∝ 1/Cooling Rate |
| Hardness vs Tempering | $HRC \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ | Inverse relationship |
| Soaking time | $t = 1 + 1 \times (thickness_{inches} - 1)$ | hours |
| Grossmann H-value | Severity of quench | Dimensionless |
| Martensite transformation | $\gamma \xrightarrow{quench} \alpha'$ | Diffusionless |
| Critical cooling rate | $R_{critical} \approx 600$ | °C/s for eutectoid steel |
| TTT Nose Temp | $T_{nose} \approx 550$ | °C for eutectoid |
| Interlamellar spacing | $\lambda = 0.0116 \cdot R^{-0.43}$ | μm |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Process Used | Reason |
|----------------------|--------------|--------|
| Aircraft landing gear | Hardening + High-temp Tempering | High strength + toughness |
| Automotive gears | Normalizing + Induction Hardening | Wear resistance + tough core |
| Cutting tools (HSS) | Hardening + Double Tempering (560°C) | Hot hardness + red hardness |
| Springs (automotive) | Hardening + Medium-temp Tempering | Elastic limit + fatigue life |
| Die sets, moulds | Through-hardening + Tempering | Dimensional stability |
| Wire drawing (low-carbon) | Spheroidizing | Maximum ductility for cold drawing |
| Railway tracks | Normalizing | Toughness + wear resistance |
| Bolts, nuts, fasteners | Quenching + Tempering | Strength + ductility balance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Diameter (D_C) — Grossman Method

The **critical diameter** $D_C$ is the largest diameter of a round bar that can be hardened to **50% martensite at the center** when quenched in a "perfect" quench medium (H → ∞).

### Step 1: Concept of Severity of Quench
Grossmann defined the heat transfer coefficient of a quenching medium as a dimensionless number **H** (in⁻¹). For a given steel, the surface heat transfer is:

$$ h = H \cdot k_{steel} $$

where $k_{steel}$ is the thermal conductivity of steel and $H$ has units of in⁻¹.

### Step 2: Cooling Time at Center
The time required for the **center of a round bar of diameter D** to cool from 800 °C to 500 °C (just past the TTT nose) in a medium of severity H is:

$$ t_{800 \to 500} = \frac{5.4 \cdot D^2}{H} \quad [\text{for round bar}] $$

> This is the **Grossmann time-temperature relationship** governing through-hardening.

### Step 3: Critical Diameter Formula
For a "perfect" quench (H = ∞), the time becomes zero, and the corresponding bar diameter that *just* achieves 50% martensite is the **critical diameter D_C**:

$$ D_C = \sqrt{\frac{t_{nose} \cdot H_{\infty}}{5.4}} $$

For a real quench of severity H, the diameter achieving 50% martensite is the **ideal critical diameter D_I**:

$$ D_I = D_C \cdot \left(\frac{H}{H_{ideal}}\right)^{0.5} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D_I** is the hardenability index used in KTU exam problems. It is independent of the quenching medium and depends only on **austenite grain size** and **alloy content** of the steel.

### Step 4: Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A eutectoid steel (D_C = 0.7 in) is quenched in oil with H = 1.0 in⁻¹. Calculate the ideal critical diameter D_I.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify given values.
- $D_C = 0.7$ in
- $H_{oil} = 1.0$ in⁻¹
- For "ideal" quench, $H_{ideal} = \infty$ → but use reference: for still water, H = 1.0 (so use normalized H).

Step 2: Apply Grossmann formula for a real quench medium.
For oil, $H_{oil} = 1.0$ in⁻¹ (relative to brine = 5.0):
$$ D_I = D_C \times \left(\frac{H_{oil}}{H_{water}}\right)^{0.5} $$

Step 3: For a still-water quench reference, H = 1.0. So:
$$ D_I = 0.7 \times \left(\frac{1.0}{1.0}\right)^{0.5} = 0.7 \text{ in} $$

Step 4: For oil (H = 0.3 in⁻¹ relative to water):
$$ D_I = 0.7 \times \left(\frac{0.3}{1.0}\right)^{0.5} = 0.7 \times 0.547 = 0.383 \text{ in} $$

**Result:** In oil, only a bar of diameter ≤ 0.383 inch will achieve 50% martensite at the center.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Jominy End-Quench Hardenability Test

The Jominy test is the **standard KTU-recognized method** for measuring hardenability.

### Step 1: Test Setup
- A cylindrical specimen: **25.4 mm diameter × 101.6 mm length (1 in × 4 in)**
- **Austenitized** at the appropriate temperature.
- **Water jet** is sprayed on **one end face only** (the bottom end).
- Other surfaces are air-cooled.

### Step 2: Cooling Rate vs. Distance
The cooling rate at the **jominy distance J** (in 1/16 inch units) from the quenched end is:

$$ R(J) = R_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda J} $$

where $R_0$ is the cooling rate at the quenched end (~600 °C/s for water) and $\lambda$ is a decay constant.

| Jominy Distance J (1/16 in) | Equivalent Cooling Rate (°C/s) |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | ~250 |
| 4 | ~50 |
| 8 | ~20 |
| 12 | ~8 |
| 16 | ~4 |

### Step 3: Hardness Profile
Hardness is measured at **0.5 mm** intervals from the quenched end, producing the **Jominy Curve** (HRC vs. J).

> [!NOTE]
> The Jominy curve is a "fingerprint" of a specific steel grade. Steels with **higher alloy content** (Cr, Ni, Mo) have **flatter** Jominy curves → better hardenability.

### Step 4: Worked Example

**Problem:** A Jominy test on a steel gives HRC = 55 at J = 4 and HRC = 35 at J = 12. A gear of diameter 50 mm is required to have HRC ≥ 45 at its center. Determine suitability.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Find position on Jominy curve corresponding to cooling rate of gear center.
For a 50 mm diameter bar quenched in oil, the equivalent Jominy distance is given by empirical correlation:
$$ J_{equivalent} = \frac{D}{2 \times 1.6} \approx \frac{50}{3.2} \approx 15.6 $$

Step 2: At J = 15.6 (interpolating from HRC=35 at J=12 to HRC=25 at J=20):
$$ HRC \approx 35 - \frac{35-25}{20-12} \times (15.6-12) = 35 - 4.5 = 30.5 \text{ HRC} $$

Step 3: Compare with requirement.
$$ HRC_{calculated} = 30.5 < 45 \text{ (required)} $$

**Conclusion:** The steel is **not suitable** for this application. Either select a steel with better hardenability or use a more severe quench medium (e.g., brine instead of oil).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of All Five Processes

| Property | Annealing | Normalizing | Hardening | Spheroidizing | Tempering |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Heating Temp** | Above A₃ (30-50°C) | Above A₃ (30-50°C) | Above A₃ (30-50°C) | Below A₁ (prolonged) | Below A₁ (100-650°C) |
| **Cooling Rate** | Very slow (furnace) | Moderate (still air) | Very fast (quench) | Very slow | Air (varies) |
| **Cooling Medium** | Furnace | Air | Water/Oil/Brine | Furnace | Air/Oil |
| **Resulting Phase** | Coarse Pearlite | Fine Pearlite | Martensite | Spheroidite | Tempered Martensite |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | Low (~10-20) | Medium (~20-30) | High (~60-65) | Lowest (~5-15) | Variable (~25-60) |
| **Ductility** | High | Medium-High | Very Low | Maximum | Medium-High |
| **Toughness** | High | High | Very Low (brittle) | Low (but workable) | High |
| **Internal Stresses** | Relieved | Reduced | Maximum (high risk) | Relieved | Reduced significantly |
| **Machinability** | Good | Good | Poor (needs tempering) | Excellent | Good |
| **Microstructure** | Coarse lamellar | Fine lamellar | BCT (acicular) | Globular carbides | Tempered structure |
| **Dimensional Change** | Slight shrinkage | Slight shrinkage | Significant distortion | Minimal | Slight change |
| **Applications** | Pre-machining | Pre-hardening | Cutting tools | Cold forming | Post-hardening |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hardness Reduction During Tempering

The **Hollomon-Jaffe parameter** describes the combined effect of tempering temperature and time on hardness:

$$ H_{tempered} = H_{quenched} - K \cdot P $$

where the **Hollomon-Jaffe parameter P** is:

$$ P = T \cdot (\log t + C) $$

with:
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**
- $t$ = tempering time in **hours**
- $C$ = constant (≈ 20 for steels)
- $K$ = material-dependent constant

### Step-by-Step Derivation
Step 1: Recognize that the tempering process follows an Arrhenius-type rate law:
$$ \frac{dH}{dt} = -A \cdot e^{-Q/RT} $$

Step 2: Integrate over time t to obtain:
$$ \Delta H = -A \cdot t \cdot e^{-Q/RT} $$

Step 3: Taking the logarithm and combining constants:
$$ \log(\Delta H) = \log t - \frac{Q}{2.3 R T} + \text{const} $$

Step 4: Rearranging:
$$ P = T \cdot (\log t + C) = \frac{Q}{2.3 R} \quad \text{at constant hardness} $$

**Engineering implication:** The same final hardness can be obtained by using a **higher temperature for a shorter time** or a **lower temperature for a longer time**. This is the **principle of temper equivalence**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Tempering Calculation

**Problem:** A quenched steel specimen of HRC 62 is tempered at 400 °C for 2 hours. Using the Hollomon-Jaffe relation with C = 20, determine the equivalent tempering time at 450 °C to obtain the same final hardness.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the Hollomon-Jaffe parameter at 400 °C.
$$ T_1 = 400 + 273 = 673 \text{ K} $$
$$ P_1 = 673 \times (\log 2 + 20) = 673 \times (0.301 + 20) = 673 \times 20.301 $$
$$ P_1 = 13{,}662.6 \text{ K·log-hr} $$

Step 2: For the same P at 450 °C:
$$ T_2 = 450 + 273 = 723 \text{ K} $$
$$ P_2 = P_1 = 13{,}662.6 $$

Step 3: Solve for log t₂:
$$ 13{,}662.6 = 723 \times (\log t_2 + 20) $$
$$ \frac{13{,}662.6}{723} = \log t_2 + 20 $$
$$ 18.897 = \log t_2 + 20 $$
$$ \log t_2 = -1.103 $$
$$ t_2 = 10^{-1.103} = 0.079 \text{ hours} $$

Step 4: Convert to minutes.
$$ t_2 = 0.079 \times 60 \approx 4.74 \text{ minutes} $$

**Result:** Tempering at 450 °C requires only ~4.74 minutes to achieve the same hardness reduction as 2 hours at 400 °C. **Higher temperature dramatically shortens the required time** — a critical concept in industrial process optimization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
