# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — From Wöhler Data to Basquin's Law

A standard **rotating-beam fatigue test** (per ASTM E466) on a polished steel specimen (UTS = $\sigma_u = 620$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa) produces the following test data:

| Specimen # | Stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | Cycles to failure $N_f$ |
|-----------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | 480 | $8.2 \times 10^3$ |
| 2 | 420 | $1.9 \times 10^4$ |
| 3 | 360 | $5.6 \times 10^4$ |
| 4 | 310 | $1.7 \times 10^5$ |
| 5 | 280 | $5.2 \times 10^5$ |
| 6 | 250 | $1.4 \times 10^6$ |
| 7 | 230 | $4.8 \times 10^6$ |

**Goal:** Determine the **Basquin's law parameters** $\sigma'_f$ and $b$, and the **endurance limit** $S'_e$.

### Step 1 — Convert all data to log form

Basquin's law: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$

Take logarithm of both sides:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b\,\log(2N_f)$$

This is a **straight line** of the form $Y = m X + c$ on a **log-log plot**, with:
- Slope $m = b$ (the fatigue strength exponent)
- Y-intercept $c = \log \sigma'_f$

### Step 2 — Tabulate transformed data

| $\log \sigma_a$ | $\log (2N_f)$ |
|-----------------|----------------|
| 2.6812 | 4.2148 |
| 2.6232 | 4.5798 |
| 2.5563 | 5.0492 |
| 2.4914 | 5.5315 |
| 2.4472 | 6.0170 |
| 2.3979 | 6.4472 |
| 2.3617 | 6.9823 |

### Step 3 — Compute the linear regression coefficients

Using the least-squares method:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i = \frac{1}{7}\sum \log(2N_f) = 38.8218/7 = 5.5459$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{7}\sum \log \sigma_a = 17.5589/7 = 2.5084$$

$$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}$$

Computing term-by-term (sampled rows):

$$(X_1-\bar X)(Y_1-\bar Y) = (4.2148-5.5459)(2.6812-2.5084) = (-1.3311)(0.1728) = -0.2300$$

Summing all seven products:

$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) \approx -0.8058$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 \approx 5.4527$$

Therefore:

$$b = \frac{-0.8058}{5.4527} = -0.1478$$

### Step 4 — Compute the intercept $\log \sigma'_f$

$$\log \sigma'_f = \bar Y - b \bar X = 2.5084 - (-0.1478)(5.5459) = 2.5084 + 0.8197 = 3.3281$$

$$\sigma'_f = 10^{3.3281} \approx 2130\ \text{MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Verify against $\sigma_u$

For many steels, $\sigma'_f \approx \sigma_u$ (within ±10%). Here $\sigma_u = 620$ MPa, but $\sigma'_f = 2130$ MPa. This is **expected and correct**: $\sigma'_f$ is the *extrapolated* intercept at $2N = 1$, not a physically meaningful stress. It is a fitting parameter.

### Step 6 — State Basquin's Law

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = 2130\,(2N_f)^{-0.1478} \quad [\text{MPa}]}$$

### Step 7 — Endurance limit (Knee-point estimate)

For steels, $S'_e$ is the value of $\sigma_a$ at $N = 10^6$ cycles. Substitute $2N_f = 2\times 10^6$:

$$\sigma_e = 2130 \times (2 \times 10^6)^{-0.1478} = 2130 \times 10^{-0.1478 \times 6.3010} = 2130 \times 10^{-0.9314}$$

$$\sigma_e = 2130 \times 0.1169 = 248.9\ \text{MPa}$$

This matches the tabulated test value (250 MPa at $1.4 \times 10^6$ cycles) to within 0.5%, confirming our fit.

### Step 8 — Empirical check

$$S'_e / \sigma_u = 248.9 / 620 = 0.401 \approx 0.4 \text{ (close to textbook 0.5 for polished specimen)}$$

Discrepancy is explained by the particular steel grade being slightly inferior in cyclic behaviour.

### Step 9 — Final summary

$$\boxed{S'_e \approx 249\ \text{MPa},\quad b = -0.148,\quad \sigma'_f = 2130\ \text{MPa}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Marin's Modified Endurance Limit

AISI 1045 hot-rolled steel shaft, $\sigma_u = 570$ MPa, rotating-bending, $d = 50$ mm, machined surface, $T = 250\ ^\circ C$, $99\%$ reliability.

### Step 1 — Baseline

$$S'_e = 0.5 \sigma_u = 0.5 \times 570 = 285\ \text{MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Surface factor $k_a$ (machined, $\sigma_u = 570$ MPa)

From the standard Marin surface-finish table:

$$k_a = a \cdot \sigma_u^b = 4.51 \times 570^{-0.265}$$

$$\log k_a = \log 4.51 - 0.265 \log 570 = 0.6542 - 0.265 \times 2.7559 = 0.6542 - 0.7303 = -0.0761$$

$$k_a = 10^{-0.0761} = 0.839$$

### Step 3 — Size factor $k_b$ ($d = 50$ mm, non-rotating)

For axial or rotating-bending on a round shaft with $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm:

$$k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = \left(\frac{50}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107}$$

$$\log k_b = -0.107 \log(50/7.62) = -0.107 \log(6.5617) = -0.107 \times 0.8171 = -0.0874$$

$$k_b = 10^{-0.0874} = 0.818$$

### Step 4 — Load factor $k_c$ (bending)

$$k_c = 1.0 \quad \text{(reliable value for pure bending)}$$

For combined loading, use the equivalent stress: $\sigma_a' = \sqrt{3\,\tau_a^2 + \sigma_a^2}$.

### Step 5 — Temperature factor $k_d$ ($T = 250\ ^\circ C$)

From Marin table at $250\ ^\circ C$: $k_d \approx 0.875$ (interpolated between $20\ ^\circ C$ and $500\ ^\circ C$).

### Step 6 — Reliability factor $k_e$ ($99\%$)

$$k_e = 0.814 \quad \text{(standard table value)}$$

### Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor $k_f$ (no corrosion, no fretting)

$$k_f = 1.0$$

### Step 8 — Compute modified $S_e$

$$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e = 0.839 \times 0.818 \times 1.0 \times 0.875 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 285$$

Computing sequentially:

$$0.839 \times 0.818 = 0.6863$$

$$0.6863 \times 1.0 = 0.6863$$

$$0.6863 \times 0.875 = 0.6005$$

$$0.6005 \times 0.814 = 0.4888$$

$$0.4888 \times 285 = 139.3\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 139\ \text{MPa}}$$

### Step 9 — Engineering interpretation

The "ideal" rotating-beam endurance limit was 285 MPa, but **real engineering factors have reduced the safe alternating stress to less than 50% of the ideal**. This is precisely why Marin factors exist in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Norton's Power Law: Time to Reach a Strain Limit

A nickel-superalloy turbine blade operates at $T = 1050\ ^\circ C$ and $\sigma = 150$ MPa. The material has:
- $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ (MPa$^{-n}$·s$^{-1}$)
- $n = 4.5$
- $Q_c = 290$ kJ/mol
- Strain limit for re-sleeving: $\varepsilon_{max} = 1.5\%$

**Find:** Maximum safe operating time.

### Step 1 — Compute $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}$ using Norton–Bailey

$$T = 1050 + 273.15 = 1323.15\ \text{K}$$

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A\,\sigma^n\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

Exponent argument:

$$-\frac{Q_c}{RT} = -\frac{290{,}000}{8.314 \times 1323.15} = -\frac{290{,}000}{11{,}001.6} = -26.36$$

$$\exp(-26.36) = 3.69 \times 10^{-12}$$

Power term:

$$\sigma^n = 150^{4.5}$$

$$\log(150^{4.5}) = 4.5 \log 150 = 4.5 \times 2.1761 = 9.7924$$

$$150^{4.5} = 10^{9.7924} = 6.19 \times 10^9\ \text{MPa}^{4.5}$$

Combining:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.19 \times 10^9 \times 3.69 \times 10^{-12}$$

Step by step:

$$2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.19 \times 10^9 = 1.5475 \times 10^{4}$$

$$1.5475 \times 10^{4} \times 3.69 \times 10^{-12} = 5.71 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to engineering units

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = 5.71 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1} \times 3600\ \text{s/h} = 2.06 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Safe time (assuming we operate only in the linear secondary regime, ignoring primary/tertiary contributions)

$$t_{safe} = \frac{\varepsilon_{max}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}} = \frac{0.015}{2.06 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}} = 72.8\ \text{h}$$

$$\boxed{t_{safe} \approx 73\ \text{hours}}$$

### Step 4 — Critical engineering insight

This is **only the secondary-creep time**. Including primary and tertiary stages (factor of $\approx 0.7$–$0.8$ of total life in secondary) the *total* time-to-rupture is shorter, around $50$–$55$ h. Real engine overhaul intervals are longer because (a) the stress is *not* always 150 MPa (mission profile averaging), and (b) $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}$ is the *design floor* — actual creep rate in early life is lower.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Larson–Miller Extrapolation

A superalloy tested in the lab at:
- $T_1 = 1100\ ^\circ C = 1373\ \text{K}$, $t_{r,1} = 100$ h
- $T_2 = 1050\ ^\circ C = 1323\ \text{K}$, $t_{r,2} = 1000$ h
- $T_3 = 1000\ ^\circ C = 1273\ \text{K}$, $t_{r,3} = 10{,}000$ h

All at the same stress. Find the **Larson–Miller constant** $C_{LM}$, then extrapolate to $T_4 = 950\ ^\circ C$ for **100,000 h life**.

### Step 1 — Compute $P_{LM}$ for each test

Using $C_{LM} = 20$:

$$P_{1} = 1373 \times (\log 100 + 20) = 1373 \times 22 = 30{,}206$$

$$P_{2} = 1323 \times (\log 1000 + 20) = 1323 \times 23 = 30{,}429$$

$$P_{3} = 1273 \times (\log 10{,}000 + 20) = 1273 \times 24 = 30{,}552$$

### Step 2 — Check consistency (should be equal at constant $\sigma$)

The values 30,206 / 30,429 / 30,552 should be **identical** at fixed $\sigma$. The spread is $\approx 1.1\%$, well within experimental error. Use the average:

$$\bar P = 30{,}396$$

### Step 3 — Back-solve for $C_{LM}$ (improved value)

Average: $T_{avg} = 1323\ \text{K}$, $t_{r,avg} \approx 2154$ h, $\log t_{r,avg} = 3.333$.

$$C_{LM} = \frac{\bar P}{T_{avg}} - \log t_{r,avg} = \frac{30{,}396}{1323} - 3.333 = 22.974 - 3.333 = 19.64$$

Use $C_{LM} = 19.64$ for this alloy.

### Step 4 — Extrapolate to $T_4 = 1223$ K, $t_r = 10^5$ h

$$P_{LM,4} = 1223 \times (5 + 19.64) = 1223 \times 24.64 = 30{,}135$$

### Step 5 — Compare to other tests

Since $P_{LM,4} = 30{,}135$ is **less** than $\bar P = 30{,}396$, the design stress at $T_4 = 950\ ^\circ C$ for 100,000 h life must be **slightly higher** than the original lab stress. From a typical $P_{LM}$ vs. $\log \sigma$ master curve (chart-based lookup in KTU), the corresponding design stress is read off.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Question Type | Method | Equation to Use |
|---------------|--------|-----------------|
| Find life at given stress, no mean stress | Basquin | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f(2N_f)^b$ |
| Find life with mean stress $\sigma_m \neq 0$ | Goodman + Basquin | $\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = \sigma_a / (1 - \sigma_m/\sigma_u)$ |
| Find life in LCF (plastic) | Coffin–Manson | $\Delta\varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ |
| Find cycles to grow a known crack | Paris Law (integrated) | $N_f = (a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2}) / [C(\Delta\sigma)^m Y^m \pi^{m/2}(1 - m/2)]$ |
| Find safe design life at high $T$ | Norton + Monkman–Grant | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A\sigma^n e^{-Q/RT}$, $t_r = C_{MG} / \dot{\varepsilon}_{min}$ |
| Extrapolate lab data to design life | Larson–Miller | $P_{LM} = T(\log t_r + 20)$ |
| Calculate safe alternating stress | Marin + Goodman | $S_e = \prod k_i S'_e$, then $\sigma_a \le S_e (1 - \sigma_m/\sigma_u)$ |

    G --> J[SIF range Delta K increases with a]
    J --> K[da/dN = C times Delta K to power m]
    K --> L[Beach marks and striations on fracture surface]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Define fatigue. What is an S-N curve? Briefly explain the endurance limit.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Fatigue (1 mark):** Fatigue is the progressive and localized structural damage of a material caused by cyclic loading at stress levels below the static yield strength, which may eventually lead to crack initiation and complete fracture after a sufficient number of load cycles. (ASTM E1823 definition acceptable)
>
> **S-N curve (1 mark):** The S-N curve (Wöhler curve) is a plot of cyclic stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (S) on the ordinate versus the number of cycles to failure $N_f$ (N) on the abscissa, drawn on a log-log or semi-log scale, obtained from rotating-bending, axial, or torsion fatigue tests on standardized specimens.
>
> **Endurance limit (1 mark):** The endurance limit $\sigma_e$ (or $S'_e$) is the maximum alternating stress below which the material can endure an *infinite* number of cycles (conventionally $\ge 10^6$–$10^7$ cycles) without failure. It is exhibited by ferrous metals (steels, Ti) and corresponds to the horizontal asymptote of the S-N curve. For non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Mg), no true endurance limit exists; the design stress is the **fatigue strength** at a specified cycle count (e.g., $10^8$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**What is creep? Name and briefly describe the three stages of creep with a neat sketch.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Creep (1 mark):** Creep is the time-dependent permanent (inelastic) deformation of a material when subjected to a constant load or constant stress at an elevated temperature, typically at $T > 0.4\,T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature).
>
> **Three stages (2 marks — write 1-line each, draw the curve):**
>
> 1. **Primary (transient) creep:** Creep rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ *decreases* with time due to strain hardening. Empirical fit: $\varepsilon \propto t^{1/3}$.
> 2. **Secondary (steady-state) creep:** Creep rate is *constant and minimum* — strain hardening balanced by thermal recovery. This stage dominates the design life and obeys Norton's law $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A\sigma^n e^{-Q_c/RT}$.
> 3. **Tertiary creep:** Creep rate *accelerates* rapidly due to internal damage (void formation, grain boundary cavitation) and necking, leading to rupture.
>
> ```
> ε  │        • tertiary (rupture)
>    │       /
>    │      /
>    │     /  secondary (linear)
>    │    /
>    │   /
>    │  /  primary (decreasing slope)
>    │ /
>    │/___________ t
>        t_r
> ```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Module 2 Internal Choice — Question Pattern (KTU 2024)**

For each Part B question in Module 2, the examiner provides **two alternative 14-mark questions** (Or pattern). The student answers **one**. Each is divided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PCIET205]**

**A.** (a) What is fatigue? With a neat sketch, explain the S-N curve and discuss the differences in the curve for ferrous and non-ferrous materials. (7 marks)
   (b) A rotating-bending fatigue test on a polished steel specimen ($\sigma_u = 700$ MPa) yields the following data:

| $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | $N_f$ (cycles) |
|---|---|
| 550 | $1 \times 10^4$ |
| 450 | $8 \times 10^4$ |
| 380 | $5 \times 10^5$ |
| 320 | $2 \times 10^6$ |
| 290 | $1 \times 10^7$ (run-out, no failure) |

   Determine the Basquin's law parameters $\sigma'_f$ and $b$, and the endurance limit $S'_e$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Fatigue is the progressive, localized permanent structural damage occurring in a material subjected to fluctuating stresses and strains at stress levels below the static yield strength, ultimately leading to crack or fracture.
2. **S-N curve sketch (2 marks):** Show $\log \sigma_a$ on Y-axis, $\log N_f$ on X-axis. Plot a downward-sloping line that becomes horizontal at the endurance limit $S'_e$ at $N \approx 10^6$–$10^7$. Label the three regions: overload, finite life, infinite life.
3. **Ferrous vs non-ferrous comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | Ferrous (Steels) | Non-Ferrous (Al, Cu, Mg) |
|---------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Curve shape | Has horizontal asymptote | Continues to slope down |
| Endurance limit | Yes, at $N \approx 10^6$ | No true endurance limit |
| Microstructural reason | BCC slip becomes athermal at low $\sigma$ | FCC cross-slip always possible |
| Design basis | $\sigma_e$ (constant) | $S_N$ at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ |
| Empirical $S'_e$ | $\approx 0.5 \sigma_u$ | $\approx 0.3$–$0.4 \sigma_u$ at $10^8$ |
| Strain-aging effect | Yes — improves endurance | None |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Form pairs $(\log \sigma_a, \log N_f)$:

- $(2.7404, 4.0000)$
- $(2.6532, 4.9031)$
- $(2.5798, 5.6990)$
- $(2.5051, 6.3010)$

(Exclude the run-out point at $10^7$ cycles for Basquin fit — it is below the knee.)

**Step 2:** Linear regression:

$$\bar X = (4.0000 + 4.9031 + 5.6990 + 6.3010)/4 = 5.2258$$

$$\bar Y = (2.7404 + 2.6532 + 2.5798 + 2.5051)/4 = 2.6196$$

**Step 3:** Compute slope $b$:

$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) = (-1.2258)(0.1208) + (-0.3227)(0.0336) + (0.4732)(-0.0398) + (1.0752)(-0.1145)$$

$$= -0.1481 - 0.0108 - 0.0188 - 0.1231 = -0.3008$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = 1.5026 + 0.1041 + 0.2239 + 1.1561 = 2.9867$$

$$b = \frac{-0.3008}{2.9867} = -0.1007 \approx -0.10$$

**Step 4:** Compute intercept $\log \sigma'_f$:

$$\log \sigma'_f = \bar Y - b\bar X = 2.6196 - (-0.1007)(5.2258) = 2.6196 + 0.5262 = 3.1458$$

$$\sigma'_f = 10^{3.1458} = 1398 \approx 1400\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Endurance limit from run-out data:

$$\boxed{S'_e \approx 290\ \text{MPa} \text{ (between } 290 \text{ MPa run-out and 320 MPa at 2×10}^6)}$$

**Step 6:** Verification — $S'_e/\sigma_u = 290/700 = 0.414$, which is in the expected range of $0.4$–$0.5$ for steels.

**Step 7:** Basquin's law:

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = 1400\,(2N_f)^{-0.10}\ \text{MPa}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PCIET205]**

**B.** (a) Explain the three stages of creep with a strain–time diagram. Derive Norton's power law for secondary creep and discuss the parameters. (7 marks)
   (b) A steel pressure vessel operates at $T = 550\ ^\circ C$ under a steady hoop stress of $80$ MPa. Norton's law constants are: $A = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ (MPa$^{-n}$·s$^{-1}$), $n = 4.2$, $Q_c = 260$ kJ/mol. Determine: (i) the steady-state creep rate, (ii) the time to reach a creep strain of $1\%$ in the secondary stage, and (iii) the rupture life using the Monkman–Grant relation with $C_{MG} = 0.05$ and $m' = 0.9$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Three stages with diagram (3 marks):** Plot $\varepsilon$ vs $t$ showing instantaneous elastic strain $\varepsilon_0$, then primary (decreasing slope), secondary (constant slope = minimum rate), and tertiary (accelerating to rupture).
2. **Derivation of Norton's law (3 marks):** Start from the physically based Weertman–Bird model for dislocation climb creep:
   $$\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \rho_m \cdot v_{climb}$$
   where $\rho_m$ is mobile dislocation density and $v_{climb}$ is climb velocity. With $v_{climb} \propto D_{sd} \sigma$ ($D_{sd}$ = self-diffusion coefficient), $D_{sd} = D_0 \exp(-Q_c/RT)$, and $\rho_m \propto \sigma^2$, we get $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \sigma^3 e^{-Q_c/RT}$. The generalized form is **Norton's law**:
   $$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A \sigma^n e^{-Q_c/RT}}$$
3. **Discussion of parameters (1 mark):**
   - $A$ = structure-dependent constant (units: MPa$^{-n}$ s$^{-1}$)
   - $n$ = stress exponent (1 = diffusional; 3–8 = dislocation climb; >8 = power-law breakdown)
   - $Q_c$ = activation energy for creep (≈ self-diffusion, e.g., 280 kJ/mol for $\gamma$-Fe, 290 kJ/mol for Ni)
   - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
   - $T$ = **absolute** temperature (K)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin:

$$T = 550 + 273.15 = 823.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponential term:

$$-\frac{Q_c}{RT} = -\frac{260{,}000}{8.314 \times 823.15} = -\frac{260{,}000}{6843.0} = -37.99$$

$$e^{-37.99} = 2.55 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the power term:

$$\sigma^n = 80^{4.2}$$

$$\log(80^{4.2}) = 4.2 \log 80 = 4.2 \times 1.9031 = 7.9930$$

$$80^{4.2} = 10^{7.9930} = 9.84 \times 10^7\ \text{MPa}^{4.2}$$

**Step 4:** (i) Steady-state creep rate:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.84 \times 10^7 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$= 8 \times 9.84 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-5+7-17} = 200.7 \times 10^{-15} = 2.007 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{s}^{-1} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Step 5:** (ii) Time to reach $\varepsilon = 1\% = 0.01$ (in secondary stage):

$$t_{1\%} = \frac{0.01}{2.007 \times 10^{-13}} = 4.98 \times 10^{10}\ \text{s}$$

Convert to hours: $4.98 \times 10^{10} / 3600 = 1.38 \times 10^7$ h $\approx 1580$ years.

$$\boxed{t_{1\%} \approx 1.38 \times 10^7\ \text{hours} \approx 1580\ \text{years}}$$

**Step 6:** (iii) Rupture life from Monkman–Grant:

$$t_r = \left(\frac{C_{MG}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}}\right)^{1/m'} = \left(\frac{0.05}{2.007 \times 10^{-13}}\right)^{1/0.9}$$

$$\frac{C_{MG}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}} = \frac{0.05}{2.007 \times 10^{-13}} = 2.49 \times 10^{11}\ \text{s}$$

$$\log(2.49 \times 10^{11}) = 11.396$$

$$(2.49 \times 10^{11})^{1/0.9} = 10^{11.396/0.9} = 10^{12.66} = 4.57 \times 10^{12}\ \text{s}$$

Convert to hours: $4.57 \times 10^{12} / 3600 = 1.27 \times 10^9$ h $\approx 145{,}000$ years.

$$\boxed{t_r \approx 1.27 \times 10^9\ \text{hours} \approx 145{,}000\ \text{years}}$$

**Step 7:** Sanity check. The computed creep rate is extremely low because the operating temperature (550 °C) is significantly below $0.4 T_m$ for steel ($0.4 \times 1538\ ^\circ C = 615\ ^\circ C$). The result is physically consistent: 550 °C is borderline — design is safe for decades, but rupture life is essentially infinite. If $T$ were raised to 650 °C, the rate would increase by $\exp[Q/R(1/823 - 1/923)] = \exp(8.0) = 2980\times$, bringing the rupture life to under 50 years.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PCIET205 Module 2:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $E$ in Coffin–Manson total-strain equation.** Students write the elastic and plastic parts but forget to multiply $\sigma'_f / E$. For steel, $\sigma'_f / E \approx 1400/210000 \approx 0.0067$, which is non-negligible.
> 2. **Wrong sign of $b$ in Basquin's law.** $b$ is *negative*. The exam has been known to deduct 1 mark for writing $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f(2N_f)^{+0.1}$.
> 3. **Mixing °C with K in Norton's law.** $T$ **must** be absolute (Kelvin). Even $1\ ^\circ C$ error → $\sim 0.3\%$ error in $1/T$ → $\sim 10\%$ error in $e^{-Q/RT}$. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units of $\dot{\varepsilon}$.** Norton's law often yields $\dot{\varepsilon}$ in s$^{-1}$. Question asks for h$^{-1}$, %/h, or mm/mm/h — you must convert.
> 5. **Confusing fatigue strength with endurance limit.** Fatigue strength is at a *given* $N$; endurance limit is at *infinite* $N$ (only for steels).
> 6. **Mean stress mishandling.** If the question gives a *fluctuating* $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$, students must *first* compute $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$ before applying Goodman/Gerber.
> 7. **Monkman–Grant exponent confusion.** Some students use $m' = 1$ universally. Use the value given in the problem; if not given, use $m' \approx 0.85$.
> 8. **S-N curve orientation.** The conventional plot has $\sigma_a$ on Y-axis (linear) and $N_f$ on X-axis (log). Drawing it reversed loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — One-Page Rapid-Revision Summary**

### **FATIGUE — MUST REMEMBER**

- [ ] **Definition:** Progressive localized damage under cyclic stress below yield.
- [ ] **Three stages:** Initiation (90% of HCF life) → Propagation (Paris law) → Final fracture.
- [ ] **S-N curve types:** Ferrous has *endurance limit* $S'_e \approx 0.5\sigma_u$ at $10^6$ cycles; non-ferrous has *no* true limit — use fatigue strength at $10^8$.
- [ ] **Cyclic stress parameters:** $\sigma_m$, $\sigma_a$, $R$, $A$ — must compute all four.
- [ ] **Basquin's law (HCF):** $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$, $b \in [-0.05, -0.12]$.
- [ ] **Coffin–Manson (LCF):** $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$, $c \in [-0.5, -0.7]$.
- [ ] **Total strain-life:** elastic + plastic both obey power law in $N$.
- [ ] **Paris law:** $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$, valid in $10^{-8}$–$10^{-5}$ mm/cycle range, $m \approx 3$–$4$.
- [ ] **Marin factors:** $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ — six factors, all $\le 1$, multiplied.
- [ ] **Goodman line (most asked):** $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_u = 1/n$. Gerber uses squared $\sigma_m/\sigma_u$.
- [ ] **Neuber's rule:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, $q$ = notch sensitivity.
- [ ] **Standard $R = -1$ test** = rotating-beam; converted to other $R$ via mean-stress corrections.

### **CREEP — MUST REMEMBER**

- [ ] **Definition:** Time-dependent plastic flow at $T > 0.4\,T_m$ under constant load.
- [ ] **Three stages:** Primary (decreasing) → Secondary (minimum rate = design basis) → Tertiary (damage → rupture).
- [ ] **Norton's power law (Norton–Bailey):** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A\sigma^n e^{-Q_c/RT}$. **Use Kelvin for $T$.**
- [ ] **$n$ values:** $n = 1$ → diffusional (Nabarro–Herring/Coble); $n = 3$–$8$ → dislocation climb (most common); $n > 8$ → power-law breakdown.
- [ ] **Monkman–Grant:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min}\,t_r^{m'} = C_{MG}$ — $m' \approx 0.85$–$1.0$.
- [ ] **Larson–Miller:** $P_{LM} = T(\log t_r + 20)$, $T$ in K, $t_r$ in hours, $C_{LM} = 20$ default.
- [ ] **Sherby–Dorn:** $P_{SD} = \log t_r - Q_c/(2.3 RT)$ — alternative master parameter.
- [ ] **Activation energies:** $Q_c \approx Q_{self-diffusion}$; 280 kJ/mol for $\gamma$-Fe, 290 kJ/mol for Ni-base, 150 kJ/mol for Al.
- [ ] **Diffusional creep mechanisms:** Nabarro–Herring (lattice, $d^2$ in denominator, $T > 0.7T_m$), Coble (grain boundary, $d^3$, fine grain).
- [ ] **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is universal; do not confuse with other R's.

### **FACTORS AFFECTING FATIGUE LIFE — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE**

| # | Factor | Effect on Life | Direction |
|---|--------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Surface roughness ↑** | Stress concentration at machining marks | ↓ Life |
| 2 | **Cross-section size ↑** | Larger stressed volume = more defects | ↓ Life ($k_b < 1$) |
| 3 | **Mean stress ↑ (tensile)** | Opens cracks | ↓ Life |
| 4 | **Mean stress ↓ (compressive)** | Closes cracks | ↑ Life (shot peening!) |
| 5 | **Temperature ↑** | Softening, oxidation, creep-fatigue interaction | ↓ Life ($k_d < 1$) |
| 6 | **Notch (stress concentration) ↑** | $K_t$ increases | ↓ Life ($K_f$ reduces $S_e$) |
| 7 | **Corrosive medium** | Stress-corrosion-cracking + cyclic | ↓ Life drastically |
| 8 | **Residual stress — compressive (shot peening, autofrettage)** | Closes surface cracks | ↑ Life 2–4× |
| 9 | **Residual stress — tensile (welding, grinding)** | Adds to applied $\sigma_m$ | ↓ Life |
| 10 | **Grain size ↑** | Easier slip band formation | ↓ Life in HCF; ↑ Life in LCF |
| 11 | **Loading frequency ↓** | More time for creep/oxidation/corrosion per cycle | ↓ Life (corrosion fatigue) |
| 12 | **Multiaxial loading** | Equivalent $\sigma_a$ by Von Mises | ↓ Life vs uniaxial |

### **FACTORS AFFECTING CREEP — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE**

| # | Factor | Effect on Creep Rate | Mechanism |
|---|--------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Temperature ↑** | Exponential ↑ ($\propto e^{-Q/RT}$ denominator effect) | Enhanced vacancy diffusion, climb |
| 2 | **Applied stress ↑** | Power-law ↑ ($\propto \sigma^n$) | More dislocation motion |
| 3 | **Melting point $T_m$ ↑** | For a given $T$, $T/T_m$ ↓, rate ↓ | Why superalloys use Ni, Co |
| 4 | **Grain size ↑** | ↓ Dislocation creep (longer slip path); ↑ Diffusional creep | $d^2$ and $d^3$ in NH and Coble |
| 5 | **Grain size ↓** | ↑ Coble creep (more GB area) | Opposite of above |
| 6 | **Alloying — solid solution ↑** | ↓ Creep rate | Solute atmosphere drags dislocations |
| 7 | **Alloying — precipitation $\uparrow$ (e.g., $\gamma'$ in Ni-superalloys)** | ↓↓ Creep rate drastically | Particles block dislocation motion (Orowan bowing) |
| 8 | **Stacking fault energy (SFE) ↑** | ↑ Cross-slip, easier recovery | ↑ Creep rate (Al high SFE vs Cu low SFE) |
| 9 | **Cold work ↑** | ↑ Initial rate, but faster primary → lower secondary rate | Strain hardening effect |
| 10 | **Environment (oxidation, hot corrosion)** | ↑ Tertiary damage | Surface degradation |
| 11 | **Crystallographic texture (single crystal blades)** | ↓ Grain boundary sliding | Used in modern HP turbine blades |
| 12 | **Loading mode (tension vs torsion vs biaxial)** | Von Mises equivalent governs | Multiaxial reduces life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor for Creep Factor #7 — the most industrially important:**
> The single largest contributor to creep resistance in Ni-base superalloys (Inconel 718, Rene 95, CMSX-4) is the **$\gamma'$ precipitate** — a coherent, ordered L1$_2$ (Ni$_3$(Al,Ti)) phase occupying **up to 70% volume fraction**. Dislocations must **cut through or bow around** these precipitates, dramatically reducing climb-controlled creep. The addition of **1 wt% Mo or W** to superalloys provides solid-solution strengthening that is stable to within 50 °C of the melting point. This is why modern jet engine HP blades can operate at gas-path temperatures of $1600\ ^\circ C$ with metal temperatures of $1150\ ^\circ C$ — a $0.78\,T_m$ regime that would have been impossible 60 years ago.

---

### **Final Study Tip for PCIET205 Module 2**

> [!TIP]
> Before entering the exam hall, ensure you can draw **from memory**:
> 1. The full S-N curve (log-log, with all four points A-B-C-D, two asymptote lines for ferr/nonferr).
> 2. The full creep curve ($\varepsilon$ vs $t$, with the three stages clearly labelled and the rupture point).
> 3. The Marin factor multiplicative chain ($S_e$ derivation).
> 4. Norton's law equation with **units checked**.
> 5. The Goodman line in the Haigh plane.
>
> These five visuals, drawn first in your answer booklet, set the tone for the examiner and unlock 2–3 bonus marks from "presentation quality."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Rolling, Types of Rolling Mills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Rolling, Types of Rolling Mills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Rolling

**Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the thickness of a workpiece (called the *stock*) is reduced by compressive forces exerted by two opposing work rolls rotating in opposite directions. The material is *drawn into the roll gap* by frictional forces between the roll surface and the workpiece, and it undergoes *plastic deformation* to emerge as a thinner, elongated product with a modified cross-section.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

> [!NOTE]
> **Rolling** is a *continuous, steady-state, bulk deformation process* in which a metallic workpiece is plastically compressed between two counter-rotating cylindrical (or shaped) rolls to reduce its cross-sectional area, elongate its length, and improve its mechanical/metallurgical properties.

The process is classified under **primary forming / hot working** operations when performed above the recrystallization temperature, and under **secondary forming / cold working** operations when performed below it.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Dough Roller

Imagine flattening chapati dough with a rolling pin on a kitchen counter:

- You push the rolling pin forward; the **friction** between the pin and dough *drags* the dough into the narrowing gap.
- The dough emerges **thinner, longer, and wider** (or with controlled width if the pin is constrained).
- If you press *too hard* in a single pass, the dough tears or sticks — so you do it in **multiple passes**, exactly like industrial rolling of steel plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: The rolls do NOT push the metal in. Friction *drags* the metal in. This is why the **coefficient of friction (μ)** dictates the **maximum possible reduction per pass** — a recurring KTU question theme.

### 1.3 Stock, Draft, Elongation, and Spread — Standard KTU Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|--------|------------|---------------|
| Initial thickness | $h_0$ | Thickness entering the rolls | 10–250 mm (hot) |
| Final thickness | $h_f$ | Thickness leaving the rolls | 1–100 mm |
| **Draft (Reduction)** | $\Delta h$ | $h_0 - h_f$ | 2–50 mm/pass |
| Coefficient of friction | $\mu$ | Between roll and stock | 0.05–0.4 |
| Roll radius | $R$ | Working roll radius | 100–800 mm |
| Roll speed | $N$ | Rotational speed | 30–1500 rpm |
| Stock width | $w$ | Lateral dimension | Constant (idealized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU theory questions, **roll separating force (F)**, **torque (T)**, and **power (P)** are the three most-asked parameters — every derivation flows from $\Delta h$, $\mu$, and $R$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Rolling — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Stock Entry**: The workpiece (billet, slab, or sheet) is fed into the roll gap either **manually, by pusher, or by preceding stand**.
2. **Bite & Draw-In**: Frictional force developed at the contact arc pulls the stock into the roll gap. For bite to occur, $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is the **angle of bite**).
3. **Plastic Deformation Zone**: Between the entry and exit planes, the material undergoes compression in the rolling direction (thickness reduction) and elongation in the longitudinal direction (length increase). Width is assumed *constant* in plane-strain analysis.
4. **Neutral Point (No-Slip Line)**: A point on the contact arc where the **horizontal velocity of the roll surface equals the material velocity**. To the entry side, the roll moves *slower* than the material (forward slip of material over roll), and friction acts to drag the material in. To the exit side, the roll moves *faster* than the material (forward slip of roll over material), and friction acts backward.
5. **Exit & Forward Slip**: The material exits the rolls at a velocity $V_f$ slightly greater than the roll surface velocity $V_r$ (because at the exit, the roll is moving faster than the average material velocity in the deformation zone). This is called **forward slip**, defined as:
   $$s = \frac{V_f - V_r}{V_r}$$
6. **Elastic Recovery (Springback)**: After the rolls, the material elastically recovers a small amount, slightly increasing the final thickness above $h_f$ inside the rolls. Neglected in elementary KTU analysis.

### 2.2 The Three Critical Angles in Rolling

- **Angle of Bite (α)**: Angle subtended at the roll center by the contact arc from entry to exit.
- **Angle of Contact (α)**: Same as angle of bite (often used interchangeably in KTU textbooks).
- **Neutral Angle (γ)**: Angle measured from the roll center to the neutral (no-slip) point.
- **Exit Angle**: Always zero by definition (reference line).

### 2.3 Why Width is Assumed Constant — The Plane-Strain Assumption

In hot and cold rolling of wide strips, **lateral spread is negligible** because the width-to-thickness ratio is very high ($w/h > 10$). This justifies the **plane-strain** assumption used in KTU derivations: $\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_z = 0$ where $x$ = rolling direction, $y$ = thickness, $z$ = width.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Angle of bite | $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$ | rad | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$ |
| 2 | Maximum reduction | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ | mm | Condition for bite: $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$ |
| 3 | Contact length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | Approximate (small $\alpha$); exact: $L = R \sin\alpha$ |
| 4 | Neutral angle | $\tan\gamma = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan\alpha$ or $\gamma \approx \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | rad | First-order approximation |
| 5 | Forward slip | $s = \dfrac{V_f - V_r}{V_r}$ | — | Dimensionless, typically 0.02–0.10 |
| 6 | Roll surface velocity | $V_r = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D = 2R$ |
| 7 | Projected contact area | $A_p = L \cdot w$ | mm² | $w$ = width |
| 8 | **Roll separating force** | $F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot A_p = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | N | $\bar{\sigma}_f$ = mean flow stress |
| 9 | **Mean flow stress** | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{\sigma_0 + \sigma_f}{2}$ (approx) | MPa | Or use average $\bar{Y}$ from true stress-strain |
| 10 | **Torque (two-high mill)** | $T = 2 F L$ (approx: force × arm) | N·m | Better: $T = 2 F a$, where $a \approx 0.5 L$ |
| 11 | **Power** | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 12 | Torque (with lever arm) | $T = 2 F \cdot (L/2) = F L$ | N·m | Each roll contributes $F \times (L/2)$ |
| 13 | Rolling power | $P = 2 F L V_r$ | W | Alternative: $P = T \omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: The most-asked numerical problem combines formulas (1) → (3) → (8) → (11). You compute $\alpha$ first, then $L$, then $F$, then $P$. Always state units in the final answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Industry**: Hot strip mills, plate mills, structural mills, and cold rolling mills for automobile and appliance sheet.
- **Aluminum Industry**: Continuous casting + rolling for can-stock, foil (down to 6 μm after many passes).
- **Railway & Structural Sections**: Rolling of I-beams, channels, rails, and T-sections in shaped roll passes.
- **Coinage**: Cold rolling of coin blanks.
- **Cost optimization**: Power and torque calculations dictate motor selection, drive sizing, and mill stand rigidity in equipment design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Angle of Bite Formula

**Given**: A roll of radius $R$ takes a bite of thickness $h_0$ and reduces it to $h_f$, with the entry and exit points on the roll circle.

**Setup**: Place the origin at the roll center. The entry and exit points both lie on the roll surface of radius $R$. Let the roll center be at vertical distance $R$ above the exit plane (which is horizontal). The entry point is at height $\dfrac{h_0 + h_f}{2}$ above the exit plane.

**Step 1**: At the entry point, the vertical distance from the roll center is:
$$y_{entry} = R - \frac{h_0 + h_f}{2}$$

**Step 2**: The horizontal distance from the roll center to the entry point (and the exit point, by symmetry of the bite on each side) is:
$$x = \sqrt{R^2 - y_{entry}^2}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the Pythagorean theorem:
$$x^2 = R^2 - \left(R - \frac{h_0 + h_f}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4**: Expand the bracket:
$$x^2 = R^2 - R^2 + 2R\left(\frac{h_0 + h_f}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{h_0 + h_f}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 5**: Simplify — the $R^2$ cancels:
$$x^2 = R(h_0 + h_f) - \frac{(h_0 + h_f)^2}{4}$$

**Step 6**: For the contact length $L$ (projected horizontal length of the arc), and noting that the total horizontal distance covered by the deformed material is $\dfrac{h_0 - h_f}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{2}$ on each side:
$$L = \sqrt{R(h_0 + h_f) - \frac{(h_0 + h_f)^2}{4} - \frac{\Delta h^2}{4}} \quad \text{(after algebraic simplification)}$$

**Step 7**: A more elegant geometric route. Consider the right triangle formed by the roll center, the exit point, and the entry point. The angle at the roll center between these two radii is the angle of bite $\alpha$. The vertical distance from the entry plane to the exit plane (over one side) is $\dfrac{h_0 - h_f}{2}$. So:
$$\cos\alpha = \frac{R - \frac{h_0 - h_f}{2}}{R} = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}$$

> **Correction Note**: Using the standard textbook approach where $\Delta h$ is defined as the total reduction and the geometry takes both sides into account, the cleanest textbook form is:
$$\boxed{\cos\alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$

where $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$.

**Step 8**: Solving for the angle:
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$$

> **Why this is a high-yield KTU formula**: It links geometry, material, and friction (via the next step).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Reduction (Bite Condition)

**Given**: For the roll to "bite" the material, the friction force component along the rolling direction must exceed or equal the horizontal component of the radial force.

**Step 1**: At the entry point, the roll surface exerts a normal force $N$ (radial) and a frictional force $\mu N$ (tangential). Decomposing into horizontal (rolling direction) components:

- Radial (normal) force horizontal component: $N \sin\alpha$ (resists entry)
- Friction force horizontal component: $\mu N \cos\alpha$ (aids entry)

**Step 2**: For the roll to draw the material in, the friction must overcome the radial component:
$$\mu N \cos\alpha \geq N \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3**: Cancel $N$ (non-zero):
$$\mu \geq \tan\alpha$$

**Step 4**: The smallest possible $\alpha$ for which bite occurs is when $\mu = \tan\alpha$. Substitute $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$ and use the identity $\tan^2\alpha = \dfrac{1 - \cos^2\alpha}{\cos^2\alpha}$:

$$\mu^2 = \tan^2\alpha = \frac{1 - \left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)^2}{\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5**: For maximum reduction, $\Delta h \ll R$, so $\cos\alpha \approx 1$. Under this small-angle approximation:
$$\mu^2 \approx \frac{2 \frac{\Delta h}{R} - \left(\frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)^2}{1} \approx \frac{2 \Delta h}{R}$$

**Step 6**: For the *upper bound* (worst case, where $\cos\alpha$ is small), the classical result is:
$$\boxed{\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R}$$

**Step 7**: Equivalently, the maximum bite angle is:
$$\alpha_{max} = \tan^{-1}\mu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Golden Rule**: The maximum possible reduction per pass equals $\mu^2 R$. If the given $\Delta h$ exceeds this, the roll will not bite — and the student must state this in the answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Neutral Angle

**Given**: The neutral point is the location on the contact arc where the **horizontal velocity of the roll surface** equals the **velocity of the material** (no relative slip along the rolling direction).

**Step 1**: The roll surface velocity at any point on the contact arc at angle $\theta$ (measured from the exit horizontal) is $V_{roll} = \omega R$, but its **horizontal component** is:
$$V_{h}(\theta) = \omega R \cos\theta$$

**Step 2**: At the entry ($\theta = \alpha$): $V_h = \omega R \cos\alpha$ — *slower* than the material (roll drags material in by friction).
At the exit ($\theta = 0$): $V_h = \omega R$ — *faster* than the material.

**Step 3**: At the neutral point ($\theta = \gamma$): $V_h = V_{material}$:
$$V_{material} = \omega R \cos\gamma$$

**Step 4**: By continuity of volume flow (mass conservation in steady rolling):
$$V_{material}(\gamma) \cdot h(\gamma) = V_f \cdot h_f$$

For a typical analysis, one can also argue that the velocity in the deformation zone increases from $V_0$ at entry to $V_f$ at exit. The condition for no slip defines the neutral point.

**Step 5**: A more accurate derivation uses force balance. Consider a thin element at angle $\theta$. The horizontal force per unit width from the rolls, integrated from the entry to the neutral point, must be zero (because the material is in steady-state equilibrium in that sub-region):

$$\int_{\gamma}^{\alpha} (p \cos\theta - \mu p \sin\theta) R \, d\theta = 0$$

Wait — the friction sign changes at the neutral point. The above integration is the wrong sign convention. Re-derive:

On the **entry side** of the neutral point (from $\theta = \gamma$ to $\theta = \alpha$):
- Pressure pushes material in: $+p \cos\theta$ (force toward the exit)
- Friction acts backward (roll moves slower than material, so friction on material is backward): $-\mu p \sin\theta$... 

Actually, on the entry side, the *roll* moves slower than the material horizontally. Friction on the material from the roll is therefore **opposite to the rolling direction** (it tries to slow the material). Hmm, but the material IS being pulled in...

Let me re-examine: Friction on the material from the roll acts in the direction of relative motion of the roll surface with respect to the material. On the entry side, the material moves *faster* than the roll surface in the rolling direction, so friction on the material is in the **rolling direction** (forward, pulling it in). On the exit side, the roll surface moves faster than the material, so friction is in the **anti-rolling direction** (backward).

**Step 5 (Corrected)**: Horizontal force balance over the entire contact arc must be zero (steady state). The horizontal component of normal pressure must equal the horizontal component of friction:

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha} p \cos\theta \, d\theta = \int_{0}^{\alpha} \mu p \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 6**: For a uniform pressure distribution (simplified assumption), $p = p_0$ (constant):
$$p_0 \int_{0}^{\alpha} \cos\theta \, d\theta = \mu p_0 \int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 7**: Integrating:
$$p_0 [\sin\theta]_{0}^{\alpha} = \mu p_0 [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\alpha}$$
$$p_0 \sin\alpha = \mu p_0 (1 - \cos\alpha)$$
$$\sin\alpha = \mu (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 8**: Using the half-angle identity $\sin\alpha = 2 \sin(\alpha/2)\cos(\alpha/2)$ and $1 - \cos\alpha = 2 \sin^2(\alpha/2)$:
$$2 \sin(\alpha/2) \cos(\alpha/2) = 2\mu \sin^2(\alpha/2)$$
$$\cos(\alpha/2) = \mu \sin(\alpha/2)$$
$$\mu = \cot(\alpha/2)$$
$$\tan(\alpha/2) = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

This is the **maximum bite condition** — confirms the previous derivation.

**Step 9**: For the **neutral point** specifically, the friction has zero net effect from entry to neutral (the material accelerates uniformly). Applying the same logic to the sub-arc from entry to neutral:
$$\sin(\alpha - \gamma) = \mu (\cos\gamma - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 10**: Using the small-angle approximation (valid for typical rolling):
$$\alpha - \gamma \approx \frac{\mu (\cos\gamma - \cos\alpha)}{1} \approx \frac{\mu}{2}(\alpha^2 - \gamma^2)$$

For small angles, this simplifies to:
$$\boxed{\gamma \approx \frac{\alpha}{2}}$$

Or, more accurately:
$$\tan\gamma = \frac{1}{2}\tan\alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the approximation $\gamma = \alpha/2$ is used unless the problem specifically states otherwise. Always state the assumption.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Roll Separating Force

**Step 1**: The total roll separating force $F$ is the integral of the radial (normal) pressure $p(\theta)$ acting on the roll surface, projected onto the vertical direction, integrated over the contact arc and width.

$$F = w \int_{0}^{\alpha} p(\theta) \cos\theta \, R \, d\theta$$

**Step 2**: Under the **simplifying assumption of uniform pressure distribution** (acceptable for KTU):
$$F = w \int_{0}^{\alpha} \bar{p} \cos\theta \, R \, d\theta = \bar{p} R w [\sin\theta]_{0}^{\alpha} = \bar{p} R w \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3**: For small $\alpha$, $\sin\alpha \approx \alpha \approx \sqrt{2\Delta h/R}$ (from $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/R$ and the small-angle approximation $\sin^2\alpha \approx 1 - \cos^2\alpha \approx 2\Delta h/R$).

**Step 4**: Substituting $\sin\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h/R}$ and defining $L = R\sin\alpha \approx \sqrt{R\Delta h}$:
$$F \approx \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L = \bar{p} \cdot A_p$$

**Step 5**: Recognizing that $\bar{p}$ is the mean flow stress $\bar{\sigma}_f$ (resistance of the material to plastic deformation under the rolling conditions), we obtain the **fundamental KTU formula**:
$$\boxed{F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w}$$

where:
- $\bar{\sigma}_f$ = mean flow stress (MPa) = $\dfrac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2}$ (approximate, with $Y_0$ and $Y_f$ being yield stresses at the entry and exit strains, accounting for strain hardening).
- $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ = contact length (mm)
- $w$ = width of the stock (mm)

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Torque and Power

**Step 1**: The torque on one roll equals the integral of the moment arm (perpendicular distance from the roll axis) times the tangential friction force.

**Step 2**: For a simplified uniform pressure model, the torque is:
$$T_{one\,roll} = \mu F R$$

Wait — this is the torque from friction. The *net* torque (which drives the mill motor) is the difference between the torque supplied at the roll surface and the resistance from the material. In a motor-driven mill, the *motor torque* equals the work done on the material, which equals $F \times L$ (force × distance the material moves through the deformation zone) divided by the angular displacement.

**Step 3 (KTU Standard Approach)**: Each roll exerts a force $F$ on the material. The horizontal projection of this force does work as the material moves a distance $L$ through the deformation zone. Equating work per unit time:
$$P_{rolling} = F \cdot V_r$$

Wait — this gives the *net* rolling power, but is dimensionally correct only if $V_r$ is the material velocity. Let me redo:

**Step 3 (Cleaner)**: Torque on a roll = (force) × (lever arm) = $F \times (L/2)$, because the resultant force $F$ acts at the centroid of the pressure distribution, which is approximately at the midpoint of the projected contact area, i.e., at a horizontal distance of $L/2$ from the roll center's vertical line.

$$T_{one\,roll} = F \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 4**: For a **two-high mill** (two powered rolls):
$$T = 2 \cdot F \cdot \frac{L}{2} = F \cdot L$$

**Step 5**: Power (each roll rotating at $N$ rpm, angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N/60$):
$$P = T \omega = F \cdot L \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Or equivalently:
$$P = 2 F L N \, \text{(in appropriate units: F in N, L in m, N in rev/s)}$$

For KTU, the most common form is:
$$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power units in KTU**: If $F$ is in N, $L$ in m, and $N$ in rpm, then $P$ comes out in **watts (W)**. Convert to **kW** by dividing by 1000. Always check units.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Workflow

| Step | What to Find | Formula Used | Output Unit |
|------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | Angle of bite | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}(1 - \Delta h/R)$ | radians or degrees |
| 2 | Check bite feasibility | Compare $\alpha$ with $\tan^{-1}\mu$ | — |
| 3 | Contact length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm |
| 4 | Roll velocity | $V_r = \pi D N/60$ | m/s |
| 5 | Neutral angle | $\gamma = \alpha/2$ | radians |
| 6 | Mean flow stress | $\bar{\sigma}_f = (Y_0 + Y_f)/2$ | MPa |
| 7 | **Separating force** | $F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | N |
| 8 | **Torque** | $T = F L$ (two-high) | N·m |
| 9 | **Power** | $P = 2\pi N T/60$ | W |

### 4.4 Types of Rolling Mills — Detailed Functional Description

| Mill Type | Roll Configuration | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Two-High Reversing** | Two rolls, direction reversed | Same rolls used for each pass | Plate mills, blooming mills |
| **Two-High Non-Reversing** | Two rolls, one direction | Stock passes through once | Continuous roughing mills |
| **Three-High** | Three rolls stacked vertically | Stock passes back and forth | Section mills (rails, beams) |
| **Four-High** | Two small work rolls + two large backup | Small work roll diameter → high $L/R$ ratio | Cold rolling of thin sheets |
| **Cluster Mill (6-roll)** | Multiple backup rolls around work rolls | Very high rolling force capability | Thin foil, precision strips |
| **Sendzimir (12-roll / 20-roll)** | Cluster arrangement with intermediate rolls | Extremely flat, thin product with high accuracy | Stainless steel foil |
| **Continuous / Tandem** | Multiple stands in series | Each stand reduces progressively | Hot strip mills, cold tandem mills |
| **Planetary Mill** | Many small work rolls around backup | Very large single-pass reduction | Direct strip casting from slabs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why smaller work rolls in 4-high mills?**
> The contact length $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$. For a given $\Delta h$, **smaller R gives smaller L** → **smaller separating force** → lower motor power. But smaller rolls deflect more, hence the need for **backup rolls** to provide rigidity.
