## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the terms (i) Angle of bite, (ii) Forward slip, and (iii) Draft as applied to a rolling process.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Angle of Bite ($\alpha$)**: It is the angle subtended at the roll center by the arc of contact between the roll and the stock, measured from the entry point to the exit point. Mathematically, $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$.

(ii) **Forward Slip ($s$)**: It is the ratio of the difference between the exit velocity of the stock and the roll surface velocity to the roll surface velocity, expressed as $s = \dfrac{V_f - V_r}{V_r}$. Typically, $s = 0.02$ to $0.10$ in industrial rolling.

(iii) **Draft ($\Delta h$)**: It is the difference between the initial and final thickness of the stock in a single pass, $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$. It represents the *amount of reduction* per pass.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State the condition for a roll to "bite" the workpiece and derive the expression for maximum reduction per pass.**

**Model Answer:**

**Condition for Bite**: For the rolls to draw the stock into the roll gap, the frictional force component along the rolling direction must be at least equal to the horizontal component of the radial force at the entry point. This gives:
$$\mu \geq \tan\alpha$$

**Derivation of Maximum Reduction**: Substituting $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$ and using $\tan\alpha = \mu$ at the limiting condition, with small-angle approximation, the classical result is:
$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$$

This represents the largest reduction per pass achievable for a given roll radius and coefficient of friction. If $\Delta h > \mu^2 R$, the roll will fail to bite and rolling is not possible.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** — **Question A (14 Marks)**

A 300 mm diameter rolling mill takes a strip of initial thickness 25 mm to a final thickness of 20 mm in one pass at a roll speed of 50 rpm. The coefficient of friction is 0.10, and the mean flow stress of the material is 200 MPa. The strip width is 200 mm.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The angle of bite, contact length, and verify whether the roll can bite the material. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The roll separating force, torque, and power required for the rolling operation. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Angle of Bite, Contact Length, and Bite Verification

**Given Data:**
- Roll diameter $D = 300$ mm → Roll radius $R = 150$ mm
- Initial thickness $h_0 = 25$ mm
- Final thickness $h_f = 20$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$
- Roll speed $N = 50$ rpm
- Mean flow stress $\bar{\sigma}_f = 200$ MPa $= 200$ N/mm²
- Strip width $w = 200$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate the Draft (Reduction)**
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Angle of Bite**
$$\cos\alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = 1 - \frac{5}{150} = 1 - 0.0333 = 0.9667$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.9667) = 14.89^\circ \text{ (approximately 0.260 rad)}$$

**Step 3: Check the Bite Condition**
$$\tan^{-1}\mu = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71^\circ$$
$$\alpha = 14.89^\circ \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tan^{-1}\mu = 5.71^\circ$$

Since $\alpha = 14.89^\circ > 5.71^\circ = \tan^{-1}\mu$, the angle of bite **exceeds** the maximum allowable angle.

**Wait — this is wrong! The bite condition requires $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$ for the roll to bite. Here $\alpha > \tan^{-1}\mu$, so the roll CANNOT bite!**

**Conclusion**: The roll will not bite the workpiece. The student must either reduce $\Delta h$ (smaller reduction per pass), increase $\mu$ (e.g., by roughening the rolls), or use a larger roll radius. The maximum possible reduction is:
$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R = (0.10)^2 \times 150 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

Since the required $\Delta h = 5$ mm $>$ $\Delta h_{max} = 1.5$ mm, **multiple passes** are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Many students compute the angle of bite but **fail to check the bite condition**. If $\alpha > \tan^{-1}\mu$, the process is infeasible as a single pass. Marks are deducted for not flagging this.

**Step 4: Contact Length (for theoretical record, even if bite fails)**
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{150 \times 5} = \sqrt{750} = 27.39 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (b) — Separating Force, Torque, and Power (Assuming corrected reduction)

**Corrected Approach**: Use the maximum feasible reduction $\Delta h = 1.5$ mm. Alternatively, the problem expects the student to proceed with the given data but note the infeasibility. We will compute for the given data assuming $\mu$ is increased or multiple passes are used (i.e., the formula remains valid for one pass of 5 mm reduction in a properly set up mill).

**Step 1: Roll Separating Force**
$$F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w = 200 \times 27.39 \times 200 = 1,095,600 \text{ N} = 1095.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Torque (Two-High Mill)**
$$T = F \cdot L = 1,095,600 \times 0.02739 \text{ (m)} = 30,008 \text{ N·m} \approx 30 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Power**
$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 50 \times 30,008}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 30,008}{60}$$
$$P = \frac{9,425,000}{60} \approx 157,080 \text{ W} \approx 157.1 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Correct Approach**: If the examiner accepts the data as given and expects force, torque, and power calculations without verifying the bite condition, the answers above are correct. The bite condition is an *additional* check, and marking is based on whether the student mentions the infeasibility. KTU usually awards **partial credit** for noting the infeasibility.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** — **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **four-high rolling mill** and a **Sendzimir mill**. Compare their applications and the rationale for using backup rolls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hot rolling mill has rolls of 500 mm diameter operating at 30 rpm on a slab of 100 mm initial thickness, reducing it to 75 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.20. Calculate:
- (i) The angle of bite and check bite feasibility
- (ii) The contact length and roll separating force, given mean flow stress $= 150$ MPa and width $= 500$ mm
- (iii) The rolling power required. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Four-High and Sendzimir Mills

**Four-High Rolling Mill**:
- Configuration: Two **small work rolls** (of small diameter) in contact with the stock, backed by two **large backup rolls** that support the work rolls against bending.
- **Working Principle**: The stock passes between the two work rolls. The small diameter of the work rolls means that, for a given reduction $\Delta h$, the contact length $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ is smaller, hence the roll separating force is lower. However, small rolls are prone to bending, and the backup rolls provide the required rigidity.
- **Application**: Cold rolling of thin sheets and strips, particularly in the final finishing stands where thickness accuracy is critical.

**Sendzimir Mill (Z-Mill / Cluster Mill)**:
- Configuration: Two work rolls of very small diameter, supported by a cluster of intermediate rolls and backup rolls arranged in a planetary fashion. Typically has 12, 20, or more rolls in total.
- **Working Principle**: The cluster arrangement provides enormous rigidity to the small work rolls, allowing very high rolling forces with minimal roll deflection. The strip can be reduced to very thin gauges (down to 0.002 mm foils) with excellent flatness.
- **Application**: Cold rolling of stainless steel, silicon steel, and other high-strength thin foils.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Four-High | Sendzimir |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Number of rolls | 4 | 12–20 |
| Work roll diameter | 50–150 mm | 5–25 mm |
| Backup rigidity | Moderate | Very high |
| Product thickness | 0.1–3 mm | 0.002–0.5 mm |
| Typical products | Sheet steel, aluminum | Stainless foil, silicon steel |
| Cost | Moderate | Very high |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Roll diameter $D = 500$ mm → $R = 250$ mm
- $h_0 = 100$ mm, $h_f = 75$ mm
- $N = 30$ rpm
- $\mu = 0.20$
- $\bar{\sigma}_f = 150$ MPa $= 150$ N/mm²
- $w = 500$ mm

**Step 1: Draft**
$$\Delta h = 100 - 75 = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (i): Angle of Bite and Bite Check**
$$\cos\alpha = 1 - \frac{25}{250} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.90) = 25.84^\circ$$

Bite check: $\tan^{-1}\mu = \tan^{-1}(0.20) = 11.31^\circ$. Since $\alpha = 25.84^\circ > 11.31^\circ$, the roll **cannot bite** in a single pass. (This is a real-world scenario; in practice, the pass is divided or surface conditions improved.)

**Maximum feasible reduction per pass:**
$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R = (0.20)^2 \times 250 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

So 25 mm must be achieved over multiple passes.

**Step 3 (ii): Contact Length and Separating Force** (for $\Delta h = 25$ mm as per problem statement)
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 25} = \sqrt{6250} = 79.06 \text{ mm}$$

$$F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w = 150 \times 79.06 \times 500 = 5,929,500 \text{ N} \approx 5930 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 (iii): Rolling Power**
$$T = F \cdot L = 5,929,500 \times 0.07906 = 468,786 \text{ N·m} \approx 468.8 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 30 \times 468,786}{60} = \frac{88,353,000}{60} = 1,472,550 \text{ W} \approx 1473 \text{ kW} \approx 1.47 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Most Common Mistake)**:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: $L$ must be in meters when computing $P$ from $T = FL$ in N·m. Many students leave $L$ in mm and get a power value 1000× too large.
> 2. **Confusing roll separating force with rolling force**: Roll separating force is the *vertical* force on the roll bearings; it is what we compute as $F = \bar{\sigma}_f L w$. The *rolling force* (tangential component driving torque) is related but smaller.
> 3. **Using $T = FL$ without the factor of 2**: For a two-high mill, each roll contributes $F(L/2)$, so total $T = FL$. Using $T = 2FL$ gives double the correct answer.
> 4. **Power formula units**: $P = 2\pi NT/60$ requires $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m to yield $P$ in watts.
> 5. **Not mentioning bite infeasibility**: If the problem gives parameters that violate $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$, ALWAYS mention it. KTU awards 1–2 marks for this observation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Rolling & Types of Rolling Mills**

- **Rolling** is a *continuous bulk deformation process* using counter-rotating rolls; friction **drags** the stock in (does not push it in).
- **Angle of Bite ($\alpha$)**: Given by $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$. Always in **radians or degrees**, not mm.
- **Bite Condition**: $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$. If violated, the roll will not grip the stock.
- **Maximum Reduction Per Pass**: $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$. Increases with roll radius and friction.
- **Contact Length**: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ (approximate, valid for small $\alpha$); exact is $L = R \sin\alpha$.
- **Neutral Angle**: $\gamma \approx \alpha/2$. The point where roll surface velocity equals material velocity.
- **Forward Slip**: $s = (V_f - V_r)/V_r$. Positive because $V_f > V_r$ at exit.
- **Roll Separating Force**: $F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ — the single most-asked formula in KTU.
- **Mean Flow Stress**: $\bar{\sigma}_f = (Y_0 + Y_f)/2$ for a linear work-hardening material; in KTU problems, often given directly.
- **Torque (Two-High)**: $T = F L$. Some textbooks use $T = 0.5 FL$ per roll (factor of 2 total = $FL$).
- **Power**: $P = 2\pi NT/60$ in watts when $N$ is in rpm and $T$ in N·m.
- **Plane Strain Assumption**: Width is constant; $\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_z = 0$.
- **Hot Rolling**: Above recrystallization temperature (~$0.6 T_m$); no work hardening retained; lower flow stress; larger $\Delta h$ per pass.
- **Cold Rolling**: Below recrystallization; work hardening retained; better surface finish and dimensional accuracy; lower reduction per pass.
- **Two-High Mill**: Simplest, used for roughing and plates.
- **Three-High Mill**: Stock passes back and forth; efficient for section rolling.
- **Four-High Mill**: Two work rolls + two backup rolls; used for cold rolling sheets.
- **Cluster/Sendzimir Mill**: Many backup rolls around small work rolls; for very thin foils and high precision.
- **Continuous/Tandem Mill**: Multiple stands in series; high productivity for strip and sheet.
- **Roll Flattening (Elastic)**: In reality, rolls flatten slightly, increasing $L$ and hence $F$. KTU basic problems ignore this.
- **Neutral Point Significance**: Defines the location of peak roll pressure and is critical for forward-slip and friction analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy**: For a 14-mark question, present the formula with derivation (3 marks), substitute values with units (3 marks), and compute stepwise (3 marks), then state the final answer with units (2 marks). A well-labeled diagram earns 2–3 extra marks. Always check the bite condition — it's a 1–2 mark "free" point that most students miss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Rolling Processes and Applications: Hot Rolling, Cold Rolling, Ring Rolling, Thread Rolling, Tube Rolling, Shape Rolling, Skew Rolling, Defects in Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Various Rolling Processes and Applications: Hot Rolling, Cold Rolling, Ring Rolling, Thread Rolling, Tube Rolling, Shape Rolling, Skew Rolling, Defects in Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rolling? (Foundational Definition)

**Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the workpiece (typically a thick slab, billet, or ingot) is passed through one or more pairs of **rotating cylindrical rollers** to reduce its cross-sectional area and/or alter its shape. It is one of the most important **bulk deformation processes** in manufacturing engineering, accounting for the majority of steel and non-ferrous metal production worldwide.

Mathematically, the process reduces the thickness from an initial value $t_0$ to a final value $t_f$, where $t_f < t_0$. The rollers rotate in opposite directions, drawing the workpiece into the gap (called the **roll gap** or **bite**) by means of **frictional forces** between the roll surface and the workpiece surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCIET205 Module 3):** Rolling is classified as a **primary metal forming process** under the broader category of *Mechanical Working of Metals*. Students must understand both the **mechanics of deformation** and the **industrial application** of each variant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dough and Pasta Machine"

Imagine you are making **chapati/roti** or rolling out **pasta dough** at home with a wooden rolling pin. As you press and push the pin forward, the dough becomes thinner and longer. Rolling in manufacturing is fundamentally the same idea — only on an industrial scale, with **steel rollers** driven by massive electric motors (often 1,000–10,000 kW), processing slabs up to 250 mm thick and 2.5 m wide at temperatures exceeding **1,200 °C**.

The key idea: **pressure + friction** converts a thick piece into a thin, elongated piece — without removing any material (no chips, no waste).

## 1.3 Classification of Rolling Processes (The Big Picture)

| Family | Sub-Process | Temperature Regime | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Temperature** | Hot Rolling | Above recrystallization temp (~0.6 × T_m) | Slabs, blooms, billets |
| | Cold Rolling | Below recrystallization temp | Sheets, strips, foils |
| **By Product Geometry** | Flat Rolling | Any | Plates, sheets, strips |
| | Shape Rolling | Any | I-beams, rails, channels |
| **By Specialized Geometry** | Ring Rolling | Hot/Cold | Flanges, bearing races |
| | Thread Rolling | Cold | Fasteners, screws |
| | Tube Rolling | Hot/Cold | Seamless tubes/pipes |
| | Skew Rolling | Hot | Balls, cross-rolling mills |

> [!NOTE]
> **Recrystallization Temperature** is the temperature above which new strain-free grains nucleate and grow, replacing the deformed grains. For low-carbon steel, this is approximately **450 °C to 550 °C**, but hot rolling is typically done at **900 °C to 1,250 °C** for ease of deformation.

## 1.4 The Seven Variants — One-Line Definitions

1. **Hot Rolling** — Rolling performed above the recrystallization temperature of the metal.
2. **Cold Rolling** — Rolling performed below the recrystallization temperature.
3. **Ring Rolling** — Rolling where a ring-shaped blank is deformed between an **idler roll** and a **main roll** to increase its diameter and reduce its wall thickness.
4. **Thread Rolling** — Cold forming process where threads are produced by rolling flat or cylindrical blanks between hardened dies (flat dies or cylindrical dies).
5. **Tube Rolling** — A process for producing **seamless tubes** by piercing a solid billet and elongating it through a series of rolls (e.g., Mannesmann mill).
6. **Shape Rolling** — Rolling through rolls with **grooved profiles** to produce long products with complex cross-sections (I-beams, T-sections, rails).
7. **Skew Rolling** — Rolling where the rolls are set at an **angle** to the workpiece axis, used for producing balls, rollers, and bearing components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hot Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.1.1 Definition
Hot rolling is performed at temperatures **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal. For steel, this means above approximately **0.6 × T_melting (in Kelvin)**. Typical hot-rolling temperatures for steel are between **900 °C and 1,250 °C**.

### 2.1.2 Why Hot Roll?
- **Lower yield strength** → Less roll force required.
- **Higher ductility** → Large reductions per pass (20–40%).
- **No work hardening** → Continuous deformation possible; recrystallization wipes out strain.
- **Breakdown of cast structure** → Refines grains and closes porosity.

### 2.1.3 Process Steps
- **Heating:** Ingot/slab is heated in a **reheating furnace** (pusher-type, walking-beam, or rotary hearth).
- **Descaling:** High-pressure water jets (≈ 100–150 bar) remove **mill scale** (FeO/Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄) before each pass.
- **Primary/ Roughing Mill:** Reduces thick slabs (~250 mm) to intermediate sections (~25–50 mm) in 5–9 passes.
- **Intermediate/Finishing Mill:** Further reduces to final gauge (2–25 mm).
- **Coiling:** The finished strip is coiled at ~600 °C into **hot-rolled coils**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mill Scale** is a hard, brittle iron-oxide layer formed during heating. It must be removed to prevent (a) surface defects, (b) roll wear, and (c) inclusions being pressed into the surface.

### 2.1.4 Key Parameters in Hot Rolling
- **Draft per pass (Δh):** Reduction in thickness = $t_0 - t_f$.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ):** 0.2–0.5 in hot rolling (lubrication is rarely used).
- **Bite angle (α):** $\alpha = \arccos\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}\right) \approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$ for small drafts.
- **Contact length (L):** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ (approximate, for small drafts).
- **Projected contact area (A):** $A = w \cdot L$, where $w$ is the width of the strip.

### 2.1.5 Real-World Application
Hot rolling is used in **integrated steel plants** (e.g., SAIL, Tata Steel, JSW, RINL) to produce **hot-rolled plates, sheets, structural sections, rails, and bars**. It is the first step in nearly all flat-product steel making.

## 2.2 Cold Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Definition
Cold rolling is performed at **room temperature** (or slightly above, but below recrystallization temperature). The starting stock is usually **hot-rolled and pickled** (acid-cleaned to remove scale).

### 2.2.2 Why Cold Roll?
- **Better surface finish** — Surface roughness as low as **0.1–0.4 μm Ra**.
- **Tighter dimensional tolerances** — ±0.025 mm possible.
- **Higher strength** — Strain hardening increases yield strength by **2 to 3 times**.
- **Improved mechanical properties** — Uniform grain elongation, preferred orientation (texture).

### 2.2.3 Process Steps
1. **Pickling** — Hot-rolled coil is passed through **HCl or H₂SO₄** baths to remove scale.
2. **Tandem Cold Rolling Mill** — Typically 4–6 stands in series, each reducing thickness by 20–40%.
3. **Annealing** — Batch or continuous annealing restores ductility.
4. **Temper Rolling / Skin Pass** — Light reduction (0.5–3%) to eliminate yield-point elongation and improve flatness.

### 2.2.4 Key Parameters
- **Small drafts per pass** — 0.1–1.0 mm typical.
- **High coefficient of friction** — μ = 0.05–0.1 (lubrication with rolling oil, e.g., mineral oil + fatty acids).
- **High roll forces** — 5–15 MN per stand.
- **Strain hardening** — Flow stress $\sigma_f$ rises with strain: $\sigma_f = K \epsilon^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Hardening Exponent (n)** for cold-rolled low-carbon steel is typically 0.15–0.25, while for stainless steel it is around 0.04–0.08.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Application
- **Automotive body panels** (sheet steel 0.7–1.0 mm).
- **Tin plate** for food cans (0.15–0.30 mm).
- **Aluminum foil** (down to 6 μm).
- **Electrical steel sheets** for motors and transformers (silicon steel).

## 2.3 Ring Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Definition
Ring rolling is used to increase the **diameter** of a ring-shaped workpiece while reducing its **wall thickness** and (sometimes) **height**. The process uses two rolls:
- **Main/Driving roll** — A large cylindrical roll that rotates the ring.
- **Idler/Pressure roll** — A smaller roll that presses radially inward on the ring wall.
- A **guide roll** controls the axial height.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle
- The ring is placed between the main roll and the idler roll.
- The idler roll moves **radially inward** continuously, squeezing the wall.
- The ring rotates due to friction with the rolls.
- The wall material is pushed **circumferentially**, so the diameter **grows** while the wall **thins**.

### 2.3.3 Key Relationships
- **Volume constancy:** $\pi \cdot D_0 \cdot t_0 \cdot h_0 = \pi \cdot D_f \cdot t_f \cdot h_f$ (assuming no axial change).
- **Diameter growth:** $D_f = D_0 \cdot \left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$ (when $h_0 = h_f$).

### 2.3.4 Real-World Application
- **Railway wheel tires.**
- **Bearing races** (inner and outer).
- **Large-diameter flanges** for pressure vessels.
- **Turbine rings** in jet engines.
- **Nuclear reactor flange rings.**

## 2.4 Thread Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Definition
Thread rolling is a **cold forming process** used to produce external or internal threads by plastically deforming the workpiece between **hardened dies** that have the inverse thread profile machined on them.

### 2.4.2 Types of Thread Rolling
1. **Flat Die Thread Rolling** — Two flat dies reciprocate linearly; one is stationary, the other moves. Used for short threads, wood screws, machine screws.
2. **Cylindrical (Rolled) Die Thread Rolling** — Two or three cylindrical dies with thread profiles rotate around the workpiece. Used for long threads, bolts, studs. Faster production rates.

### 2.4.3 Advantages over Thread Cutting
- **No chips produced** (97–100% material utilization).
- **Higher production rate** (10× faster than cutting).
- **Better surface finish** and **higher fatigue strength** (continuous grain flow along thread profile).
- **Higher tensile strength** due to work hardening.

### 2.4.4 Real-World Application
- **Bolts, screws, studs, threaded rods** (mass production in fastener industries).
- **Wood screws** (spiral thread rolling).
- **Lead screws** in motion-control systems.

## 2.5 Tube Rolling (Seamless Tube Manufacturing) — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Definition
Tube rolling is a process for producing **seamless tubes** from a solid cylindrical billet. The most important industrial method is the **Mannesmann Process**, which uses piercing and elongating rolls.

### 2.5.2 The Mannesmann Process (Cross-Roll Piercing)
- A heated cylindrical billet is fed between two **barrel-shaped rolls** set at the same angle and rotating in the **same direction**.
- The rolls pull the billet forward while simultaneously applying **tensile stresses at the center** (due to geometry of contact).
- A **piercing plug** (mandrel) in the center pierces the billet, creating a hollow shell.

### 2.5.3 The Pilger Mill
- Used for further reduction in tube diameter and wall thickness.
- The tube is fed over a **mandrel** between two long-stroke reciprocating rolls.
- The tube advances and rotates incrementally.

### 2.5.4 Plug Mill and Assel Mill
- **Plug Mill:** A two-roll stand with a stationary plug/mandrel; used for medium-diameter tubes.
- **Assel Mill:** A three-roll mill that produces tubes with a smooth inner surface and close dimensional tolerance.

### 2.5.5 Real-World Application
- **Oil and gas pipelines** (API-grade seamless tubes).
- **Boiler tubes** for power plants.
- **Automotive drive shafts, axle tubes.**
- **Hydraulic cylinder barrels.**
- **Nuclear and chemical process piping.**

## 2.6 Shape Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.6.1 Definition
Shape rolling uses rolls with **grooves of the desired cross-section** to produce long products with constant complex profiles, e.g., **I-beams, channels, rails, T-sections, angle iron**.

### 2.6.2 Process
- A bloom (square cross-section, ~150 × 150 mm) is passed through a series of rolls.
- Each successive stand has a **progressively closer approximation** of the final shape.
- This is called a **"pass schedule"** or **roll pass design**.
- The number of passes can range from 7 to 25 depending on complexity.

### 2.6.3 Pass Design Terminology
- **Bloom** → **Billet** (smaller square) → **Bar** (round/square small) → Final shape.
- **Breakdown pass:** Reduces cross-section.
- **Finishing pass:** Final shape formation.
- **Leader pass / Pinch pass:** Initial gripping of the bar.

### 2.6.4 Real-World Application
- **Structural steel** for construction (I-beams, channels, angles).
- **Rails** for railways (e.g., 60 kg/m rail).
- **Automotive axle beams and chassis components.**

## 2.7 Skew Rolling — Detailed Theory

### 2.7.1 Definition
Skew rolling (also called **cross-rolling** or **oblique rolling**) is a specialized process where the rolls are set at an **angle** to the axis of the workpiece. The rolls rotate and the workpiece rotates and translates along its axis.

### 2.7.2 Working Principle
- The two rolls are set at a **skew angle** (typically 0°–10°) and rotate in the same direction.
- The workpiece is fed axially and rotated by friction.
- Material flows in a complex helical pattern.

### 2.7.3 Applications
1. **Ball Production (Skew Ball Mill)** — Producing **steel balls** for ball bearings (10–125 mm diameter) at extremely high rates (1–10 balls/second).
2. **Cross-Roll Piercing Mill** — Part of the Mannesmann tube-making process.
3. **Roller manufacturing** for rolling mills.
4. **Cross-rolling of bars** for grain refinement.

### 2.7.4 Real-World Application
- **Ball bearing industry** — Millions of steel balls per day.
- **Pre-piercing for seamless tubes.**
- **Aircraft engine shaft components.**

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The following table contains all the critical formulas for rolling calculations. Memorize the formula, units, and the *physical meaning* of each term.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft (Δh) | $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$ | mm | Reduction per pass |
| 2 | Coefficient of friction (μ) | $\mu = \tan(\alpha_n)$ | dimensionless | Coulomb friction |
| 3 | Contact length (L) | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | Length of roll-workpiece arc |
| 4 | Bite angle (α) | $\alpha = \arccos\!\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}\right)$ | radians | Angle subtended at roll center |
| 5 | Neutral angle (αₙ) | $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{2} \arctan(\mu)$ | radians | Angle where velocity is equal |
| 6 | Projected area (A) | $A = w \cdot L$ | mm² | Contact area on the strip |
| 7 | Average roll pressure (p) | $p = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2h_f}\right)$ | MPa | Hill's simplified equation |
| 8 | Roll separating force (F) | $F = p \cdot A = p \cdot w \cdot L$ | N | Force pushing rolls apart |
| 9 | Rolling torque per roll (T) | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·mm | Torque on one roll |
| 10 | Rolling power (P) | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | W (J/s) | Power required per roll |
| 11 | True strain (ε) | $\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$ | dimensionless | Logarithmic strain |
| 12 | Engineering strain (e) | $e = \frac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0} = \frac{\Delta h}{t_0}$ | dimensionless | % reduction |
| 13 | Volume constancy (flat) | $t_0 \cdot w_0 \cdot L_0 = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot L_f$ | mm³ | Conservation of volume |
| 14 | Ring growth (ring) | $D_f = D_0 \cdot \left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$ | mm | Final ring diameter |
| 15 | Maximum reduction (limit) | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 \cdot R$ | mm | Limit of bite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bite condition for rolling to occur:** $\alpha \le \arctan(\mu)$. The bite angle must be less than the friction angle, or the rolls will "slip" and refuse to draw the workpiece in. This is why $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Contact Length (L) in Flat Rolling

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup
Consider a single roll of radius $R$ acting on a workpiece of initial thickness $t_0$ and final thickness $t_f$. The reduction per pass is $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$.

Let the bite angle be $\alpha$ (the angle from the roll center to the entry and exit points of the contact arc).

### 3.1.2 Derivation Steps

Consider the geometry. The center of the roll is at height $R$ above the roll gap floor. The workpiece enters tangentially at point A and exits tangentially at point B.

**Step 1 — Drop in vertical height at the bite.**
The vertical drop from A to B equals half the draft on each side (for symmetric roll), but more correctly, the geometric relationship is:

$$
\cos \alpha = \frac{R - \frac{\Delta h}{2}}{R} = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}
$$

This comes from the right triangle formed by the roll center, the entry point A, and the vertical line to the roll gap.

**Step 2 — Solve for cos α.**

$$
\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply small-angle approximation for the bite angle.**
For typical rolling, $\alpha$ is small (3°–10°), so we can use the Taylor series: $\cos \alpha \approx 1 - \frac{\alpha^2}{2}$.

$$
1 - \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \approx 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for α.**

$$
\frac{\alpha^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta h}{2R} \implies \alpha^2 = \frac{\Delta h}{R} \implies \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the contact length.**
The contact length is the arc length of the roll-workpiece interface:

$$
L = R \cdot \alpha
$$

Substituting the value of $\alpha$ from Step 4:

$$
L = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}
$$

This is the famous **Hitchcock formula** for projected contact length in flat rolling, valid for small drafts.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Possible Reduction (Bite Limit)

### 3.2.1 Physical Condition
For rolling to occur, the rolls must be able to "grip" the workpiece. The bite angle $\alpha$ must be less than the **friction angle** $\beta$ where $\tan \beta = \mu$.

$$
\alpha \le \beta \implies \tan \alpha \le \mu
$$

For small angles, $\tan \alpha \approx \alpha$, so:

$$
\alpha \le \mu
$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Substitute the small-angle formula for α.**

$$
\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bite condition.**

$$
\sqrt{\frac{\Delta h_{max}}{R}} \le \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides.**

$$
\frac{\Delta h_{max}}{R} \le \mu^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta h_{max}$.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 \cdot R}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example
If $R = 250$ mm and $\mu = 0.3$ (hot rolling):

$$
\Delta h_{max} = (0.3)^2 \cdot 250 = 0.09 \cdot 250 = 22.5 \text{ mm}
$$

This means a single pass can reduce thickness by at most **22.5 mm** under these conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation: Neutral Angle (αₙ) in Rolling

### 3.3.1 Physical Concept
The neutral point (or no-slip point) is the location on the contact arc where the **roll surface velocity equals the workpiece velocity**. To the left of this point (entry side), the workpiece moves slower than the roll surface, so friction acts in the rolling direction. To the right (exit side), friction reverses.

### 3.3.2 Velocity Analysis

Let $V_R$ = roll surface velocity = $\omega R$ (angular velocity × roll radius). Let $V_0$ = entry velocity, $V_f$ = exit velocity. By **volume constancy** (per unit width):

$$
V_0 \cdot t_0 = V_f \cdot t_f
$$

### 3.3.3 Horizontal Velocity of Roll Surface
At any point on the contact arc at angle $\phi$ from the vertical, the roll surface has a horizontal velocity component:

$$
V_{R,x} = \omega R \cos \phi
$$

where $\phi$ is measured from the vertical centerline of the roll.

### 3.3.4 Velocity of the Workpiece
At the entry side, the workpiece moves at $V_0$. At the exit side, at $V_f$. Within the bite, the velocity can be related to the geometry.

### 3.3.5 Equilibrium Condition at the Neutral Point
At the neutral point, roll surface velocity = workpiece velocity. Through a detailed force/velocity balance (per Siebel's analysis):

$$
\alpha_n = \frac{1}{2} \arctan(\mu)
$$

### 3.3.6 Final Result

$$
\boxed{\alpha_n = \frac{1}{2} \arctan(\mu)}
$$

For $\mu = 0.3$, $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{2} \times 16.7° = 8.35°$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Roll Separating Force (F) — Hill's Simplified Formula

### 3.4.1 Concept
The roll separating force is the **total vertical force** that tends to push the two rolls apart. It is the product of the **average roll pressure** ($p$) and the **projected contact area** ($A = w \cdot L$).

$$
F = p \cdot A = p \cdot w \cdot L
$$

### 3.4.2 Average Roll Pressure (Hill's Equation)

Hill's analysis gives the average pressure considering the frictional hill effect:

$$
p_{avg} = Y_{f} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)
$$

where:
- $Y_f$ = yield stress of the material at the average strain-hardened state.
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction.
- $L$ = contact length.
- $h_f$ = final thickness.

### 3.4.3 Steps to Roll Separating Force

**Step 1 — Compute average flow stress.**

$$
Y_{avg} = \frac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2}
$$

where $Y_0$ and $Y_f$ are yield stresses corresponding to strains at entry and exit (using $\sigma_f = K\epsilon^n$).

**Step 2 — Compute contact length (Hitchcock).**

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hill's pressure formula.**

$$
p = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute projected area.**

$$
A = w \cdot L
$$

**Step 5 — Compute roll separating force.**

$$
\boxed{F = p \cdot w \cdot L = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) \cdot w \cdot L}
$$

### 3.4.4 Worked Numerical Example
A 600 mm wide steel strip ($Y_{avg}$ = 350 MPa) is cold rolled from 4.0 mm to 3.0 mm using rolls of radius 250 mm. Take $\mu = 0.08$.

**Step 1 — Compute draft.**

$$
\Delta h = 4.0 - 3.0 = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute contact length.**

$$
L = \sqrt{250 \cdot 1.0} = \sqrt{250} = 15.81 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average pressure.**

$$
p = 350 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.08 \cdot 15.81}{2 \cdot 3.0}\right) = 350 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1.265}{6.0}\right) = 350 \cdot 1.211 = 423.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute roll separating force.**

$$
F = 423.8 \cdot 600 \cdot 15.81 = 4.02 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 4.02 \text{ MN}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Torque and Power

### 3.5.1 Torque per Roll

The torque is computed by treating the roll force as acting at the **midpoint of the contact arc**, giving a moment arm of $L/2$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot L
$$

### 3.5.2 Power per Roll

The mechanical power required to drive each roll at angular speed $\omega$:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi N
$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per second (Hz).

$$
\boxed{P = 2\pi N T = \pi N F L}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Process Comparison Table

| Parameter | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling | Ring Rolling | Thread Rolling | Tube Rolling | Shape Rolling | Skew Rolling |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | > 0.6 T_m | Room temp | Hot/Cold | Cold | Hot/Cold | Hot | Hot |
| **Draft per pass** | 20–40% | 0.5–3% (per stand) | Continuous | Full thread in 1 pass | 5–15% | Variable | Continuous |
| **Roll force (typical)** | 5–20 MN | 5–15 MN | 1–5 MN | 50–500 kN | 2–8 MN | 2–10 MN | 0.5–3 MN |
| **Friction μ** | 0.2–0.5 | 0.05–0.1 | 0.1–0.3 | 0.05–0.1 | 0.1–0.3 | 0.2–0.4 | 0.2–0.4 |
| **Lubrication** | None/minimal | Heavy | Light | Yes | Heavy | None/light | None |
| **Surface finish (μm Ra)** | 5–25 | 0.1–1.0 | 1–5 | 0.4–1.6 | 1–5 | 5–20 | 2–10 |
| **Strain hardening** | None (recrystallizes) | Significant | Significant | Significant | Variable | Variable | Variable |
| **Primary product** | Plates, bars, blooms | Sheets, foils | Rings, flanges | Bolts, screws | Seamless tubes | I-beams, rails | Bearing balls |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Hot Rolling. Why is hot rolling preferred for breakdown of large ingots?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Hot rolling is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation of the workpiece is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal (typically above 0.6 × T_melting in Kelvin). For steel, hot rolling is performed between **900 °C and 1,250 °C**.
- **Reasons preferred for large ingots (2 marks):**
  1. **Lower flow stress** — The yield strength drops sharply at high temperatures, so the roll force required is **5–10 times lower** than at room temperature. Large ingots (200–250 mm thick) can be reduced economically.
  2. **No work hardening** — Dynamic recrystallization continuously replaces the deformed grains with new strain-free grains, allowing **unlimited total reduction** without the material becoming brittle.
  3. **Better ductility** — The metal can sustain large strains per pass (20–40% reduction) without cracking.
  4. **Breakdown of cast structure** — Hot rolling closes shrinkage porosity, breaks up the coarse as-cast dendritic structure, and refines the grain size, improving toughness.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Compare Hot Rolling and Cold Rolling. Give any six points.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 6 points × 0.5 each):**

| # | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Done above recrystallization temperature | Done below recrystallization temperature |
| 2 | Temperature: 900–1,250 °C (steel) | Temperature: room temperature to ~200 °C |
| 3 | Higher reduction per pass (20–40%) | Lower reduction per pass (0.5–3%) |
| 4 | No work hardening (recrystallization) | Significant work hardening (strain hardening) |
| 5 | Surface finish is rough (mill scale) | Excellent surface finish (0.1–0.4 μm Ra) |
| 6 | Used for breakdown of ingots/slabs | Used for final gauge sheets, strips, foils |
| 7 | No lubricant (or water spray) | Lubricant required (rolling oil) |
| 8 | Lower dimensional accuracy | Very high dimensional accuracy (±0.025 mm) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Mannesmann Process** for manufacturing **seamless tubes** with a neat sketch. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 300 mm wide aluminum strip is to be cold rolled from **5.0 mm to 2.5 mm** in one pass. The roll radius is **200 mm** and the coefficient of friction is **0.08**. The average flow stress of aluminum at the rolling strain is **180 MPa**. Calculate:
  (i) The contact length.
  (ii) The roll separating force.
  (iii) The torque required per roll, and
  (iv) The power required if the roll speed is **30 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Mannesmann Process (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Mannesmann process, developed by **Reinhard and Max Mannesmann in 1885**, is a method for producing **seamless tubes** from a solid cylindrical billet. The process is the most widely used industrial method for seamless tube manufacturing worldwide.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. A heated cylindrical billet (typically 100–400 mm diameter) at **1,200–1,300 °C** is descaled and fed into a **two-roll piercing mill** (also called a **cross-roll mill**).
2. The two barrel-shaped rolls are set at the **same angle** (4°–12°) and rotate in the **same direction**. The billet passes between them, gripped by friction and pulled forward.
3. Because the rolls rotate in the **same direction** and the billet axis is at an angle, the contact region experiences a **secondary tensile stress** in the axial direction due to the geometry of the contact. This tensile stress at the center of the billet causes the material to **rupture internally** — a phenomenon called **"Mannesmann effect"**.
4. A **piercing plug** (mandrel) is positioned at the center of the mill, which pierces the softened center and creates a **hollow shell** that emerges from the rolls.
5. The hollow shell is then sent to a **pilger mill**, **plug mill**, or **Assel mill** for further reduction in wall thickness and diameter, and finally through a **sizing mill** for accuracy.

**Sketch (1 mark — describe in words since Mermaid cannot show 3D):**
The setup resembles two cones whose axes cross at a small angle. The billet enters horizontally between the rolls, and a piercing plug is positioned axially in the center. The rolls rotate in the same direction, the plug is fixed, and a hollow tube emerges at the exit side.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Produces seamless tubes of uniform quality, suitable for high-pressure and high-temperature service (boilers, oil pipelines).
- High production rate and material efficiency.
- Wide range of diameters (15 mm to 700 mm) and wall thicknesses.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- High capital investment.
- Limited to ductile metals (not suitable for brittle alloys at the same temperatures).
- Wastage of the central piercing (the plug creates a "pip" defect at the front and back ends which must be cropped off).
- Wall thickness uniformity is not perfect without further reduction.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Width $w = 300$ mm
- Initial thickness $t_0 = 5.0$ mm
- Final thickness $t_f = 2.5$ mm
- Roll radius $R = 200$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.08$
- Average flow stress $Y_{avg} = 180$ MPa
- Roll speed $N = 30$ rpm = 0.5 rev/s

**Step 1 — Calculate the draft (Δh).**

$$
\Delta h = t_0 - t_f = 5.0 - 2.5 = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(½ mark)**

**Step 2 — Calculate the contact length (L) using Hitchcock's formula.**

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{200 \times 2.5} = \sqrt{500} \approx 22.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Calculate the average roll pressure (Hill's equation).**

$$
p = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 t_f}\right) = 180 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.08 \times 22.36}{2 \times 2.5}\right)
$$

$$
p = 180 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1.789}{5.0}\right) = 180 \cdot (1 + 0.358) = 180 \times 1.358 = 244.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Calculate the roll separating force (F).**

$$
F = p \cdot w \cdot L = 244.4 \times 300 \times 22.36 = 1.639 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 1.64 \text{ MN}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Calculate the torque per roll (T).**

$$
T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 1.639 \times 10^6 \times 22.36 = 1.832 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm} = 18,320 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Calculate the power per roll (P).**

$$
P = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times 0.5 \times 18,320 = 5.756 \times 10^4 \text{ W} \approx 57.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Final answers box.**

$$
\boxed{L \approx 22.36 \text{ mm}; \quad F \approx 1.64 \text{ MN}; \quad T \approx 18.32 \text{ kN·m}; \quad P \approx 57.6 \text{ kW per roll}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain the **Ring Rolling process** with a neat diagram. State its **advantages** over forging and casting for ring-shaped components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 mm wide strip is hot rolled from **10 mm to 5 mm** thickness in a single pass. The roll diameter is **600 mm** and the roll speed is **40 rpm**. The average coefficient of friction is **0.3** and the average flow stress is **200 MPa**.
  (i) Check if the rolling is feasible (bite condition).
  (ii) Calculate the roll separating force.
  (iii) Calculate the power required per roll. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Ring Rolling Process (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Ring rolling is a metal forming process used to produce seamless ring-shaped components with **uniform wall thickness** and large diameters. The process is classified as a **specialized rolling process** and is widely used for bearing races, railway wheel tires, flanges, and turbine rings.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. A **ring-shaped blank** (preform) — produced by forging, piercing, or casting — is placed between two rolls:
   - A **main roll** (driving roll) which is large and rotates the ring continuously.
   - An **idler roll** (pressure roll) which is smaller and moves **radially inward** at a controlled rate.
2. A **guide roll** controls the axial height of the ring and prevents it from tilting.
3. As the idler roll presses inward, the ring's **wall thickness decreases** continuously. By **volume constancy**, the displaced material flows **circumferentially**, causing the **diameter to grow**.
4. The process continues until the ring reaches the desired diameter and wall thickness, after which the rolls are retracted and the ring is removed.

**Diagram (1 mark — describe):**
The main roll is at the bottom, the idler roll is at the top, and a guide roll is on the side. The ring is shown expanding outward as the idler roll moves inward.

**Mathematical Relation (1 mark):**
By volume constancy (assuming constant height):

$$
D_f = D_0 \cdot \left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right)
$$

**Advantages over forging and casting (1 mark):**
- **Material saving:** Near-net-shape; less machining allowance than forging.
- **Better grain flow:** Continuous circumferential grain flow enhances fatigue strength — critical for bearing races.
- **Larger diameters possible:** Forgings are limited by press capacity; castings have porosity issues.
- **Dimensional accuracy:** Tighter tolerances than casting, less forging draft required.
- **Faster production:** Continuous process compared to die forging cycle times.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Width $w = 250$ mm
- Initial thickness $t_0 = 10$ mm
- Final thickness $t_f = 5$ mm
- Roll diameter $D = 600$ mm → $R = 300$ mm
- Roll speed $N = 40$ rpm = 0.667 rev/s
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$
- Average flow stress $Y_{avg} = 200$ MPa

**Step 1 — Calculate the draft.**

$$
\Delta h = t_0 - t_f = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(½ mark)**

**Step 2 — Check feasibility using the bite condition.**

The maximum allowable draft:

$$
\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 \cdot R = (0.3)^2 \times 300 = 0.09 \times 300 = 27 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $\Delta h = 5$ mm $< \Delta h_{max} = 27$ mm, **rolling is feasible**.

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Calculate the contact length.**

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 5} = \sqrt{1500} \approx 38.73 \text{ mm}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Calculate the average roll pressure.**

$$
p = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 t_f}\right) = 200 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.3 \times 38.73}{2 \times 5}\right)
$$

$$
p = 200 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{11.62}{10}\right) = 200 \cdot (1 + 1.162) = 200 \times 2.162 = 432.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Calculate the roll separating force.**

$$
F = p \cdot w \cdot L = 432.4 \times 250 \times 38.73 = 4.187 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 4.19 \text{ MN}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Calculate the torque and power.**

Torque per roll:

$$
T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 4.187 \times 10^6 \times 38.73 = 8.107 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm} = 81,070 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Power per roll:

$$
P = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times 0.667 \times 81,070 = 3.397 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 339.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Feasible}; \quad F \approx 4.19 \text{ MN}; \quad P \approx 340 \text{ kW per roll}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** When calculating power, students often forget to convert **rpm to rev/s** (Hz). One rpm = 1/60 rev/s. Marks are usually lost in this one step.
>
> 2. **Using initial thickness instead of final in Hill's formula:** The denominator in Hill's pressure formula is **$2 \cdot h_f$** (final thickness), not $2 \cdot h_0$. This is a recurring error.
>
> 3. **Wrong choice of bite angle formula:** Students sometimes write $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ as the **exact** formula, but it is an **approximation** valid for small drafts. Always state the exact formula $\alpha = \arccos(1 - \Delta h / 2R)$ for full credit and then mention the small-angle approximation.
>
> 4. **Confusing hot and cold rolling conditions:** Cold rolling uses **μ ≈ 0.05–0.10** with lubricant; hot rolling uses **μ ≈ 0.2–0.5** without lubricant. Mixing these values gives wildly incorrect roll forces.
>
> 5. **Skipping the feasibility check:** Always check $\Delta h \le \mu^2 R$ **first** before proceeding with the calculation. Examiners reward this step.
>
> 6. **Diagram mistakes:** In ring rolling, students often draw a single roll instead of two (main + idler). In Mannesmann, the rolls must rotate in the **same direction**, not opposite — losing 1 mark for this error is common.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention recrystallization temperature in hot rolling definitions:** This is the most-missed phrase in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Rolling** is a bulk deformation process using rotating cylindrical rolls; reduces cross-sectional area by **friction-driven plastic flow**.
- [x] **Hot rolling** is performed **above the recrystallization temperature** (≈ 0.6 T_m); no work hardening; large reductions; rough surface.
- [x] **Cold rolling** is performed **below the recrystallization temperature**; gives work hardening, high strength, tight tolerances, and excellent surface finish.
- [x] **Hitchcock's contact length formula:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ (approximate, for small drafts).
- [x] **Bite condition:** $\alpha \le \arctan(\mu)$, leading to **maximum draft** $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$.
- [x] **Neutral angle:** $\alpha_n = (1/2) \arctan(\mu)$ — the no-slip point on the contact arc.
- [x] **Hill's average roll pressure:** $p = Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)$ — accounts for frictional hill.
- [x] **Roll separating force:** $F = p \cdot w \cdot L$.
- [x] **Torque per roll:** $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$.
- [x] **Power per roll:** $P = 2 \pi N T$ (N in rev/s).
- [x] **Ring rolling** uses a **main roll** + **idler roll** + **guide roll**; diameter grows as wall thins, governed by $D_f = D_0 (t_0 / t_f)$.
- [x] **Thread rolling** is a **cold forming** process using flat or cylindrical dies; no chips, higher strength, used for fasteners.
- [x] **Tube rolling (Mannesmann)** uses two **barrel-shaped rolls rotating in the same direction** at an angle; a piercing plug creates the central hole; produces seamless tubes.
- [x] **Shape rolling** uses rolls with grooved profiles to produce I-beams, rails, channels.
- [x] **Skew rolling** uses rolls at an angle to the workpiece axis; primarily for **bearing ball production** and **Mannesmann piercing**.
- [x] **Defects in rolling** are classified as **surface defects** (scabs, scoring, cracks), **internal defects** (centerburst, porosity, inclusions), and **geometric defects** (wavy edges, camber, thickness variation).
- [x] **Centerburst** ("chevron cracking") is caused by **excessive reduction in a single pass** in cold rolling; it is a tensile failure at the center.
- [x] **Wavy edges** result from **non-uniform roll pressure distribution**; controlled by roll bending, intermediate rolls, and crown profile.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging: Classification of Forging, Various Forging Operations: Cogging, Coining, Heading, Piercing, Hubbing, Swaging, Forging Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forging: Classification of Forging, Various Forging Operations: Cogging, Coining, Heading, Piercing, Hubbing, Swaging, Forging Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Forging

**Forging** is a metal forming process in which localized compressive forces are applied to a workpiece (billet, bar, or ingot) using dies, hammers, or presses, causing the metal to plastically deform into the desired shape while retaining its solid state. The process fundamentally relies on the ductility of metals and is performed at either room temperature (cold forging) or elevated temperature (warm/hot forging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Forging is a hot/cold working process in which the metal is shaped by plastic deformation under compressive forces applied by dies, hammers, or presses, without any addition or removal of material."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you have a piece of **play-dough** or **clay**. If you press it between your palms, it flattens and changes shape — but no material is added or removed. The volume stays the same. This is essentially what forging does, except:

- The "play-dough" is actually a heated steel billet (often glowing red-orange at **950 °C to 1250 °C** for hot forging).
- The "palms" are replaced by massive mechanical hammers or hydraulic presses exerting forces ranging from **a few kN (small smithy work) to over 100 MN (large industrial presses)**.
- The metal is forced to flow into a die cavity, taking the exact shape of the die.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is forging special?** Unlike casting, forging aligns the internal grain structure of the metal along the contours of the part. This creates a "fibrous flow line" that gives forged parts superior **strength, toughness, and fatigue resistance** — which is why critical components like crankshafts, connecting rods, and landing gear are forged.

### 1.3 Classification of Forging (Hierarchical Overview)

Forging is broadly classified based on the **die geometry**, **temperature of working**, and **type of equipment** used.

| Basis of Classification | Types |
|-------------------------|-------|
| **Based on Temperature** | Cold Forging, Warm Forging, Hot Forging |
| **Based on Die Geometry** | Open-Die Forging, Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging |
| **Based on Equipment** | Hammer Forging (Drop, Board, Power), Press Forging (Hydraulic, Mechanical), Horizontal Forging Machines (Upsetters) |
| **Based on Forging Operation Type** | Cogging, Coining, Heading, Piercing, Hubbing, Swaging, Upsetting, Drawing Out, Bending, Punching |
| **Special Processes** | Roll Forging, Rotary Forging, Isothermal Forging, Orbital Forging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Students must be able to (a) Classify forging processes, (b) Explain at least six forging operations with neat sketches, and (c) Identify forging defects with their causes and remedies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Forging — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Based on Temperature

| Type | Temperature Range | Characteristics | Applications |
|------|------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Cold Forging** | Below recrystallization temp (≈ 0.3 T<sub>m</sub>) | High dimensional accuracy, good surface finish, high strength (work hardening), no scale | Bolts, screws, fasteners, gears |
| **Warm Forging** | 0.3 T<sub>m</sub> to 0.5 T<sub>m</sub> | Reduced flow stress, less tool wear, moderate accuracy | Automotive components, complex shapes |
| **Hot Forging** | Above recrystallization temp (≈ 0.6 T<sub>m</sub>) | Low flow stress, high ductility, scale formation, lower accuracy | Large structural parts, crankshafts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Recrystallization Temperature Rule of Thumb:** T<sub>recryst</sub> ≈ **0.4 × T<sub>melting</sub>** (in Kelvin). For low-carbon steel, T<sub>melting</sub> ≈ 1500 °C, so hot forging is done above ~600 °C, but practically **950–1250 °C** for steel.

### 2.1.2 Based on Die Geometry

#### (a) Open-Die Forging (Hand Forging / Smith Forging)
- Performed between **flat or simple-shaped dies** (anvil + hammer).
- The metal is free to flow laterally except where contacted by the dies.
- Used for very large parts (turbine rotors, ship propeller shafts) and for pre-shaping (cogging).
- Tolerance: **± 5 mm** to **± 25 mm**.

#### (b) Closed-Die Forging (Impression-Die Forging)
- Metal is forced into a die cavity that fully encloses the workpiece.
- The flash (excess metal) escapes through a thin gutter.
- Produces complex 3D shapes with high accuracy.
- Tolerance: **± 0.4 mm** to **± 1.6 mm**.

#### (c) Upset Forging
- Performed in **horizontal upsetters** (header machines).
- Billet length increases up to 2.5 times its diameter.
- Used for making heads on bolts, axles, and tubes.

### 2.1.3 Based on Equipment
- **Hammer Forging**: Uses impact energy (Drop hammers, Board hammers, Steam/Air hammers).
- **Press Forging**: Uses sustained pressure (Mechanical presses, Hydraulic presses).
- **Special Machines**: Horizontal forging machines (Upsetters), Ring-rolling machines, Rotary forging.

## 2.2 Detailed Forging Operations

### 2.2.1 COGGING (Drawing Out)

**Definition:** Cogging is a hot forging operation used to **reduce the cross-sectional area** of a workpiece and **increase its length** by repeated blows of a hammer along its length.

**Working Principle:**
- A heated ingot is placed on an anvil.
- The hammer strikes the top surface while the ingot is gradually indexed (rotated 90°–120°) between blows.
- The reduction in height per pass is limited to **25–40%** of the original height to avoid internal cracking.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Bite (a)**: Depth of indentation per pass
- **Feed (f)**: Linear displacement between successive blows
- **Number of Passes (n)**: Total blows required

**Applications:** Breaking down cast ingot structure, producing shafts, bars, and slabs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy for Cogging:** Think of rolling a thick ball of clay (like making a long snake) on a table by pressing it with your palm and rolling it forward — each press flattens it lengthwise, and you rotate it to avoid cracking.

### 2.2.2 COINING

**Definition:** Coining is a cold forging (or slightly warm) precision forging operation in which a **closed die** is used to produce parts with **sharp impressions, fine details, and high surface finish**.

**Characteristics:**
- Very high compressive stresses (up to **2000 MPa**) are applied.
- Material flow is restricted; hence stresses are 3–4 times higher than impression-die forging.
- No flash is produced (precision operation).
- Achieves dimensional accuracy of **± 0.025 mm**.

**Applications:** Minting of coins, medals, decorative parts, key blanks, jewelry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coining vs. Embossing:** Coining produces a sharp, mirror-finish impression by squeezing metal plastically into the die cavity. Embossing, in contrast, uses thinner sheet metal and only deforms the surface layer.

### 2.2.3 HEADING (Upsetting)

**Definition:** Heading is a forging operation in which the **end of a bar or wire** is thickened (upset) to form a head or boss, typically for fasteners, bolts, and rivets.

**Working Principle:**
- A bar stock is placed in a heading die.
- A die (or punch) strikes the end of the bar, causing it to expand radially.
- Performed in **horizontal upsetters** (header machines) at high speeds (50–200 parts/min).

**Key Rule:** The unsupported length of the bar during heading should not exceed **2.5 times its diameter (2.5 D)** — otherwise buckling occurs.

**Applications:** Bolt heads, rivet heads, screw heads, valve stems, gear blanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2.5 D rule?** Beyond this length-to-diameter ratio, the bar buckles like a column under compression. Beyond **3 D**, buckling is almost certain. Some modern upsetters handle up to **4 D** with proper die support.

### 2.2.4 PIERCING

**Definition:** Piercing is a forging operation in which a **hole is made in a hot workpiece** by driving a punch through it. The metal flows around the punch to fill the surrounding cavity.

**Characteristics:**
- Performed at **hot working temperatures** (above recrystallization).
- The hole is formed by **lateral flow of material**, not by cutting/removal.
- Used for making hollow parts, ring-shaped components, and tube blanks.
- Punch force ranges from **10–100 kN** depending on size.

**Applications:** Tube blanks, ring gears, valve bodies, hollow shafts, flanges.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Piercing vs. Drilling:** Piercing uses plastic deformation (no chips are produced). Drilling is a material-removal process (chips are produced). A pierced hole has a smooth, seamless, fiber-flow-continuous structure, whereas a drilled hole has a discontinuous structure that may act as a stress concentrator.

### 2.2.5 HUBBING

**Definition:** Hubbing is a cold forging (or warm forging) operation used to **manufacture forging dies** themselves. A hardened master punch (hub) is pressed into a softer die block to create a die cavity.

**Working Principle:**
- A hardened steel master (the "hub") is forced into a die block of softer material (e.g., low-carbon steel, Meehanite).
- Plastic flow creates a negative impression in the die block.
- The hub is then withdrawn, leaving a precise cavity.

**Advantages:**
- Eliminates the need for skilled die-machining.
- Produces highly accurate die cavities.
- Reduces die manufacturing time by 40–60%.

**Applications:** Manufacturing impression dies for forging, die-casting dies, plastic mold cavities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hubbing vs. Die Casting:** In die casting, molten metal is poured. In hubbing, the die itself is being shaped by plastic deformation. Hubbing is essentially a "die-making" operation.

### 2.2.6 SWAGING

**Definition:** Swaging is a cold/hot forging operation in which the **cross-section of a bar, tube, or wire is reduced** by repeated radial blows using reciprocating dies.

**Working Principle:**
- The workpiece is fed axially into a set of reciprocating dies.
- The dies strike the workpiece radially in a **hammering pattern** (typically 4-blow or 8-blow).
- Each blow compresses the cross-section slightly; the part is then rotated and fed forward.
- Used for both **internal swaging** (reducing tube ends) and **external swaging** (tapering rods).

**Types of Swaging:**
- **Die Swaging**: Uses reciprocating dies (most common).
- **Hammer Swaging**: Uses a rotary hammer.
- **Ring Swaging**: Uses tapered rollers.

**Applications:** Tapered shafts, tube ends, gun barrels, needle manufacturing, fishing rods.

## 2.3 Forging Defects — Comprehensive Analysis

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Topic:** Forging defects are heavily tested in both Part A and Part B. Students must know: defect name, its cause, and at least one remedy.

| Defect | Description | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------------|-------|--------|
| **Cracks (Internal/External)** | Visible fracture on surface or inside | Excessive deformation, low temperature, high strain rate, incorrect die design | Preheat dies, increase forging temp, reduce reduction per pass |
| **Folds (Laps)** | Wrinkles or overlapping metal surfaces | Sharp die corners, improper material flow, insufficient flash | Add generous radii in dies, control flash thickness |
| **Cold Shut** | A discontinuity or void on the surface where two metal streams fail to fuse | Low temperature, sharp die corners, improper die design | Increase die radii, raise forging temp |
| **Scale Pits** | Surface depressions caused by oxide scale being hammered into the surface | Excessive scale formation, no descaling | Use scale-removal step (e.g., water spray, brushing) |
| **Incomplete Filling** | Die cavity not completely filled | Insufficient material, low temperature, high friction | Increase billet size, raise temp, use proper lubrication |
| **Flash (Excess Material)** | Thin sheet of metal extruded at parting line | Intentional design feature, but excessive flash is a defect | Optimize flash gutter, control forging force |
| **Bursts** | Internal cracks in the center of the billet | Excessive tension in the central region, over-reduction in cogging | Limit reduction per pass to < 30%, rotate workpiece |
| **Mismatched Forging (Shift)** | Two halves of a forging are displaced relative to each other | Misalignment of die halves, worn guides, asymmetric die wear | Maintain die alignment, replace worn guides |
| **Die Shift / Die Wear** | Dimensions deviate from design | Die wear, improper die material, insufficient lubrication | Use harder die steel, re-grind dies regularly |
| **Surface Decarburization** | Carbon depletion on surface during hot forging | Holding at high temperature in oxidizing atmosphere | Control heating time, use protective atmosphere |
| **Underfill** | Insufficient metal to fill cavity | Insufficient material, miscalculated volume | Use correct billet weight |
| **Overfill / Flash Wasting** | Excess material flows into flash gutter | Too much material, poor flash design | Calculate correct volume, optimize gutter |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No coding is used in this formula sheet — all are engineering equations relevant to forging mechanics.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Homologous Temperature | $T_H = \dfrac{T_{work}}{T_{melt}}$ (in Kelvin) | Dimensionless | Decides cold/warm/hot forging |
| 2 | Recrystallization Temperature (Rule) | $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \times T_{melt}$ (in Kelvin) | °C or K | Hot forging lower limit |
| 3 | Forging Ratio (Cogging) | $R_f = \dfrac{A_{initial}}{A_{final}} = \dfrac{L_{final}}{L_{initial}}$ | Dimensionless | Quality of ingot breakdown |
| 4 | Volume Constancy | $V_{initial} = V_{final}$ | mm³ or m³ | Calculates final dimensions |
| 5 | Heading Length-to-Diameter Limit | $\dfrac{L}{D} \leq 2.5$ | Dimensionless | Prevents buckling |
| 6 | Forging Force (Slab Method, simple) | $F = \sigma_f \times A \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu \times L}{h}\right)$ | N (Newton) | Closed-die forging force |
| 7 | True Stress in Compression | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \left(\dfrac{A_0}{A}\right)^n$ | MPa | Cold/hot forging pressure |
| 8 | Strain (Engineering) in Forging | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Plastic strain |
| 9 | Upsetting Reduction per Pass | $\Delta h \leq (0.30 \text{ to } 0.40) \times h_0$ | mm | Prevents internal bursts |
| 10 | Power Required for Hammer | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \times n$ | J/s = W | Hammer selection |

**Symbol Glossary:**
- $T_{work}$ = Working temperature
- $T_{melt}$ = Melting temperature (in K)
- $A_0, A$ = Initial and current cross-section area
- $\sigma_f$ = Flow stress
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction
- $L, h$ = Die contact length and height
- $\sigma_0$ = Yield stress
- $n$ = Strain-hardening exponent
- $h_0, h_f$ = Initial and final height
- $m$ = Mass of hammer; $v$ = velocity; $n$ = blows/sec

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by forging engineers to (1) size hammers and presses, (2) select die materials, (3) optimize process parameters, and (4) perform FEA (Finite Element Analysis) simulations of forging flows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forging Force Using Slab Method (Compression between Parallel Plates)

This is a classic KTU 2024 exam derivation. We derive the forging force for **upsetting a cylindrical billet between flat parallel dies** with friction at the die-workpiece interface.

### Step 1: Setup and Assumptions
Consider a cylindrical billet of:
- Initial height = $h_0$
- Initial radius = $R_0$
- Coefficient of friction between die and billet = $\mu$

**Assumptions:**
- Material is rigid-plastic.
- Deformation is uniform.
- Friction follows Coulomb's law.
- Plane strain analysis is used (or axisymmetric for cylinders).

### Step 2: Force Balance on a Slab Element
Consider a thin horizontal slab of height $dh$ at a distance $h$ from the bottom. Apply axial equilibrium:

The differential force balance on a horizontal slab element (von Mises / Tresca approach) is:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{2\tau}{h}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = axial compressive stress at radius $x$ from center
- $\tau$ = shear stress at die interface = $\mu \cdot \sigma$
- $h$ = current height of the slab

### Step 3: Integrate the Differential Equation
Substituting $\tau = \mu \sigma$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{2\mu\sigma}{h}
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = \frac{2\mu}{h} \, dx
$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ (center, $\sigma = \sigma_0$ = average flow stress) to $x = R$ (die edge, $\sigma = \sigma_{edge}$):

$$
\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma_{edge}} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = \frac{2\mu}{h} \int_{0}^{R} dx
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\sigma_{edge}}{\sigma_0}\right) = \frac{2\mu R}{h}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Maximum Pressure
The maximum (edge) pressure is therefore:

$$
\sigma_{edge} = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right)
$$

The **average forging pressure** is:

$$
p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} \left[\exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right) - 1\right]
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Total Forging Force
For a cylindrical billet, the projected area $A = \pi R^2$:

$$
F = p_{avg} \times A = \frac{\pi R^2 \, \sigma_0}{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} \left[\exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Which simplifies to:

$$
F = \frac{\pi R^2 \, \sigma_0 \, h}{2\mu R} \left[\exp\left(\frac{2\mu R}{h}\right) - 1\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows that as the **radius-to-height ratio (R/h) increases**, the friction effect becomes dominant, and the forging force grows exponentially. This is why tall, thin billets are easier to forge than short, fat ones (the opposite of intuition!).

## 3.2 Derivation: Forging Ratio and Number of Passes in Cogging

### Given:
- Initial cross-sectional area = $A_0$
- Final cross-sectional area = $A_f$
- Maximum reduction per pass = $r_{max} = 0.30$ to $0.40$

### Step 1: Forging Ratio Definition
By **volume constancy** (no material lost):

$$
V_0 = V_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f
$$

Forging Ratio:

$$
R_f = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{L_f}{L_0}
$$

### Step 2: Number of Passes (Geometric Series)
If each pass reduces the area by a fixed ratio $k = A_{i+1}/A_i$:

$$
A_f = A_0 \cdot k^n
$$

Taking logarithm:

$$
n = \frac{\ln(A_0 / A_f)}{\ln(1/k)} = \frac{\ln(R_f)}{-\ln(k)}
$$

**Example:** If $A_0 = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ and $A_f = 25 \text{ cm}^2$, with $k = 0.8$:
- $R_f = 100/25 = 4$
- $n = \ln(4) / \ln(1/0.8) = 1.386 / 0.223 = 6.21 \approx 7$ passes

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Forging Operation

| If the Goal is... | Use This Operation | Key Equipment | Typical Product |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Reduce cross-section, increase length | **Cogging** | Open-die hammer/press | Shafts, bars, slabs |
| Produce sharp, high-detail impressions | **Coining** | Coining press | Coins, medals, key blanks |
| Form heads on bars | **Heading** | Header/upsetter | Bolts, rivets, screws |
| Make holes in a forging | **Piercing** | Piercing punch + die | Rings, hollow shafts |
| Manufacture forging dies | **Hubbing** | Hubbing press | Die cavities, mold inserts |
| Reduce cross-section with rotation | **Swaging** | Swaging machine | Tapered rods, tube ends |
| Compress end of bar | **Upsetting** | Upsetter | Bolt heads, valve stems |
| Bend a workpiece | **Bending** | Open dies | Eye-bolts, hooks |
| Cut off excess material | **Punching/Trimming** | Trimming press | Forged blanks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forging Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FORGING PROCESS]:::root --> B[BASED ON TEMPERATURE]
    A --> C[BASED ON DIE GEOMETRY]
    A --> D[BASED ON EQUIPMENT]

    B --> B1[COLD FORGING]
    B --> B2[WARM FORGING]
    B --> B3[HOT FORGING]

    C --> C1[OPEN-DIE FORGING]
    C --> C2[CLOSED-DIE FORGING]
    C --> C3[IMPRESSION-DIE FORGING]

    D --> D1[HAMMER FORGING]
    D --> D2[PRESS FORGING]
    D --> D3[UPSETTER FORGING]
    D --> D4[ROLL FORGING]

    C1 --> C1A[Cogging]
    C1 --> C1B[Drawing Out]
    C1 --> C1C[Bending]
    C2 --> C2A[Coining]
    C2 --> C2B[Heading]
    C2 --> C2C[Hubbing]
    C2 --> C2D[Swaging]
    C2 --> C2E[Piercing]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff;
```

## 4.2 Cogging Operation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heated Ingot on Anvil] --> B[First Hammer Blow - Center]
    B --> C[Rotate Ingot 90 deg]
    C --> D[Second Blow]
    D --> E[Continue Repetitive Blows]
    E --> F{Reduction per pass < 40 percent}
    F -->|Yes| G[Move to Next Section]
    F -->|No| H[Stop and Reheat]
    G --> I[Cogged Slab or Bar]
    H --> B

    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Coining Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Placed in Die] --> B[Top Die Descends]
    B --> C[Very High Pressure Applied]
    C --> D[2 to 4x Higher than Impression Forging]
    D --> E[Metal Flows into Cavity]
    E --> F[No Flash Produced]
    F --> G[Sharp Detailed Part Ejected]

    style A fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#87cefa,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Heading Operation — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bar Stock Fed into Header] --> B[Bar Projected from Die]
    B --> C[Punch Strikes Bar End]
    C --> D[End Upsets to Form Head]
    D --> E{L over D less than 2.5}
    E -->|Yes| F[Successful Head Formed]
    E -->|No| G[Buckling Failure]

    style A fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#98fb98,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#ff7f7f,stroke:#000
```

## 4.5 Piercing Operation — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Workpiece in Container] --> B[Heated to Forging Temp]
    B --> C[Punch Descends with Force]
    C --> D[Metal Flows Laterally]
    D --> E[Hole Formed without Material Removal]
    E --> F[Finished Hollow Part]

    style A fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#ff4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000
```

## 4.6 Hubbing Operation — Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hardened Master Hub] --> B[Placed above Die Block]
    B --> C[Hub Pressed into Soft Die Block]
    C --> D[Plastic Flow Creates Cavity]
    D --> E[Hub Withdrawn]
    E --> F[Finished Die Cavity Ready]

    style A fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#daa520,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.7 Swaging Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece Fed Axially] --> B[Reciprocating Dies Strike Radially]
    B --> C[Workpiece Rotates and Advances]
    C --> D[Cross-section Reduces Progressively]
    D --> E{Tapered or Reduced Section}
    E -->|Yes| F[Swaged Part]
    E -->|No| G[Re-evaluate Die Geometry]

    style A fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#cd5c5c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#7cfc00,stroke:#000
```

## 4.8 Forging Defects — Causation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forging Defect] --> B[Process Parameters]
    A --> C[Die Design Issues]
    A --> D[Material Issues]
    A --> E[Equipment Issues]

    B --> B1[Temperature]
    B --> B2[Reduction per Pass]
    B --> B3[Strain Rate]

    C --> C1[Sharp Corners]
    C --> C2[Flash Design]
    C --> C3[Die Alignment]

    D --> D1[Composition]
    D --> D2[Defects in Billet]
    D --> D3[Scale Formation]

    E --> E1[Press Capacity]
    E --> E2[Hammer Energy]
    E --> E3[Lubrication]

    B1 --> F1[Cracks, Cold Shut, Bursts]
    B2 --> F2[Bursts, Folds]
    C1 --> F3[Folds, Cold Shut]
    C2 --> F4[Excess Flash]
    D3 --> F5[Scale Pits]
    E3 --> F6[Surface Defects]

    style A fill:#b22222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000
    style F2 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000
    style F3 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000
```

## 4.9 Complete Forging Process Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
