<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Welding — The Fundamental Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding** is a **permanent joining process** in which two or more pieces of similar or dissimilar materials are coalesced (joined) by the application of **heat, pressure, or both**, with or without the addition of a filler material, to produce a **metallurgical bond** at the joint interface.

In KTU 2024 terminology, welding is broadly classified into **fusion welding** (where the base metal is melted) and **solid-state welding** (where joining occurs below the melting point). Every welding process must produce a **metallurgically continuous**, mechanically sound, and serviceable joint.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of welding like **re-freezing two ice cubes together with a drop of water**. When you place two ice cubes side by side and add a drop of water at the contact line, the water seeps into the gap, then freezes, locking both cubes into a single solid mass. Welding does the same thing: heat melts the edges (and a filler rod, if used), the molten pool flows into the gap, and upon cooling it **solidifies** to lock the two parts into one continuous piece of metal.

## 1.2 Weldability — Definition and Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weldability** is the *capacity of a material* to be welded under the **imposed fabrication conditions** into a specific, suitably designed structure that performs satisfactorily in the **intended service condition**.

It is **not a single measurable property**; rather, it is a **composite index** depending on:
- **Metallurgical weldability** — the response of the metal to the thermal cycle.
- **Fabrication weldability** — ease of producing a defect-free joint.

### 1.2.1 Carbon Equivalent (CE) — The Weldability Index

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is the single most quoted number in weldability assessment. The **IIW (International Institute of Welding) formula** is:

$$
\text{CE} \;=\; C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}
$$

where all elemental values are in **weight %**.

> [!NOTE]
> - **CE < 0.40** → Excellent weldability, no preheat required for mild steel.
> - **CE = 0.40 – 0.60** → Preheating and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) recommended.
> - **CE > 0.60** → Difficult to weld; high risk of **cold cracking** in the HAZ.

## 1.3 Solidification of Weld Metal — The Casting Analogy

The weld pool behaves like a **miniature casting** integrated with the base metal. Solidification begins on the **already-solid base metal grains** at the fusion boundary, and proceeds toward the weld centreline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epitaxial Solidification (Epitaxial Growth):** At the fusion boundary, the new grains of the weld metal *inherit the same crystallographic orientation* as the parent grains of the base metal. There is **no nucleation barrier**; the atoms simply stack onto the existing substrate lattice. This is the single most important reason why weld metal grain structure is a **direct continuation** of the HAZ/base-metal grains.

## 1.4 Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) — The "Un-melted but Damaged" Region

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the region of the base metal *adjacent to the weld* that has **not melted** but whose **microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered** by the heat of welding. It is the most common site for **weld failures**.

The HAZ experiences temperatures ranging from the **melting point** (at the fusion line) down to **ambient** (far away). Different sub-temperature ranges produce different microstructural sub-zones (detailed in Section 2).

## 1.5 Quick Reference — Welding Process Family Tree

| Family | Processes Covered in Module 4 |
|---|---|
| **Oxy-fuel Gas Welding** | Gas Welding (Oxy-acetylene) |
| **Arc Welding (Fusion)** | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG), **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)**, **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**, **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** |
| **Resistance Welding** | **Spot Welding**, **Seam Welding** |
| **Solid-State Welding** | **Friction Welding**, **Ultrasonic Welding** |
| **Thermochemical** | **Thermit Welding** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Heat input is universally reported in **kilojoules per millimetre (kJ/mm)**. The constant **60** converts seconds-to-minutes and **1000** converts watts-to-kilowatts in the heat-input formula.

## 1.6 Welding Defects — A Brief Preview

> [!WARNING]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, students MUST memorise the **8 most-asked defects**: (1) **Porosity**, (2) **Slag Inclusion**, (3) **Undercut**, (4) **Cracks (Hot & Cold)**, (5) **Lack of Penetration**, (6) **Lack of Fusion**, (7) **Spatter**, (8) **Distortion / Warping**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Weldability — Detailed Sub-Topics

### 2.1.1 Factors Influencing Weldability

The weldability of a metal is governed by **four pillars**:

1. **Chemical Composition** — Carbon and alloy content (quantified by CE).
2. **Physical Properties** — Thermal conductivity, coefficient of thermal expansion, melting point.
3. **Mechanical Properties** — Ductility, toughness, hardenability.
4. **Metallurgical Condition** — Prior microstructure (e.g., cold-worked, annealed, quenched).

### 2.1.2 Weldability Testing Methods

| Test | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Controlled Thermal Severity (CTS) Test** | Susceptibility to HAZ cracking |
| **Lehigh Restraint Test** | Tendency for root or underbead cracking |
| **Tekken (Y-groove) Test** | HAZ hydrogen cracking under restraint |
| **Varestraint (Variable Restraint) Test** | Hot-cracking susceptibility |
| **Gleeble Hot-Cracking Test** | Quantitative hot-tearing temperature |

## 2.2 Solidification of Weld Metal — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Steps in Weld Pool Solidification

1. **Heat extraction** occurs primarily through the **base metal** (the only heat sink).
2. **Epitaxial growth** initiates on parent grains at the fusion boundary.
3. **Columnar grains** grow *antithermal* to the heat-flow direction (i.e., **opposite to the maximum heat-flow gradient**, which points from the weld pool centre into the cool base metal).
4. **Grain morphology** depends on the **temperature gradient (G) to growth rate (R) ratio**:
   - **High G/R** → Planar / cellular growth.
   - **Low G/R** → Dendritic growth (columnar dendrites are typical in welds).
5. **Centreline segregation** — solute-rich liquid is rejected to the weld centreline, often the **last-to-freeze region**; this is the prime site for **centreline cracks**.

### 2.2.2 Solidification Modes (Constitutional Supercooling Criterion)

The morphology transition is governed by **Constitutional Supercooling Theory** (Rutter & Chalmers / Tiller):

$$
\frac{G}{R} \;<\; \frac{m \, C_0 \, (1-k)}{k \, D_L}
$$

where:
- $G$ = thermal gradient in liquid (K/m)
- $R$ = growth rate (m/s)
- $m$ = slope of liquidus line (K/wt%)
- $C_0$ = nominal alloy composition
- $k$ = partition coefficient (solid/liquid)
- $D_L$ = solute diffusivity in liquid

> If the inequality is satisfied, the interface becomes **morphologically unstable** → cells or dendrites form.

## 2.3 Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) — Sub-Zone Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HAZ is **heterogeneous**. For a low-carbon steel weld, the HAZ can be **sub-divided into 5 microstructural sub-zones**, each with distinct properties. This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question.**

| Sub-Zone | Peak Temperature Range | Microstructure (Steel) | Critical Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CGHAZ (Coarse Grain HAZ)** | ~1100 – 1450 °C (just below melting) | Coarse prior-austenite grains | Lowest toughness, brittle fracture risk |
| **FGHAZ (Fine Grain HAZ)** | ~Ac3 to 1100 °C | Fine equiaxed austenite → fine ferrite + pearlite | Good toughness, recrystallised |
| **ICHAZ (Intercritical HAZ)** | ~Ac1 to Ac3 | Partial transformation (ferrite + new austenite islands) | Mismatch with surrounding zones |
| **SCHAZ (Sub-Critical HAZ)** | ~400 °C to Ac1 | Untouched ferrite + pearlite; only tempering effects | Tempered martensite (TMCP steels) |
| **ICMZ (Intercritical Matrix Zone)** | Marginal, very narrow | Fine, mixed | Generally ignored in plain C-Mn steels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ac1** = 727 °C (eutectoid temperature for Fe-C) and **Ac3** ≈ 910 °C (upper critical for 0.2 % C steel) are the standard reference points.

## 2.4 Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene Welding, OAW)

### 2.4.1 Process Principle
A **fuel gas (acetylene, C₂H₂)** is mixed with **oxygen (O₂)** in a **welding torch** to produce a **flame** that melts the base metal (and filler rod).

### 2.4.2 The Three Flame Types

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Flame Temp (°C) | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neutral Flame** | 1 : 1 | ~3200 | **General-purpose welding of mild steel** |
| **Carburising (Reducing) Flame** | < 1 : 1 (excess acetylene) | ~2900 | Hard-facing, stellite welding |
| **Oxidising Flame** | > 1 : 1 (excess O₂) | ~3500 | Brass & bronze welding (Mn-brass with Zn fumes) |

### 2.4.3 Acetylene Generation
Commercial plants generate C₂H₂ by reacting **calcium carbide (CaC₂) with water**:

$$
CaC_2 + 2H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; C_2H_2 \uparrow \;+\; Ca(OH)_2
$$

Acetylene must be stored at pressures **< 1.5 bar** (≈ 101 kPa gauge) — above this, it **decomposes explosively**.

## 2.5 Arc Welding — Master Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **arc welding processes** share one principle: an **electric arc** struck between an **electrode** and the **workpiece** (or between two electrodes) provides the heat for fusion. The **arc temperature** ranges from **6000 °C to 20 000 °C** — far higher than oxy-fuel flames.

### 2.5.1 Heat Input Formula — The KTU Hot-Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important numerical formula in welding. **Memorise it.**

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \, V \, I \, 60}{S \, \times \, 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where:
- $H$ = heat input (kJ/mm)
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (process factor, dimensionless)
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $S$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $60/1000$ = conversion factors (s → min, W → kW)

**Process efficiencies (η) — KTU table:**

| Process | η (Thermal Efficiency) |
|---|---|
| Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG) | 0.65 – 0.85 |
| **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** | **0.30 – 0.60** (low, because most heat goes into electrode) |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** | **0.85 – 0.99** (highest — heat trapped by flux) |
| Oxy-fuel Gas Welding | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Resistance Spot Welding | 0.80 – 0.90 |

## 2.6 Detailed Sub-Processes (Module 4 Topics)

### 2.6.1 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)

- **Electrode:** **Non-consumable tungsten** (thoriated W-2 % ThO₂ or lanthanated W).
- **Shielding gas:** **Pure Argon** (sometimes helium for thicker sections).
- **Filler:** Added *separately* by the welder's other hand.
- **DCEN (Electrode Negative)** is standard — electrons flow from electrode to workpiece, giving **deep penetration** and clean weld.
- **DCEP (Electrode Positive)** is used only for **aluminium / magnesium** (oxide cleaning — "cathodic cleaning action").
- **Applications:** Stainless steel, aluminium, titanium, exotic alloys, root pass of thick pipes.

### 2.6.2 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)

- **Key distinction from GTAW:** The arc is **constricted** by a **copper nozzle orifice** and a **secondary plasma gas**, producing a **columnar, high-velocity, high-energy-density plasma jet** (~1–10 × energy density of GTAW).
- Two operating modes:
  - **Melt-in mode** (current < 100 A) — like GTAW.
  - **Keyhole mode** (current > 100 A) — arc pierces the workpiece, creating a **vapour cavity** (keyhole) that traverses the joint, giving **full penetration in a single pass** on thick plates (up to 12 mm stainless in one pass).
- Applications: Aerospace, tube welding, missile bodies, dairy/pharma stainless tubes.

### 2.6.3 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)

- The arc is **completely buried under a granular flux** — no visible arc, no spatter, no UV radiation.
- Flux functions: (i) **arc stabiliser**, (ii) **shielding gas generator**, (iii) **slag former**, (iv) **alloying element donor**.
- **Continuous wire electrode** is fed automatically.
- **Highest deposition rates** of any arc process (up to **20 kg/h** vs ~3 kg/h for SMAW).
- Used for **thick-plate longitudinal seams** — ship hulls, pressure vessels, spiral pipes.

### 2.6.4 Resistance Welding — Spot & Seam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Principle: Heat is generated by **Joule heating (I²R)** at the interface of the workpieces, while **pressure** is applied through copper alloy electrodes.

$$
Q \;=\; I^2 \, R \, t
$$

where $Q$ = heat (J), $I$ = current (A), $R$ = interface resistance (Ω), $t$ = time (s).

**Spot Welding:**
- Used for **lap joints** in sheet metal (car bodies, white goods).
- Sequence: **Squeeze → Weld → Hold → Off** (the Squeeze/Forge/Hold cycle is a standard 7-mark question).
- **Nugget** = the cast weld zone at the interface.
- Sheet thickness limit: typically up to **3 mm (mild steel)** per spot.

**Seam Welding:**
- **Continuous** version of spot welding — wheel electrodes roll along the joint, with **intermittent current** (or continuous current with overlapping spots).
- Produces a **continuous leak-tight joint** — fuel tanks, radiators, transformer cans.

### 2.6.5 Friction Welding (FW)

- **Solid-state** process — one workpiece is **rotated** (or linearly oscillated) against a stationary partner under **axial pressure**.
- Frictional heat raises the interface to a **plastic state** (below melting point).
- A **flash** (upset metal) is expelled, removing surface contaminants — the joint is forged at the same time.
- Types: **Direct Drive Rotary**, **Inertia (Flywheel)**, **Linear Friction**, **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**.
- Applications: Drill bits, valve stems, drive shafts, aerospace blisks.

### 2.6.6 Ultrasonic Welding (USW)

- High-frequency (**20 – 70 kHz**) **vibratory shear stress** combined with moderate clamping pressure.
- No arc, no filler, no melting in the conventional sense — bonding occurs by **interfacial shear and micro-plastic deformation**, breaking up oxide films and producing atomic contact.
- Used for **thin foils, dissimilar metals, non-ferrous wires, and polymer films** — battery tabs, aluminium foils, copper-aluminium terminal welding.

### 2.6.7 Thermit Welding (TW)

- **Thermochemical** process: an **exothermic aluminothermic reaction** provides the heat.

$$
Fe_2O_3 + 2\,Al \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,Fe + Al_2O_3 + \text{Heat} \;(\approx 3000\,°C)
$$

- The molten iron flows into a preheated mould around the joint and fuses the parts.
- **No external power** required — used in **field repairs** of heavy sections:
  - Rails (rail-joint welding)
  - Large shafts, ship's propeller stern, broken castings.
- Variants: **Pressure Thermit Welding (PTW)** — pressure is applied after pouring to improve grain refinement.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon equivalent for weldability of low-alloy steels |
| 2 | $H = \frac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat input in kJ/mm (KTU numerical must-know) |
| 3 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Heat generated in resistance welding |
| 4 | $P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\pi d^2/4}$ | Forge pressure in friction / resistance welding |
| 5 | $T_{cool} (800 \rightarrow 500\,°C) = \frac{E}{2\pi \lambda \rho c}\left[\frac{1}{500 - T_0} - \frac{1}{800 - T_0}\right]^{-1}$ | Cooling time t8/5 (cooling parameter for HAZ) |
| 6 | $Fe_2O_3 + 2Al \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3$ | Thermit reaction (exothermic) |
| 7 | $CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_2 + Ca(OH)_2$ | Acetylene generation (gas welding) |
| 8 | $\frac{G}{R} < \frac{m C_0 (1-k)}{k D_L}$ | Constitutional supercooling criterion (morphology) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Heat input $H$ directly controls HAZ **width, grain coarsening, and cooling rate t8/5** — which in turn controls the risk of **hard martensite** and **hydrogen cracking**. Reducing H (lower current, higher travel speed) refines HAZ grains but may cause **lack of fusion**; trade-off engineering is the essence of welding process selection.

## 2.8 Welding Defects — Comprehensive Classification

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Trapped gas (H₂, N₂, CO) in solidifying weld | Dry electrodes, clean base metal, correct shielding gas |
| **Slag Inclusion** | Entrapped flux between passes | Proper inter-pass cleaning, correct electrode angle |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current → groove melted but not filled | Reduce current, reduce travel speed, correct weave |
| **Lack of Penetration** | Insufficient heat, root gap too small | Increase current, slower travel, proper joint prep |
| **Lack of Fusion** | Poor sidewall melting | Higher voltage, proper torch angle, clean surfaces |
| **Hot Cracking (Solidification Crack)** | Centreline segregation, high restraint | Lower CE, use austenitic filler, buttering |
| **Cold Cracking (HIC)** | Diffusible H + martensite + residual stress | Preheat, PWHT, low-hydrogen electrodes |
| **Spatter** | Violent arc, large droplets | Correct voltage, anti-spatter spray |
| **Distortion / Warping** | Non-uniform thermal expansion + contraction | Balanced sequence, fixturing, backstep technique |
| **Overlap** | Insufficient travel speed, excessive filler | Reduce current, faster travel |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Heat Input Calculation (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A single-pass **SAW weld** is made on a 20 mm thick mild-steel plate with arc voltage 30 V, current 600 A, and travel speed 25 cm/min. Given thermal efficiency η = 0.95, compute the heat input in kJ/mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters**
- Voltage, $V = 30$ V
- Current, $I = 600$ A
- Travel speed, $S = 25$ cm/min $= 25 \times 10$ mm/min $= 250$ mm/min
- Thermal efficiency, $\eta = 0.95$

**Step 2 — Substitute in the heat-input formula**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \, V \, I \, 60}{S \, \times \, 1000}
$$

$$
H = \frac{0.95 \times 30 \times 600 \times 60}{250 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation**

$$
0.95 \times 30 = 28.5
$$

$$
28.5 \times 600 = 17\,100
$$

$$
17\,100 \times 60 = 1\,026\,000
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation**

$$
250 \times 1000 = 250\,000
$$

**Step 5 — Final division**

$$
H = \frac{1\,026\,000}{250\,000} = 4.104 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{H \approx 4.10 \text{ kJ/mm}}$

> [!NOTE]
> For a 20 mm thick plate, an H of ~4 kJ/mm is on the **higher side** — this means the **HAZ will be wide** and **coarse-grained**. A lower H (≈ 1.5–2 kJ/mm) is recommended for fine-grain structural steels to avoid CGHAZ embrittlement.

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Carbon Equivalent & Weldability Decision

> **Problem:** A low-alloy steel has the following composition (wt%): C = 0.18, Mn = 1.20, Cr = 0.50, Mo = 0.25, V = 0.05, Ni = 0.40, Cu = 0.30. Determine the carbon equivalent using the IIW formula and advise on the welding procedure.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply IIW formula**

$$
CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each term**

- $C = 0.18$
- $Mn/6 = 1.20/6 = 0.2000$
- $(Cr+Mo+V)/5 = (0.50 + 0.25 + 0.05)/5 = 0.80/5 = 0.1600$
- $(Ni+Cu)/15 = (0.40 + 0.30)/15 = 0.70/15 = 0.0467$

**Step 3 — Sum**

$$
CE = 0.18 + 0.20 + 0.16 + 0.0467
$$

$$
CE = 0.5867 \;\approx\; 0.59
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result**

$$
CE = 0.59 \;\in\; (0.40, 0.60)
$$

> **Result & Advice:** $\boxed{CE \approx 0.59 \rightarrow \text{Preheat to 150-200 °C; PWHT recommended}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU marking mistake:** Students often forget the **denominator 15 for (Ni + Cu)** and write it as a single sum divided by 5. The IIW formula has **three distinct denominators — 6, 5, and 15** — and these are *not* interchangeable. Memorise the exact form.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Resistance Spot Welding Heat Calculation

> **Problem:** In a spot-welding operation, a current of 8000 A flows for 0.16 s through a total interface resistance of 200 μΩ. Calculate the heat generated at the interface.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**
- $I = 8000$ A
- $R = 200 \,\mu\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6} \,\Omega = 2 \times 10^{-4} \,\Omega$
- $t = 0.16$ s

**Step 2 — Apply Joule's Law**

$$
Q = I^2 R t
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $I^2$**

$$
I^2 = 8000^2 = 64\,000\,000 = 6.4 \times 10^7 \;\; A^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $I^2 R$**

$$
I^2 R = 6.4 \times 10^7 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 12\,800 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $t$**

$$
Q = 12\,800 \times 0.16 = 2048 \;\; \text{J}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{Q \approx 2.05 \text{ kJ}}$

**Engineering check:** The energy needed to form a 6 mm-diameter nugget in 1.5 mm mild steel is typically 1.5 – 3 kJ, so 2.05 kJ is in the **acceptable range**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HAZ Microstructure vs Steel Composition

| Steel Type | Cooling Rate Effect on HAZ | Critical Defect Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain C-Mn Steel (CE < 0.4)** | Forms ferrite + pearlite; benign | Low |
| **CMn-Nb-V (HSLA, CE 0.4–0.5)** | Risk of **toughness loss** in CGHAZ due to grain coarsening | **Reheat cracking** in CGHAZ |
| **Quenched & Tempered (CE > 0.5)** | Untempered martensite forms on rapid cool | **Hydrogen (cold) cracking** |
| **Austenitic Stainless (304, 316)** | Sensitisation in 600–850 °C range | **Intergranular corrosion**, **knife-line attack** |
| **Ferritic Stainless (430)** | Excessive grain growth | **HICC — HAZ intergranular corrosion** |
| **Creep-Resistant (Cr-Mo-V)** | Hardness drop in ICR/sub-critical | **Type IV cracking** in service |

## 3.5 Derivation — Why the Heat-Input Constant Is 60/1000

The electrical power input to the arc is $P = V \times I$ in **watts (J/s)**. To convert this to **heat per unit length of weld** in **kJ/mm**:

1. Multiply power by time: $P \times t = V \cdot I \cdot t$ → energy in **joules**.
2. Travel speed $S$ has units of **mm/min** → travel time $t = \dfrac{\text{length}}{S} = \dfrac{1}{S}$ min for 1 mm of weld.
3. Convert **minutes to seconds**: multiply by **60**.
4. Convert **joules to kilojoules**: divide by **1000**.

Combining with efficiency $\eta$:

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \cdot \frac{60}{1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> This is the **canonical form**; KTU exam setters expect students to write it out with units explicitly.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define weldability and list any two factors affecting the weldability of steel. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Weldability is the capacity of a metal to be welded under imposed fabrication conditions into a structure that performs satisfactorily in intended service. It depends on the chemical composition (carbon and alloy content), physical properties (thermal conductivity, expansion), mechanical properties (ductility, hardness) and metallurgical condition (prior heat treatment).
**(Two factors required.)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keywords examiner looks for:** *capacity, fabrication conditions, intended service, chemical composition* — present all four to score full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] What is the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)? Why is it considered the weakest region in a weld joint? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The HAZ is the region of the base metal adjacent to the weld that has not melted but whose microstructure and properties have been altered by welding heat. It is the weakest region because:
1. It experiences a thermal cycle causing **coarse grain growth** (CGHAZ) and possible **untempered martensite** formation.
2. It suffers **loss of toughness** and is most prone to **hydrogen-induced cracking** and **stress corrosion cracking**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions — Full Internal Choice (Choose ANY ONE)

### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Q(A). (a) Explain in detail the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** in a low-carbon steel weldment, with a neat sketch showing the various sub-zones and their peak-temperature ranges. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) is the region of the base metal adjacent to the weld that does not melt but whose microstructure is altered by the welding thermal cycle. It is the most critical region for weld integrity.

**Sub-zones from weld centre to unaffected base metal:**

| Sub-Zone | Peak Temp | Microstructure | Property Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CGHAZ** (Coarse Grain) | 1100 – 1450 °C | Coarse prior-austenite → coarse ferrite + pearlite | Lowest toughness, brittle |
| **FGHAZ** (Fine Grain) | Ac3 (~910 °C) – 1100 °C | Fine recrystallised austenite → fine ferrite + pearlite | Good toughness |
| **ICHAZ** (Intercritical) | Ac1 (727 °C) – Ac3 | Partial transformation — ferrite + new fine austenite | Mismatch with surroundings |
| **SCHAZ** (Sub-Critical) | 400 – Ac1 | No phase change; only tempering of existing phases | Softening in TMCP steels |

> The **CGHAZ** is the most vulnerable — coarse austenite grains transform to coarse ferrite + Widmanstätten structures, drastically reducing **Charpy impact toughness**.

The student is expected to draw a sketch with weld pool in the centre, **fusion line**, and the four sub-zones arranged from fusion line outward to unaffected base metal, marking peak temperatures.

---

### Q(A). (b) Describe the **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** process with a neat schematic. List its **advantages, limitations, and applications**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** In SAW, the arc is **completely buried** under a blanket of granular flux. A **continuous wire electrode** is fed mechanically toward the workpiece; the arc, the weld pool, and the molten metal are all submerged beneath the flux.

**Equipment / Schematic Elements:**
1. Wire-feed mechanism with spool of continuous electrode.
2. Flux hopper with delivery tube above the arc.
3. Welding head with current-carrying contact tip.
4. Work-clamp and cable from power source (usually DC or AC constant-voltage).
5. Flux recovery / re-circulation system.

**Functions of the Flux:**
- Generates shielding gas (CO, H₂) on decomposition → protects weld pool from atmosphere.
- Forms a slag crust on the solidified bead → slows cooling and protects from oxidation.
- Adds alloying elements (Mn, Si) to the weld pool → deoxidation.
- Reflects radiated heat back into the weld pool → **thermal efficiency rises to 0.85–0.99**.

**Advantages:**
1. **Highest deposition rate** of all arc processes (up to 20 kg/h).
2. **No spatter, no flash, no UV radiation** — excellent working conditions.
3. **Deep penetration** with single-pass welds on thick plates (≥ 25 mm).
4. Smooth, uniform bead appearance; less operator skill needed.
5. Suitable for **longitudinal and circumferential seams** in pressure vessels, pipes.

**Limitations:**
1. Limited to **horizontal / flat positions** — flux cannot be retained on vertical or overhead welds.
2. High capital cost (flux handling, recovery).
3. Flux may retain moisture — must be dried to avoid porosity.
4. Limited to **ferrous metals and some nickel alloys** — cannot be used for Al, Mg, Ti.

**Applications:**
- Spiral-pipe manufacturing.
- Ship hull longitudinal seams.
- Pressure vessels, storage tanks.
- Heavy structural fabrication (bridges, towers).

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question

### Q(B). (a) Explain the **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)** process with a schematic. Discuss the role of tungsten electrode, shielding gas, and the choice of DCEN vs DCEP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** GTAW uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** to strike an arc with the workpiece. The arc, electrode, and weld pool are shielded by an **inert gas** (typically **argon**) flowing from a ceramic nozzle. Filler metal, when required, is added *separately* by the welder.

**Schematic Elements (top-down view):**
- TIG torch with **tungsten electrode** clamped in a collet.
- **Ceramic gas nozzle** surrounding the electrode.
- **Argon gas inlet** and flow regulator.
- **Power cable** to the collet.
- **Work clamp** to the workpiece.
- Separate **filler rod** dipped manually into the leading edge of the pool.

**Role of Tungsten Electrode:**
- Melting point **3422 °C** — far above arc temperature.
- Typically **W-2 % ThO₂ (thoriated)** for DC welding; **pure W or W-ZrO₂** for AC aluminium welding.
- **Non-consumable** — does not melt into the weld pool, ensuring **clean, oxide-free welds**.

**Role of Shielding Gas (Argon):**
- **Inert** — does not react with the molten metal.
- Heavier than air → forms a protective blanket over the pool.
- Ionised in the arc, providing a stable, constricted arc.
- For higher heat input on stainless / nickel → **argon + 2–5 % H₂**; for aluminium → **pure Ar or He**.

**DCEN vs DCEP — Critical Choice:**

| Polarity | Electron Flow | Effect | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEN** (Electrode –) | Workpiece → Electrode | **Deep, narrow penetration**; tungsten stays cool (long life) | **Standard for steel, stainless, Ti** |
| **DCEP** (Electrode +) | Electrode → Workpiece | **Cathodic cleaning** of oxide film (Al₂O₃, MgO); tungsten erodes fast | **Aluminium & magnesium** welding |

> For aluminium, AC is most common — it alternates between DCEN (penetration) and DCEP (cleaning) every half cycle.

**Advantages of GTAW:**
1. Highest quality welds; clean, slag-free, spatter-free.
2. Precise heat control → ideal for **thin sections (down to 0.1 mm)**.
3. Excellent for **stainless steel, aluminium, titanium, exotic alloys**.

**Limitations:**
1. Low thermal efficiency (η = 0.3 – 0.6) → low deposition rate.
2. Requires high operator skill (two-hand filler addition).
3. Sensitive to drafts — shielding easily disturbed.

---

### Q(B). (b) With a neat diagram, explain the **Resistance Spot Welding** process. Discuss the **Squeeze-Weld-Hold-Off** cycle and the factors affecting the weld nugget size. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Two copper-alloy electrodes clamp two overlapping sheets. A **high current (5 000 – 20 000 A) at low voltage (5 – 15 V)** is passed for a short time. The **I²R heat** generated at the faying surface melts the interface, forming a **cast nugget**. Pressure is maintained during cooling to forge the nugget and prevent cracking.

**Schematic Elements:**
- Upper and lower **water-cooled copper electrodes** (RWMA Class 2 — Cu-Cr or Cu-Zr).
- Sheets clamped between electrodes with **electrode force** F.
- **Weld timer** controlling the four-stage cycle.

**The Four-Stage Cycle:**

| Stage | Current | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Squeeze** | OFF | Establish electrode-to-sheet contact; break surface oxides; pre-load pressure |
| **Weld** | ON (full I²R) | Heat interface to melting → **nugget forms** |
| **Hold (Forge)** | OFF | Pressure maintained while nugget solidifies → prevents shrinkage voids & cracks |
| **Off** | OFF | Electrodes retract; sheet indexes to next spot location |

**Factors Affecting Nugget Size:**

1. **Welding current (I)** — most influential; nugget diameter ∝ I².
2. **Weld time (t)** — longer time → larger nugget (but risk of expulsion).
3. **Electrode force (F)** — higher force → lower contact resistance → smaller nugget; too low → splash.
4. **Electrode tip diameter & shape** — concentrates current density.
5. **Sheet surface condition** — clean, pickled surfaces give consistent R; rusty/oxidised → high R → spatter.
6. **Material properties** — resistivity, thermal conductivity, melting point (e.g., stainless needs **40 % more current** than mild steel of same thickness).
7. **Sheet thickness** — nugget diameter ≈ $4\sqrt{t}$ (where t = sheet thickness, mm), per standard design rules.

**Applications:** Automotive body assembly (car has 3000–5000 spot welds), white-goods cabinets, sheet-metal enclosures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked "explain the cycle", students often **omit the Squeeze stage** and jump directly to "Weld". The Squeeze stage is **non-negotiable** for full marks — it provides the *initial contact resistance* essential for I²R heating. Examiners at KTU penalise 1–2 marks for missing it.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 20 Must-Remember Bullets for Module 4 — Welding**

- ☐ **Weldability** is not a single property; it is assessed via **Carbon Equivalent (CE)**.
- ☐ **CE < 0.40** → no preheat; **CE 0.40–0.60** → preheat 150–200 °C; **CE > 0.60** → difficult to weld.
- ☐ **Epitaxial solidification** at the fusion line — no new nucleation, just grain continuation.
- ☐ Weld solidification is **antithermal** — grains grow opposite to the heat-flow direction.
- ☐ **Centreline** is the last-to-freeze zone — prime site for **hot cracking**.
- ☐ **HAZ sub-zones** (lowest to highest peak T): SCHAZ → ICHAZ → FGHAZ → CGHAZ → fusion line.
- ☐ **CGHAZ has the lowest toughness** — coarse grains + Widmanstätten ferrite.
- ☐ **Oxy-acetylene flame** types: Neutral (1:1), Carburising (excess C₂H₂), Oxidising (excess O₂).
- ☐ **Acetylene** is generated by **CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂** and stored at **< 1.5 bar**.
- ☐ **Heat input formula**: $H = \eta V I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ in **kJ/mm**.
- ☐ **GTAW (TIG)**: non-consumable W electrode + Argon shielding + DCEN for steel, DCEP/AC for Al.
- ☐ **PAW** has a **constricted arc** from copper nozzle → keyhole mode gives full penetration in one pass.
- ☐ **SAW** has the **highest thermal efficiency (0.85–0.99)** and deposition rate (up to 20 kg/h); flux covers arc completely.
- ☐ **Spot welding** is governed by **I²R**; uses **Squeeze-Weld-Hold-Off** cycle.
- ☐ **Seam welding** = continuous spot welding with rolling wheel electrodes.
- ☐ **Friction welding** is **solid-state** — flash expels contaminants; forge pressure applied after rotation stops.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic welding** uses **20–70 kHz** vibrations for thin foils, dissimilar metals, polymer films.
- ☐ **Thermit welding** uses exothermic reaction **Fe₂O₃ + 2Al → 2Fe + Al₂O₃** (~3000 °C); no external power; for rails and large field repairs.
- ☐ **Welding defects**: Porosity, Slag Inclusion, Undercut, Lack of Penetration, Lack of Fusion, Hot Crack, Cold Crack, Distortion, Spatter, Overlap.
- ☐ **Hot crack** = during solidification (centreline); **Cold crack** = after cooling (HAZ, hydrogen-induced, 3 conditions: H + martensite + stress).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesive Joints: Basic Principles and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adhesive Joints: Basic Principles and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adhesive Bonding** is a materials joining process in which two similar or dissimilar engineering substrates (called **adherends**) are permanently united by a non-metallic substance (the **adhesive**) placed between them. The adhesive solidifies through chemical reaction, solvent loss, or cooling, transferring load across the joint primarily through **shear** rather than through fusion or mechanical interlocking.

**Key Terminology Block:**

| Term | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Adherend** | The substrate (metal, plastic, composite, wood) being joined |
| **Adhesive** | The bonding agent (e.g., epoxy, phenolic, polyurethane) |
| **Cohesive Failure** | Failure *within* the adhesive layer |
| **Adhesive Failure** | Failure at the *interface* between adhesive and adherend |
| **Wetting** | The ability of the adhesive to spread and make intimate molecular contact with the adherend surface |
| **Cure** | The chemical/physical transformation of the adhesive from liquid to solid load-bearing state |
| **Lap Joint** | The simplest geometry — two adherends overlapping with adhesive between them |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of adhesive bonding like a double-sided tape holding two pieces of paper together.** The tape (adhesive) doesn't melt the paper; it just glues them by sticking at both surfaces. Now imagine thousands of microscopic "hooks" on the tape gripping onto tiny pores on the paper. That's essentially what adhesion does — except on a molecular scale, with two mechanisms:
>
> 1. **Mechanical interlocking** – the glue flows into microscopic pores and hardens (like anchors in concrete).
> 2. **Chemical/physical bonding** – molecules of the glue form bonds with molecules of the substrate (like magnets snapping together).
>
> The **strength** of the joint is limited by the *weakest link*: the glue itself, the glue-substrate interface, or the substrate.

---

## 1.3 Why Adhesive Joints Exist — The Industrial Motivation

| Limitation of Traditional Joining | How Adhesive Bonding Solves It |
|---|---|
| Welding creates **heat-affected zones (HAZ)** that weaken parent metal | Room-temperature bonding → no thermal distortion |
| Riveting/bolting adds **weight** and creates **stress concentrations** at holes | Continuous load distribution → no holes, no peak stresses |
| Brazing/soldering cannot join **dissimilar metals** (galvanic issues) | Adhesives are **electrically insulating** → no galvanic corrosion |
| Mechanical fasteners **loosen** under vibration | Properly cured adhesives are **viscoelastic** and absorb vibration (damping) |
| Cannot join **thin sheets, ceramics, glass, or composites** easily | Adhesives bond **brittle, thin, or non-metallic** substrates seamlessly |

---

## 1.4 The Five Engineering Requirements for a Strong Adhesive Bond

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "**5 Ss of Adhesion**" — every KTU question on this topic rests on these:
>
> 1. **Surface preparation** — clean, rough, chemically active surface
> 2. **Selection of correct adhesive** — match adhesive chemistry to adherend chemistry
> 3. **Sufficient wetting** — adhesive must flow and spread (low contact angle)
> 4. **Sound joint design** — design so that loads create **shear**, not **peel** or **cleavage**
> 5. **Solid cure schedule** — correct temperature, pressure, and time to achieve full polymerization

---

## 1.5 Basic Working Principle — How the Joint Carries Load

A typical **single lap adhesive joint** carries an externally applied tensile load $P$ along the adherends. This load is transferred to the adhesive layer through shear at the overlap interface. The fundamental stress relation is:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P}{b \cdot L}}$$

where:
- $\tau_{\text{avg}}$ = **average shear stress** in the adhesive (in **MPa** or **N/mm²**)
- $P$ = applied tensile load (in **N**)
- $b$ = width of the joint (in **mm**)
- $L$ = overlap length (in **mm**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** This single equation $\tau_{\text{avg}} = P/(bL)$ is the **starting point for almost every numerical problem** on adhesive joints. Memorize it.

---

## 1.6 Advantages and Disadvantages Summary

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Uniform stress distribution over the joint area | Requires careful **surface preparation** |
| Joins dissimilar materials (metal-plastic, glass-metal) | **Curing time** can be long (minutes to hours) |
| Lightweight (no holes, no extra fasteners) | Limited **temperature resistance** (most organic adhesives < 200 °C) |
| Seals, dampens vibration, insulates electrically | Difficult to **inspect** bond quality non-destructively |
| Smooth external surface (aerodynamic/aesthetic) | Joints can fail under **peel or cleavage** loads very easily |
| No heat input → no distortion | **Reversibility** — once cured, cannot be undone without heat or solvent |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Adhesives

Adhesives are classified in two ways KTU examiners prefer:

### (a) By Origin / Chemistry

| Type | Examples | Curing Mechanism | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural** | Animal glue, starch, casein, rubber latex | Solvent loss / cooling | Low strength, low cost |
| **Synthetic — Thermoplastic** | Polyvinyl acetate (PVA), hot-melt EVA, acrylic | Cooling / solvent loss | Re-soften on heating, recyclable |
| **Synthetic — Thermosetting** | Epoxy, phenolic, polyester, urea-formaldehyde, polyurethane | Irreversible chemical reaction (cross-linking) | High strength, high temperature resistance |

### (b) By Structural / Load-Bearing Function

| Type | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | Epoxy, phenolic, modified acrylic | Aircraft, automotive, load-bearing joints (strength > **7 MPa**) |
| **Non-structural** | PVA, animal glue, pressure-sensitive adhesives | Packaging, labels, temporary holding |
| **Sealants** | Silicone, polysulfide | Sealing gaps, not for primary load transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asked:** *Differentiate between structural and non-structural adhesives.* The cutoff is **shear strength of ~7 MPa** — anything below is non-structural; above is structural.

---

## 2.2 Types of Adhesive Joints (Geometries)

The **joint geometry** is what controls whether the adhesive sees **shear** (good) or **peel/cleavage** (bad).

| Joint Type | Schematic Description | Stress State | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lap (single / double)** | Two adherends overlap; load is tensile | **Shear** in adhesive | High |
| **Butt joint** | Adherends meet end-to-end | **Tension & shear** in adhesive | Low |
| **Scarf joint** | Tapered ends of adherends meet at an angle | **Shear** (combined with compression) | Very high if tapered correctly |
| **Strap joint** | Strap plates reinforce a butt joint | **Shear** (double) | Higher than butt |
| **Sleeve / Cylindrical joint** | Cylindrical rod inserted into sleeve | **Shear + radial compression** | High |
| **Corner / Angle joint** | Two adherends at 90° angle | **Shear + peel** | Low |
| **Tongue-and-groove** | Mechanical interlock + adhesive | **Shear** | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule (KTU golden rule):** *Design the adhesive joint so that the load tends to put the adhesive in SHEAR, not in peel or cleavage.* This is why **lap joints** dominate real engineering practice.

---

## 2.3 Stresses in Adhesive Joints

A bonded joint experiences **six possible stress components** in the adhesive layer:

$$\sigma = \{\sigma_x,\ \sigma_y,\ \sigma_z,\ \tau_{xy},\ \tau_{yz},\ \tau_{zx}\}$$

Of these, the **four that govern failure** are:

| Stress | Symbol | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile stress** (normal to bond plane) | $\sigma_n$ | Cohesive rupture of adhesive |
| **Shear stress** (parallel to bond plane) | $\tau$ | Shear yielding of adhesive |
| **Peel stress** (concentrated at edge, line loading) | $\sigma_p$ | Adhesive separation at edge |
| **Cleavage stress** (concentrated at edge, point loading) | $\sigma_c$ | Wedge-opening at edge |

---

## 2.4 The Volkersen Analysis — Shear Stress Distribution in a Single Lap Joint

In a real lap joint, the **shear stress is NOT uniform**. Due to differential straining between the two adherends (one stretches, one compresses relative to the joint centerline), shear stress peaks at the **two ends** of the overlap.

The classical Volkersen equation for the **average shear stress** in a lap joint is:

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P}{b \cdot L}$$

But for the **peak shear stress at the ends** of the overlap (KTU frequently tests this):

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{P}{b \cdot L} \cdot \left[ \frac{\lambda L}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)} \right]}$$

where the **lap joint parameter** $\lambda$ is defined as:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}}$$

and:
- $G_a$ = shear modulus of the adhesive
- $t_a$ = thickness of the adhesive layer
- $E_1, t_1$ = Young's modulus and thickness of upper adherend
- $E_2, t_2$ = Young's modulus and thickness of lower adherend
- $L$ = overlap length
- $b$ = joint width

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU insight:** The factor in brackets is the **stress concentration factor $K$** for the lap joint. It is *always > 1*, meaning the peak shear stress at the edges is always larger than the average. To reduce this concentration: increase overlap, increase adhesive thickness, or increase adherend stiffness.

---

## 2.5 Peel Stress and Cleavage Stress

These two stresses are **disproportionately destructive** — even a small peel or cleavage force can rupture a joint that would withstand 100× the load in pure shear.

**Peel stress** — measured by the **T-peel test (ASTM D1876)**:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{peel}} = \frac{w_{\text{peel}}}{b} \quad \text{(N/mm of width)}}$$

where $w_{\text{peel}}$ is the peel load per unit width.

**Typical peel strength values (KTU reference numbers):**

| Adhesive | Peel Strength (N/mm) |
|---|---|
| Natural rubber | 1–3 |
| Epoxy (rigid) | 2–5 |
| Polyurethane (flexible) | 5–15 |
| Pressure-sensitive acrylic | 0.5–2 |

---

## 2.6 Surface Preparation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequent 3-mark question:** *Explain the importance of surface preparation in adhesive bonding.*

| Method | Application | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Solvent degreasing** | Removal of oils, grease | Chemically clean surface |
| **Mechanical abrasion** (sandblasting, grit blasting, Scotch-Brite) | Metals, plastics | Increased surface area, removes oxide layer |
| **Chemical etching** (e.g., chromic-sulfuric acid for aluminum, sodium naphthalenide for PTFE) | Aerospace-grade aluminum, polyolefins | Chemically active surface, micro-roughness |
| **Primer / coupling agents** (silanes for glass, isocyanates for rubbers) | Glass, rubber-to-metal | Chemical bridging between adherend and adhesive |
| **Plasma / corona treatment** | Polymers, films | Activates surface, improves wetting |
| **Flame treatment** | Polyolefins | Oxidative surface activation |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Adhesive Joints

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}$ | Average shear stress in lap joint | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}$ | Direct tensile stress in butt joint | MPa |
| 3 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{G_a}{t_a}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$ | Lap joint stiffness parameter | mm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $K_{\text{lap}} = \dfrac{\lambda L}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}$ | Stress concentration factor in lap joint | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau_{\max} = K_{\text{lap}} \cdot \tau_{\text{avg}}$ | Peak shear stress at overlap edges | MPa |
| 6 | $f_{\text{peel}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{b}$ | Peel strength per unit width | N/mm |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{joint}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{joint}}}{\tau_{\text{adherend}}} \times 100\%$ | Joint efficiency (KTU definition) | % |
| 8 | $P_{\max} = \tau_{\max} \cdot b \cdot L$ | Maximum load-carrying capacity of joint | N |
| 9 | $F_{\text{shear, peel ratio}} \gg 1$ | Design rule: prefer shear loading | — |

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility of Adhesive Bonding

| Industry | Why Adhesive Bonding is Used |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Bonded aluminum honeycomb panels in Boeing 787 / Airbus A350; weight saving of 15–20% |
| **Automotive** | Bonded windshields (urethane), composite body panels (epoxy), brake-linings (phenolic) |
| **Electronics** | Die-attachment (silver-filled epoxy), underfills (flip-chip BGA), PCB laminates |
| **Medical** | Surgical tapes, dental composites, transdermal patches, orthopedic bone cements (PMMA) |
| **Packaging** | Hot-melt adhesives for cardboard, labels, lamination films |
| **Civil / Construction** | Structural glazing (silicone), tile adhesives (cementitious + polymer), FRP strengthening of bridges |
| **Footwear / Sports** | EVA hot-melt for shoe soles, ski laminates, tennis-racquet string bonding |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Average Shear Stress in a Single Lap Joint

**Given:** A single lap joint of overlap length $L$, width $b$, carrying a tensile axial load $P$.

**Step 1: Force equilibrium on the bonded area.**
The entire applied load $P$ must be transferred across the bonded area $A = b \times L$ through shear in the adhesive layer. By equilibrium:

$$P = \tau \cdot A = \tau \cdot b \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Solve for the average shear stress.**
Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P}{b \cdot L}}$$

This is the **fundamental lap-joint equation** that forms the basis for joint design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Joint Efficiency of an Adhesive Lap Joint

**Definition (KTU):** *Joint efficiency* $\eta$ is the ratio of strength of the jointed specimen to the strength of the unjointed (virgin) adherend, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 1: Strength of the unjointed adherend (cross-section).**

$$P_{\text{adherend}} = \sigma_{\text{adherend}} \cdot b \cdot t$$

where $\sigma_{\text{adherend}}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the adherend and $t$ is its thickness.

**Step 2: Strength of the bonded lap joint.**

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \tau_{\text{joint}} \cdot b \cdot L$$

where $\tau_{\text{joint}}$ is the **shear strength of the adhesive** (a material property of the cured adhesive).

**Step 3: Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{adherend}}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{joint}} \cdot b \cdot L}{\sigma_{\text{adherend}} \cdot b \cdot t}$$

Cancelling $b$:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\tau_{\text{joint}} \cdot L}{\sigma_{\text{adherend}} \cdot t} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 4: Numerical illustration (typical KTU problem).**
*Given:* Steel adherend, $\sigma_u = 400$ MPa, $t = 2$ mm. Epoxy adhesive, $\tau = 20$ MPa. Find overlap $L$ for $\eta = 80\%$.

**Step 5: Substitute.**

$$0.80 = \frac{20 \times L}{400 \times 2} = \frac{L}{40}$$

**Step 6: Solve.**

$$L = 0.80 \times 40 = 32 \text{ mm}$$

So an overlap of **32 mm** gives 80% joint efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Volkersen Peak Shear Stress in a Single Lap Joint

**Setup:** Consider a single lap joint with two adherends of different or identical moduli and thicknesses. The adhesive layer has shear modulus $G_a$ and thickness $t_a$. A tensile load $P$ is applied along the adherends.

**Step 1: Define the differential shear–strain relation.**
The shear strain $\gamma$ in the adhesive layer is the difference in axial displacement between the upper and lower adherend surfaces, divided by adhesive thickness:

$$\gamma(x) = \frac{u_1(x) - u_2(x)}{t_a}$$

**Step 2: Shear stress is shear strain times shear modulus.**

$$\tau(x) = G_a \cdot \gamma(x) = \frac{G_a}{t_a}\bigl[u_1(x) - u_2(x)\bigr]$$

**Step 3: Force balance on each adherend element.**
Let $\sigma_1(x)$ be the axial stress in the upper adherend. The load in it decreases as we move away from the free end (toward the joint center):

$$\frac{d\sigma_1}{dx} = -\frac{\tau(x)}{t_1}$$

Similarly for the lower adherend:

$$\frac{d\sigma_2}{dx} = +\frac{\tau(x)}{t_2}$$

**Step 4: Use Hooke's law for each adherend.**
$\sigma_1 = E_1 \cdot \dfrac{du_1}{dx}$ and $\sigma_2 = E_2 \cdot \dfrac{du_2}{dx}$.

Differentiating $\tau(x)$ twice and substituting yields the classical governing differential equation:

$$\frac{d^2\tau}{dx^2} - \lambda^2 \,\tau = 0$$

where

$$\lambda^2 = \frac{G_a}{t_a}\!\left(\frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Solve the ODE.**
General solution:

$$\tau(x) = A\cosh(\lambda x) + B\sinh(\lambda x)$$

**Step 6: Apply boundary conditions.**
By symmetry about the joint centerline ($x = 0$), the shear stress is symmetric, so the sinh term vanishes ($B = 0$). The boundary condition on overall load gives:

$$A = \frac{\lambda P}{2 b} \cdot \frac{1}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}$$

Therefore the **shear stress distribution** along the overlap is:

$$\boxed{\tau(x) = \frac{\lambda P}{2b}\cdot\frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)}}$$

**Step 7: Find the peak shear stress at the overlap edges** ($x = \pm L/2$):

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{\lambda P}{2b}\cdot\frac{\cosh(\lambda L/2)}{\sinh(\lambda L/2)} = \frac{P}{bL}\cdot\frac{\lambda L}{2}\cdot\coth(\lambda L/2)}$$

For most engineering lap joints $\lambda L \gg 1$, so $\coth(\lambda L/2) \to 1$ and the **simplified peak stress** is:

$$\tau_{\max} \approx \frac{P}{bL}\cdot\frac{\lambda L}{2}$$

**Step 8: Numerical illustration.**
*Given:* $P = 5000$ N, $b = 25$ mm, $L = 50$ mm, $\lambda = 0.05$ mm⁻¹.
Average shear:

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{5000}{25 \times 50} = 4.0 \text{ MPa}$$

Stress concentration factor:

$$K = \frac{0.05 \times 50}{2} \cdot \coth\!\left(\frac{0.05 \times 50}{2}\right) = \frac{2.5}{2}\cdot\coth(1.25) = 1.25 \times 1.3764 = 1.72$$

Peak shear stress:

$$\tau_{\max} = 1.72 \times 4.0 = 6.88 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU observation:** Notice that $\tau_{\max} > \tau_{\text{avg}}$ — the joint fails at the edges first, not uniformly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes and Their Diagnostic Signatures

| Failure Mode | Where it Occurs | Surface Appearance | Cause | Cure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cohesive failure (in adhesive)** | *Inside* the cured adhesive layer | Adhesive visible on **both** adherends | Adhesive strength < joint stress | Use stronger adhesive, increase bond area |
| **Adhesive failure (at interface)** | *Between* adhesive and adherend | Clean adherend surface on one side, adhesive only on the other | Poor wetting / contamination | Better surface prep, use primer |
| **Substrate failure (in adherend)** | *Inside* the adherend itself | Adherend fibres/material visible | Joint stronger than adherend | Good! Indicates maximum joint strength achieved |
| **Mixed-mode failure** | Combination of above | Both adhesive and cohesive zones visible | Intermediate conditions | Optimize both adhesive selection and surface prep |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU standard answer cue:** When asked *what is the ideal failure mode?* — answer: **cohesive failure within the adhesive** OR **substrate failure**, since these indicate the joint is *as strong as it can possibly be*.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Maximum Load Capacity of a Lap Joint

**Problem (KTU-type):** A single lap joint is made of aluminum adherends (yield strength = 120 MPa, thickness = 1.6 mm) bonded with epoxy adhesive (shear strength = 18 MPa). Joint width = 30 mm, overlap = 40 mm. Find: (a) the maximum load the joint can carry, (b) the joint efficiency.

**Part (a) — Maximum load:**

$$P_{\max} = \tau_{\text{adhesive}} \times b \times L = 18 \times 30 \times 40$$

$$P_{\max} = 21\,600 \text{ N} = 21.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b) — Joint efficiency:**

Strength of unjointed adherend across its full cross-section:

$$P_{\text{adherend}} = \sigma_y \times b \times t = 120 \times 30 \times 1.6 = 5760 \text{ N} = 5.76 \text{ kN}$$

Wait — this is *less* than the joint capacity, which means the **adherend would yield first** before the joint fails. Therefore the joint efficiency is **>100%**, meaning the adherend is the limiting component. In this case we say:

$$P_{\text{actual}} = \min\!\left(P_{\text{joint}},\ P_{\text{adherend}}\right) = 5.76 \text{ kN}$$

Joint efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{5.76}{5.76}\times 100\% = 100\%$$

The adherend controls; the adhesive joint is over-designed. *In practice we would reduce the overlap to save adhesive cost.*

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Bond-Line Stress in a Cylindrical Sleeve Joint

**Problem:** A steel shaft (diameter $d = 25$ mm) is bonded into a steel sleeve (inner diameter $D = 25.05$ mm) using epoxy (shear strength = 15 MPa, bond thickness $t_a = 0.025$ mm). The bond length is $L = 30$ mm. Find the maximum axial load the bond can transmit.

**Solution:** The bond area on the cylindrical interface is:

$$A = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \times 25 \times 30 = 2356.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

Maximum axial load:

$$P_{\max} = \tau \cdot A = 15 \times 2356.2 = 35\,343 \text{ N} \approx 35.3 \text{ kN}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an adhesive and an adherend. Differentiate between cohesive failure and adhesive failure.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model 3-Mark Answer]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Adhesive** is a non-metallic substance capable of joining two substrates (adherends) by surface attachment, which develops strength through chemical reaction, solvent loss, or cooling.
>
> **Adherend** is the substrate (metal, plastic, composite, wood) that is being joined by the adhesive.
>
> **Cohesive failure** is failure *within the bulk of the adhesive layer itself*; adhesive residue is visible on **both** broken adherend surfaces. It indicates that the adhesive's internal cohesive strength has been exceeded.
>
> **Adhesive failure** (also called interfacial failure) is failure *at the interface between the adhesive and the adherend*. One adherend surface appears clean while the other carries all the adhesive. It indicates inadequate wetting, contamination, or poor surface preparation.
>
> From a design viewpoint, cohesive failure is preferred — it shows the joint reached its maximum possible strength given the chosen adhesive.

---

### Question 2: List any four advantages of adhesive bonding over conventional joining methods.

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Model 3-Mark Answer]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Uniform stress distribution** — the load is spread over the entire bonded area, avoiding the localized stress concentrations caused by rivet/bolt holes.
2. **Joins dissimilar materials** — easily bonds metal-to-plastic, glass-to-metal, ceramic-to-composite without galvanic or thermal incompatibility.
3. **Lightweight** — no additional fasteners; weight savings of 15–20% compared to riveted/welded structures (critical in aerospace).
4. **Sealing, damping, and insulation** — bonded joints are airtight, vibration-damping, and electrically insulating in a single operation.

*(Other acceptable answers: smooth external finish, no heat input / no thermal distortion, ability to bond thin sheets.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: **(a)** Explain in detail the classification of adhesives with suitable examples. **(b)** A single lap joint of overlap length 40 mm and width 25 mm carries an axial tensile load of 30 kN. Calculate (i) the average shear stress in the adhesive and (ii) the joint efficiency if the adherend has ultimate tensile strength 250 MPa and thickness 2 mm.

`[KTU University Exam — July 2024 | 14 Marks]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

Adhesives can be classified on the basis of **origin**, **chemistry**, and **structural function**.

**(i) Classification by origin:**

- **Natural adhesives** — derived from animal, vegetable, or mineral sources. Examples: animal glue (from bones/hides), starch paste, casein (milk protein), rubber latex, sodium silicate. Cheap and biodegradable but low strength and water sensitive.

- **Synthetic adhesives** — manufactured from polymers. Examples: epoxy, phenolic, polyester, polyurethane, acrylic, cyanoacrylate, polyvinyl acetate (PVA), hot-melt EVA.

**(ii) Classification by chemistry (curing behaviour):**

- **Thermoplastic adhesives** — soften on heating and re-harden on cooling. Bond formation is *physical* (no chemical reaction). Examples: hot-melt EVA, PVA emulsion, acrylic pressure-sensitive adhesives. Advantage: reversible/recyclable. Limitation: limited heat resistance.

- **Thermosetting adhesives** — undergo irreversible chemical cross-linking during cure. Bond formation is *chemical*. Examples: epoxy, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde, melamine-formaldehyde, polyester. Advantage: high strength, high heat resistance, chemical resistance. Limitation: not re-processable.

- **Elastomeric adhesives** — based on rubbery polymers. Examples: natural rubber, neoprene, silicone, polysulfide. Advantage: high flexibility and peel strength. Limitation: low shear strength.

**(iii) Classification by structural function:**

- **Structural adhesives** — shear strength above **7 MPa**, capable of carrying significant loads over long service life. Examples: epoxy, phenolic, modified acrylic.
- **Non-structural adhesives** — shear strength below 7 MPa, used for packaging, labels, temporary holding. Examples: PVA, animal glue.
- **Sealants** — gap-fillers, primarily for sealing, not load transfer. Examples: silicone, polysulfide.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $L = 40$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $P = 30$ kN $= 30\,000$ N, $\sigma_u = 250$ MPa, $t = 2$ mm.

**(i) Average shear stress in the adhesive:**

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P}{b \cdot L} = \frac{30\,000}{25 \times 40} = \frac{30\,000}{1000} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Joint efficiency:**

Strength of unjointed adherend:

$$P_{\text{adherend}} = \sigma_u \times b \times t = 250 \times 25 \times 2 = 12\,500 \text{ N}$$

Strength of bonded joint:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \tau_{\text{avg}} \times b \times L = 30 \times 25 \times 40 = 30\,000 \text{ N}$$

Since $P_{\text{joint}} > P_{\text{adherend}}$, the **adherend is the weaker member** — it would fail first.

Joint efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{adherend}}}{P_{\text{adherend}}} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

> The joint is *over-designed*; the adherend controls. The adhesive is not fully utilized. Practically, the overlap could be reduced for economy.

---

### Question B: **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of adhesive joint geometries. State the design rule for selecting a joint geometry. **(b)** With the help of a stress–strain sketch, explain why peel and cleavage loads are far more destructive than shear loads in an adhesive joint.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | 14 Marks]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The common adhesive joint geometries are:

1. **Lap joint (single/double)** — two adherends overlap; adhesive is in shear.
2. **Butt joint** — adherends meet end-to-end; adhesive in tension and shear.
3. **Scarf joint** — ends of adherends tapered to an angle; adhesive in combined shear and compression.
4. **Strap joint** — butt joint reinforced with external strap plates; adhesive in shear (double).
5. **Cylindrical / sleeve joint** — one rod inside a sleeve; adhesive in shear on cylindrical surface.
6. **Tongue-and-groove joint** — mechanical interlock plus adhesive shear.
7. **Corner / angle joint** — adherends meet at 90°; adhesive sees shear and peel.

**Design Rule (KTU golden rule):** *Select a joint geometry that subjects the adhesive layer to **SHEAR** rather than **peel, cleavage, or tension*** — because adhesive shear strength is typically **10–100× greater** than peel or cleavage strength.

The preferred choices in order are: **scarf > double lap > single lap > strap > butt > corner**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

In a stress–volume sense, the adhesive layer behaves very differently under different loading modes:

- Under **shear**, the entire bonded area carries the load simultaneously. The stress distribution is approximately uniform (with mild edge peaking). Failure occurs only after the *full volume* of adhesive yields — typically at **15–30 MPa**.

- Under **peel**, the load is concentrated along a **single line** (the front of the peeling front). Only a small volume of adhesive — essentially a thin wedge near the bond line — resists the load. Failure initiates at very low stress levels, typically **0.5–5 N/mm of width**.

- Under **cleavage**, the load is concentrated at a **point** (the corner of the joint). The stress concentration factor is extreme; failure occurs at minute loads.

| Loading Mode | Stressed Volume | Typical Strength | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear | Full bond area | 15–30 MPa | **1× (reference)** |
| Peel | Thin line | 1–5 N/mm | 1/1000 in strain energy |
| Cleavage | Single point | < 1 N | 1/10⁶ in energy |

The **peel-to-shear strength ratio** is therefore typically in the range **1:50 to 1:100**. This is why joint designs that subject the adhesive to peel or cleavage are always avoided.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in adhesive-joint questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing adhesive failure with cohesive failure.** A "clean" adherend surface on one side means *adhesive failure*. Adhesive on both sides means *cohesive failure*. Examiners mark this carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — kN to N, cm² to mm². The numerical answer is then off by a factor of 100 or 1000.
> 3. **Using $b \cdot L$ for cylindrical joints** — for a cylindrical sleeve joint, area is $\pi d L$, not $b \cdot L$.
> 4. **Mis-defining joint efficiency** — efficiency compares joint strength to *adherend* strength, not to adhesive strength. The formula $\eta = \tau_{\text{joint}} L / (\sigma_{\text{adherend}} t)$ is the one to write.
> 5. **Confusing Volkersen average and peak stresses** — examiners often ask for $\tau_{\max}$ separately. Use $K_{\text{lap}}$.
> 6. **Forgetting the design rule** — most 7-mark joint-design questions require stating that *load should produce shear, not peel/cleavage*. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 4 — Adhesive Joints:**
>
> - **Adhesive** = the bonding agent. **Adherend** = the substrate. Never interchange these in definitions.
> - **Five Ss of Adhesion:** Surface prep, Selection, Sufficient wetting, Sound design, Solid cure.
> - **Classification by chemistry:** thermoplastic (reversible) vs thermosetting (irreversible cross-linking) vs elastomeric (rubbery).
> - **Classification by function:** structural (≥ 7 MPa shear) vs non-structural (< 7 MPa) vs sealants.
> - **Fundamental equation:** $\tau_{\text{avg}} = P / (b L)$. Memorize first; everything else builds on it.
> - **Joint efficiency:** $\eta = \tau_{\text{joint}} L / (\sigma_{\text{adherend}} t) \times 100\%$.
> - **Volkersen peak stress:** $\tau_{\max} = (\lambda L / 2) \cdot \coth(\lambda L / 2) \cdot \tau_{\text{avg}}$ — always greater than average.
> - **Lap joint parameter:** $\lambda = \sqrt{(G_a / t_a)(1/E_1 t_1 + 1/E_2 t_2)}$.
> - **Design rule (KTU golden):** *load the adhesive in SHEAR, not in peel or cleavage.*
> - **Six stress components** act on the bond line: $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$. Of these, **peel and cleavage are the killers**.
> - **Failure modes** ranked from best (joint is excellent) to worst (joint is poor): **substrate failure > cohesive failure > mixed-mode > adhesive failure**.
> - **Surface preparation methods:** degreasing, abrasion, chemical etch, primers/coupling agents, plasma/corona, flame.
> - **Cylindrical sleeve joint area** = $\pi d L$, not $b L$.
> - **Peel-to-shear strength ratio** is approximately **1 : 50 to 1 : 100** — quote this in any comparative question.
> - **Applications:** aerospace (honeycomb, fuselage), automotive (windshield, brake-lining), electronics (die-attach), medical (transdermal patches, bone cements), construction (structural glazing, FRP bridge retrofit), packaging (hot-melt carton sealing).
> - **Typical KTU keywords to use in answers:** "wetting", "surface energy", "contact angle", "stress concentration factor", "viscoelastic damping", "cross-linking", "thermosetting", "structural adhesive".

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET205 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing): Basic Principles, Processes, Materials Used, Applications, Future Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, popularly known as **3D Printing**, is defined by the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** standard as:
> *"A process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing and formative manufacturing methodologies."*

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes AM as a **process of fabricating physical objects directly from digital Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models through the successive addition of material**, typically in a layer-by-layer fashion, with each layer representing a thin cross-section of the final component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> The 2024 KTU scheme for PCIET205 expects students to (a) classify the seven standard AM process families, (b) identify process-material compatibility, (c) evaluate process selection criteria, and (d) recognize industry applications and emerging trends. This topic primarily maps to **CO3 (Identify suitable manufacturing processes)** and **CO4 (Understand modern manufacturing trends)**.

### 1.2 The "Additive vs. Subtractive" Distinction

| Manufacturing Paradigm | Material Flow | Example |
|------------------------|---------------|---------|
| **Subtractive** | Material REMOVED from a block (stock) | CNC milling, turning, drilling |
| **Formative** | Material DEFORMED/SHAPED using force | Forging, casting, sheet metal forming |
| **Additive** | material ADDED layer-by-layer | FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layer Cake" Model

Imagine you are baking a **multi-tiered cake shaped exactly like a small house**:
- You don't carve the house out of a giant cake block (that would be subtractive).
- You don't pour batter into a house-shaped mold (that would be formative/casting).
- Instead, you **slice the house into 50 thin horizontal cross-sections**, then **deposit one thin layer of batter at a time**, building from the bottom up.

This is precisely how additive manufacturing works. A 3D model is virtually **sliced** into hundreds or thousands of horizontal cross-sections (typically **0.02 mm to 0.5 mm** thick). The printer then physically reproduces each slice using a chosen material, fusing them together to form the final part.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **STL File**: Standard Tessellation Language — the de facto file format that approximates a 3D model as a mesh of triangles.
> - **Slicing Software**: Converts the STL into G-code-style toolpaths (often called "sliced layers").
> - **Build Volume**: The maximum 3D envelope (X × Y × Z) that a printer can produce in a single job.
> - **Layer Thickness (Z-resolution)**: Vertical height of each deposited layer, usually **0.025 mm to 0.3 mm**.

### 1.4 The Generic AM Process Chain

The fundamental workflow for any AM process, regardless of the specific technology, follows a standard 6-stage pipeline:

1. **CAD Modeling** → Create a 3D solid model in software like SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360.
2. **STL Export** → Convert the CAD file into a triangulated mesh (.STL format).
3. **Slicing** → Slice the STL into 2D horizontal layers using a slicer (e.g., Cura, Simplify3D).
4. **Process Parameter Setup** → Set layer thickness, infill, orientation, support structures.
5. **Build Execution** → The AM machine physically constructs the part layer-by-layer.
6. **Post-Processing** → Support removal, surface finishing, curing, infiltration, heat treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Distinction — Direct vs. Indirect AM:**
> - **Direct AM**: Part is built ready-to-use or near net-shape (e.g., FDM, SLM).
> - **Indirect AM**: A "green part" is 3D printed and then undergoes secondary processing like sintering (e.g., Binder Jetting followed by furnace sintering).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Standard AM Process Families (ISO/ASTM 52900)

The KTU 2024 syllabus **mandatorily** requires students to be able to **classify** and **describe** the seven standardized AM process categories. Each is driven by a distinct energy/material deposition mechanism.

#### (A) Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)
- **Energy Source**: Ultraviolet (UV) or visible light.
- **Mechanism**: A vat of liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured (hardened) by a light source.
- **Sub-processes**:
  - **Stereolithography (SLA)** — Scanning laser cures resin point-by-point.
  - **Digital Light Processing (DLP)** — A digital projector flashes an entire layer's image at once.
- **Materials**: Photopolymer resins (acrylic, epoxy-based).
- **Pros**: Very high surface finish (Ra ≤ 5 µm), high accuracy (±0.05 mm).
- **Cons**: Limited to photopolymers, requires support structures, post-curing needed.

#### (B) Material Extrusion (MEX)
- **Energy Source**: Thermal (heated nozzle).
- **Mechanism**: Thermoplastic filament or paste is extruded through a heated nozzle and deposited along a toolpath.
- **Sub-processes**:
  - **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** — Most common desktop technology.
  - **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)** — Generic term for FDM.
- **Materials**: ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, TPU, PC, PEEK.
- **Pros**: Low cost, simple, wide material range.
- **Cons**: Anisotropic mechanical properties, visible layer lines, slower than other methods.

#### (C) Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
- **Energy Source**: Laser or electron beam.
- **Mechanism**: A thin layer of powder is spread; an energy source selectively fuses cross-sectional areas.
- **Sub-processes**:
  - **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** — Polymer powders, does not fully melt.
  - **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** — Metal powders, fully melted.
  - **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** — Metal powders, sintered not melted.
  - **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** — Metal powders, vacuum environment.
- **Materials**: Nylon (SLS), Stainless Steel, Ti6Al4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718.
- **Pros**: High strength, complex geometry, no support structures (SLS).
- **Cons**: High machine cost, post-processing required.

#### (D) Material Jetting (MJT)
- **Mechanism**: Droplets of build material are selectively jetted (similar to 2D inkjet printing) and instantly cured.
- **Sub-processes**: PolyJet, MultiJet Modeling (MJM), NanoParticle Jetting.
- **Materials**: Photopolymers, wax, multi-material combinations.
- **Pros**: Multi-material and full-color parts, very high resolution (~14 µm).
- **Cons**: High cost, limited material range.

#### (E) Binder Jetting (BJT)
- **Mechanism**: A liquid binder is selectively deposited onto a powder bed to bond particles together.
- **Sub-processes**: Metal Binder Jetting, Sand Binder Jetting (for casting molds).
- **Materials**: Metal powders, sand, ceramics.
- **Pros**: Large build volumes, full-color sand printing.
- **Cons**: Requires post-processing (infiltration/sintering), lower accuracy.

#### (F) Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
- **Mechanism**: Focused thermal energy (laser, plasma, electron beam) is used to melt material as it is deposited.
- **Sub-processes**: Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM), Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM).
- **Materials**: Metal powders or wires (titanium, stainless steel, Inconel).
- **Pros**: Repair applications, multi-axis deposition, large parts.
- **Cons**: Limited resolution, expensive.

#### (G) Sheet Lamination (SHL)
- **Mechanism**: Sheets of material are bonded together layer-by-layer and then cut to shape.
- **Sub-processes**: Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM).
- **Materials**: Paper, plastic, metal foils, composites.
- **Pros**: Low cost, fast for large parts.
- **Cons**: Limited geometric complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Processes NOT Classified by ISO 52900 but Often Asked in KTU:**
> - **3D Bioprinting** — Extrusion of living cells (bio-ink) for tissue engineering.
> - **4D Printing** — 3D printed objects that change shape over time in response to stimuli (heat, moisture, light).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations and parameters are **directly applicable** to numerical problems asked in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{build} = \dfrac{H}{t_{layer}}$ | Build time = part height / layer thickness | hours | — |
| 2 | $T_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{R_{dep} \cdot t_{layer}}$ | Build time using deposition rate | hours | $R_{dep}$ in mm³/s |
| 3 | $E_{total} = P_{machine} \cdot T_{build}$ | Total energy consumed | kWh | $P_{machine}$ in kW |
| 4 | $V_{part} = L \cdot W \cdot H$ | Rectangular part bounding volume | mm³ | — |
| 5 | $\rho_{relative} = \dfrac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{bulk}} \times 100\%$ | Relative density (porosity indicator) | % | SLM: 99.5%+, EBM: 99.0%+ |
| 6 | $\eta_{material} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{m_{powder\,used}} \times 100\%$ | Material utilization efficiency | % | DMLS: ~95%, FDM: ~100% |
| 7 | $Ra_{surface} \approx k \cdot t_{layer}$ | Surface roughness proportional to layer thickness | µm | k ≈ 30–50 |
| 8 | $T_{cool} = T_{melt} - \Delta T_{undercool}$ | Solidification temperature | °C | Ti6Al4V: ~1660°C |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise powder temperature | J | $c_p$ = specific heat |
| 10 | $P_{laser} = \dfrac{E_{v}}{t \cdot A_{spot}}$ | Required laser power density | W/mm² | SLM Ti: ~10⁶–10⁷ W/mm² |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU ANSWERS:**
> Always **state the formula first**, then **substitute values with units**, and finally **box the final answer**. Examiners award **partial marks** for correct methodology even if arithmetic is wrong.

### 2.3 Why These Process Families Matter in Engineering

| Process Family | Real-World Engineering Application |
|----------------|------------------------------------|
| VPP (SLA/DLP) | Dental crowns, jewelry patterns, microfluidic devices |
| MEX (FDM) | Prototyping, jigs and fixtures, low-cost end-use parts |
| PBF (SLM/SLS) | Aerospace brackets, medical implants, automotive functional parts |
| MJT (PolyJet) | Multi-material prototypes, medical models with varying stiffness |
| BJT | Large sand casting molds, low-cost metal prototypes |
| DED | Turbine blade repair, large aerospace structural parts |
| SHL (LOM) | Architectural models, paper-based prototypes |

### 2.4 Process Selection — The Key Decision Matrix

When selecting an AM process, engineers balance these six factors (often tested as 7-mark questions in KTU 2024 ESE):

1. **Material Compatibility** — What material does the application demand?
2. **Mechanical Performance** — Required tensile strength, fatigue life, isotropy.
3. **Dimensional Accuracy** — Tolerance required (±0.05 mm vs ±0.5 mm).
4. **Surface Finish** — Ra value required (as-printed vs. post-processed).
5. **Build Volume** — Maximum part size.
6. **Cost per Part** — Material, machine time, labor, post-processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Theory Questions:**
> - For **high accuracy + smooth finish** → Choose **VPP (SLA/DLP)**.
> - For **low cost + prototyping** → Choose **MEX (FDM/FFF)**.
> - For **functional metal parts** → Choose **PBF (SLM/DMLS)** or **DED**.
> - For **multi-material prototypes** → Choose **MJT (PolyJet)**.
> - For **large sand molds/castings** → Choose **BJT**.
